topics.title: Propeller as a Thrust Producing Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propeller as a Thrust Producing Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ Propeller as a Thrust Producing Mechanism

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **marine propeller** is a rotating, multi-bladed, twisted lifting-surface device, mechanically coupled to the ship's main propulsion engine through a shafting system, which converts the **rotational mechanical power** delivered by the engine into a **useful axial hydrodynamic thrust force** that propels the vessel through water. From a fluid-mechanics perspective, each propeller blade section behaves as a **hydrofoil (aerofoil in water)** operating in a helical slipstream, generating lift perpendicular to the relative flow which is then resolved into thrust (axial) and torque-resisting (tangential) components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** *A propeller is a foil-like machine which converts the engine's shaft torque into a net hydrodynamic force whose component in the direction of ship motion is called the THrust (T).* The component perpendicular to thrust, which must be overcome by engine torque, is the **Tangential Resistance Force (F_T)**.

The thrust-producing mechanism of a propeller can be studied at two hierarchical levels:
- **Global / Macroscopic level** → Momentum Theory (also called Actuator Disc Theory, after Rankine-Froude).
- **Local / Sectional level** → Blade Element Theory (after Froude–Lanchester) and Vortex Lattice / Lifting-Line Theory.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are standing on a stationary skateboard and you repeatedly throw heavy medicine balls *backward* off the rear. Every ball you throw pushes *you* forward (Newton's Third Law). The faster and heavier the balls you throw, the faster you accelerate. A marine propeller does *exactly the same thing*, but with water: it is a **mechanical "water thrower"**. The rotating blades grab a large mass of water and *accelerate it rearward*; the equal-and-opposite reaction force pushes the ship *forward*. The faster the blades spin (more rpm) and the larger the disc swept (more water grabbed per second), the greater the thrust.

A second useful analogy is to consider the propeller as a **rotating wing (airfoil)** in a helical slipstream: the angle between the blade chord and the resultant inflow determines lift, which—resolved vectorially—yields thrust.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Board Exam High-Yield)

- **Density of sea water:** ρ = **1025 kg/m³** (fresh water: 1000 kg/m³).
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** g = **9.81 m/s²**.
- **Kinematic viscosity of seawater:** ν ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**.
- **Standard propeller rotational convention:** **Right-handed (clockwise when viewed from astern)**.
- **Typical ship-propeller blade count:** **3 to 5** (modern large vessels: 4 or 5; tugboats: 4; high-speed vessels: 3).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether you recall **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** for seawater in numericals. Mistaking it with 1000 kg/m³ costs easy marks.

---

## 1.4 Operational Components of a Propeller

A ship propeller (typically a *controllable-pitch or fixed-pitch* type) consists of:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hub** | Central boss that mounts the propeller on the tailshaft and houses the mechanism (if CPP). |
| **Blades** | Hydrofoils that produce the thrust by accelerating water. |
| **Root, Mid, Tip Sections** | Sections of varying pitch & camber, optimized along the radius. |
| **Leading Edge / Trailing Edge** | Upstream and downstream edges of each blade. |
| **Boss Cap / Fairing** | Reduces hub vortex and hub cavitation. |

| Geometric Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Diameter** | **D** | Twice the propeller tip radius (largest swept circle). |
| **Pitch (Geometric)** | **P** | Distance a blade element would advance in one revolution in a solid (no-slip) medium. |
| **Pitch Ratio** | **P/D** | Ratio of pitch to diameter (typical: **0.6 – 1.4**). |
| **Expanded Blade Area** | **A_E** | Sum of actual developed blade areas (not projected). |
| **Disc Area Ratio** | **A_E/A_O** | Where A_O = πD²/4; typical: 0.4 – 0.85. |
| **Rake** | – | Aftward slant of blades (degrees). |
| **Skew** | – | Asymmetric circumferential offset of the blade sections. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Third-Law Mechanism (Most Elementary Description)

The engine supplies **torque Q** to the propeller shaft. The propeller, by virtue of its rotating blades, exerts a **tangential force F_T on the water**, accelerating it circumferentially. By Newton's third law, the water exerts an **equal and opposite tangential force on the blades**. The vertical (axial) force on each blade, integrated over the disc, equals the **Thrust T**.

The reaction force on the ship hull (via shaft, thrust block, hull girder) overcomes the ship's calm-water resistance R_T, producing forward motion.

## 2.2 Momentum (Actuator Disc) Theory — The Core Engineer's Model

### Step-by-step logical breakdown:

**Step 1 — Idealization.** Replace the propeller by an *imaginary frictionless disc* of the same diameter **D** and area **A_O = πD²/4**, through which water is accelerated uniformly.

**Step 2 — Velocity field.**
- Let **V_A** = ship advance velocity through water (m/s).
- Let **v_a** = axial induced velocity at the disc (m/s).
- Let **V_w = V_A + 2v_a** = far-wake velocity (m/s).

**Step 3 — Why is far-wake velocity V_A + 2v_a?**  
By Bernoulli applied to the slipstream boundary (constant pressure), the velocity jump across the disc is **2v_a**, so the wake has accelerated from V_A by exactly 2v_a.

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate through the disc:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a)$$

**Step 5 — Thrust from axial momentum balance:**
$$T = \dot{m} \times \Delta V = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a)(2v_a)$$

**Step 6 — Useful (propulsive) power:**
$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

**Step 7 — Froude ideal efficiency (maximum achievable):**
$$\eta_F = \frac{2 v_a}{V_A + 2 v_a} \quad \text{(max = 1, only as } v_a \rightarrow \infty\text{)}$$

**Step 8 — Useful thrust in terms of thrust loading coefficient:**
$$T = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_O V_A^2 \, C_T \quad \text{where} \quad C_T = \dfrac{4 v_a(V_A+v_a)}{V_A^2}$$

## 2.3 Blade Element Theory — Sectional Force Resolution

Each blade section at radius **r** is treated as a 2-D hydrofoil moving with **inflow velocity components** (axial V_A and rotational π n r). The resultant velocity **W** makes an angle β with the plane of rotation. The blade section is set at angle (β + α_i) relative to the rotation plane, where **α_i** is the local hydrodynamic angle of attack.

Two orthogonal lift-drag forces are resolved:
- **Thrust dT** along the shaft axis.
- **Torque arm force dF_T** perpendicular to thrust, acting at radius r.

Integrating radially over all blades gives total **T** and **Q**.

## 2.4 Euler's Turbomachinery Equation Applied to the Propeller

For an inviscid, incompressible flow through a rotating blade row, **Euler's head equation** gives:

$$g H_E = U_2 V_{\theta 2} - U_1 V_{\theta 1}$$

For a propeller (energy *added* to fluid):
$$g H_E = U \, V_{\theta 2} - U \, V_{\theta 1} = U(V_{\theta 2} - V_{\theta 1}) = U \cdot \Delta V_\theta$$

Multiplying by ṁ:
$$P = \dot{m} \, U \, \Delta V_\theta = T \cdot V_A$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Euler's equation is the *exact* energy-transfer statement linking shaft work to thrust. KTU frequently asks to derive T from Euler's principle or compare momentum vs. blade-element vs. vortex theories.

## 2.5 🚀 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Advance coefficient** | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Thrust coefficient** | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho \, n^{2} \, D^{4}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Torque coefficient** | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \, n^{2} \, D^{5}}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Power coefficient** | $K_P = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, n^{3} \, D^{5}} = 2\pi K_Q$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Open-water (rotative) efficiency** | $\eta_O = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Slip ratio (apparent)** | $S = \dfrac{P - V_A/n}{P} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| 7 | **Real-slip slip ratio** | $S_R = \dfrac{P - V_A/n_w}{P}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Thrust power (useful)** | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | Watts |
| 9 | **Shaft power** | $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ | Watts |
| 10 | **Froude ideal efficiency** | $\eta_F = \dfrac{2 v_a}{V_A + 2 v_a}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Disc area** | $A_O = \pi D^{2}/4$ | m² |
| 12 | **Wake fraction** | $w = 1 - V_A/V$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Thrust deduction** | $t = (T - R_T)/T$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | **Hull efficiency** | $\eta_H = \dfrac{1 - t}{1 - w}$ | dimensionless |
| 15 | **Propulsive efficiency** | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | **Mass flow (actuator disc)** | $\dot{m} = \rho A_O(V_A + v_a)$ | kg/s |
| 17 | **Thrust from momentum** | $T = 2 \rho A_O v_a(V_A + v_a)$ | Newtons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid Markdown-Breaking:** All vertical-bar absolutes are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) so the table never breaks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Domain | Where Thrust-Propeller Theory is Applied |
|---|---|
| Commercial shipping (tankers, bulk carriers) | Optimizing fuel consumption via B-Series Wageningen or K-series propellers. |
| Warships & submarines | Cavitation-free tip design, low-radiated-noise skew. |
| Tugs & offshore vessels | High-thrust ducted or contra-rotating propellers. |
| Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) | Compact ducted thrusters using the same T-V_A-n-D scaling. |
| Wind-turbine analogy | The *windmill* is the time-reverse of a propeller; blade-element theory is shared. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Rule:** When the KTU question says *"Derive"*, you must start from a fundamental principle (Newton's 2nd/3rd law or Euler's equation) and show every line. Skipping the "Bernoulli applied across slipstream boundary" line will cost 2 marks.

---

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Thrust from Momentum (Actuator Disc) Theory

**Objective:** Derive a closed-form expression for the thrust **T** of a marine propeller in terms of advance velocity V_A, induced axial velocity v_a, water density ρ and disc area A_O.

### Pre-requisites / Assumptions:
1. Incompressible, steady, axisymmetric flow.
2. Propeller modelled as an **actuator disc** of zero thickness, infinite blade count.
3. No rotational slip (uniform pressure across disc).
4. Far upstream and far downstream pressures equal to ambient (hydrostatic).
5. Gravity neglected; horizontal momentum balance only.

### Step 1: Control Volume Selection
Enclose the propeller disc in a stream-tube of cross-section A_O that contracts downstream (the slipstream). Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to the steady control volume:

$$\sum F_x = \dot{m}_{\text{out}} V_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{in}} V_{\text{in}}$$

### Step 2: Identify Inlet and Outlet Velocities
- Inlet (far upstream): $V_{\text{in}} = V_A$
- Outlet (far downstream, wake): $V_{\text{out}} = V_A + 2 v_a$

The factor **2v_a** is the *total* velocity jump; the disc itself accelerates the flow by exactly half, v_a (a classical Rankine–Froude result derived from Bernoulli across the disc).

### Step 3: Mass Conservation
Because the stream-tube has the same mass-flow entering and leaving:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A_O \, V_A = \rho \, A_{\text{disc}} \, (V_A + v_a) = \rho \, A_w \, (V_A + 2 v_a)$$

This confirms $A_{\text{disc}} \cdot (V_A + v_a)$ is the correct mass flow *at* the disc.

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law (Axial)
The only external axial force on the CV is the **thrust T** exerted by the shaft on the fluid (which equals, by Newton's 3rd law, the fluid force on the propeller blades):

$$T = \dot{m}_{\text{disc}} \, (V_{\text{out}} - V_{\text{in}})$$

### Step 5: Substitute
$$T = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a) \, \big[(V_A + 2v_a) - V_A\big]$$

$$T = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a) \, (2 v_a)$$

### Step 6: Final Thrust Equation
$$\boxed{T = 2 \rho \, A_O \, v_a \, (V_A + v_a)}$$

This is the canonical *momentum-theory* thrust expression. When v_a is small compared to V_A, it reduces to the BEMT-engineering approximation $T \approx 2 \rho A_O v_a V_A$.

### Step 7: Power Relations
The useful (thrust) power delivered to the ship is:

$$P_T = T \cdot V_A = 2 \rho A_O v_a (V_A + v_a) \, V_A$$

The kinetic energy *imparted* to the wake per unit time is:

$$P_{KE} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} \big[(V_A + 2v_a)^2 - V_A^2\big] = 2 \rho A_O v_a (V_A + v_a)^2$$

The ideal propulsive (Froude) efficiency is therefore:

$$\eta_F = \frac{P_T}{P_{KE}} = \frac{V_A}{V_A + v_a}$$

(or equivalently $\dfrac{2v_a}{V_A + 2v_a}$ via the alternate form) — a derivation that KTU may ask in Part B.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Deriving Euler's Turbomachinery Equation for a Propeller

**Objective:** Show that thrust power = $T V_A = \dot{m} U (V_{\theta 2} - V_{\theta 1})$.

### Step 1: Angular Momentum Balance
For an open rotor (no casing), apply the angular-momentum theorem to a control volume enclosing one blade passage:

$$Q = \dot{m} \, (r_2 V_{\theta 2} - r_1 V_{\theta 1}) = \dot{m} \, U \, \Delta V_\theta$$

since $U = \pi n D_m$ (the blade speed at the mean diameter). For an *inlet guide vane absent* rotor, $V_{\theta 1} \approx 0$ (free stream has no swirl), giving:

$$Q = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2}$$

### Step 2: Energy Equation
By Euler's equation for a turbomachine:

$$g H_E = U_2 V_{\theta 2} - U_1 V_{\theta 1} = U V_{\theta 2}$$

Multiplying by mass flow $\dot{m}$ gives power transfer:

$$P = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2} = Q \, (2\pi n) = T \, V_A$$

### Step 3: Final Linking Identity

$$\boxed{P = T \cdot V_A = 2\pi n \cdot Q = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This single identity is the **golden equation** that connects thrust, torque, advance speed and shaft rpm. It is used in nearly every propeller numerical problem in KTU papers.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A four-bladed propeller of diameter **D = 5 m** rotates at **n = 120 rpm** while the ship advances at **V_A = 18 knots**. The thrust developed is **T = 800 kN**. Seawater density ρ = **1025 kg/m³**. Compute (a) advance coefficient J, (b) thrust coefficient K_T, and (c) the open-water efficiency if K_Q = 0.045.

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Convert units:**
- $n = 120/60 = 2$ rps
- $V_A = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.2592$ m/s

**Step (b) — Advance coefficient:**

$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{9.2592}{2 \times 5} = 0.9259$$

**Step (c) — Thrust coefficient:**

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{4}} = \frac{800\,000}{1025 \times 4 \times 625} = \frac{800\,000}{2\,562\,500} = 0.3122$$

**Step (d) — Open-water efficiency:**

$$\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.9259}{6.2832} \cdot \frac{0.3122}{0.045} = 0.1474 \times 6.938 = 1.022$$

This value exceeds 1, indicating an inconsistency in the input data (likely an unrealistic K_Q). The student should state: *"The computed η_O > 1 indicates non-physical input parameters; in practice K_Q for this K_T lies near 0.060 – 0.075, giving η_O ≈ 0.65 – 0.75."*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students forget to convert **knots → m/s** (multiply by 0.5144). This silent error wipes out 3 marks on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: The Three Theories

| Aspect | Momentum Theory | Blade-Element Theory | Vortex / Lifting-Line Theory |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Whole propeller | Radial strip of blade | 3-D wake geometry |
| **Assumptions** | Ideal disc, uniform loading | 2-D foil data at each section | Bound + trailing vortices |
| **Output** | T, η_F (ideal) | T, Q, η_O distribution | T, Q, η_O + cavitation details |
| **Limitation** | No blade geometry info | Needs 2-D C_L, C_D | Computationally heavy |
| **Use in KTU** | Derivation Q1 | Sketch of forces Q2 | Brief mention |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🛠️ Structural Diagrams & Functional Architecture

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Propeller Thrust Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Engine - Prime Mover] -->|Shaft Torque Q| B[Reduction Gearbox]
    B -->|Stepped n, Multiplied Q| C[Tailshaft Line]
    C --> D[Thrust Block - Hull Girder]
    C --> E[Propeller Hub]
    E -->|Rotational Energy| F[Blades Hydrofoils]
    F -->|Inflow Va and pi n r| G[Resultant Velocity W]
    G -->|Lift L and Drag D| H[Resolution into Axial Thrust dT]
    G -->|Tangential Force dFt| I[Resistance Torque dQ]
    H --> J[Integrate over Disc - Total Thrust T]
    I --> K[Integrate over Disc - Total Torque Q]
    J --> L[Hull Experiences Forward Force]
    K --> M[Engine Must Supply P = 2 pi n Q]
    L --> N[Ship Translates at Advance Speed VA]
    M --> O[Propulsive Efficiency eta_D Computed]
    F --> P[Wake Induced - Adds Slipstream]
    P --> Q[Far Wake Velocity VW equals VA plus 2va]
    Q --> R[Froude Ideal Efficiency eta_F]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow traces power from the engine (left) to translation of the ship (right), with parallel feedback loops showing how blade-element forces produce the integrated thrust and torque that the ship experiences.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Chemical Energy in Fuel] --> P2[Thermal Energy in Engine]
    P2 --> P3[Mechanical Shaft Power PD]
    P3 --> P4[Power into Propeller P equals 2 pi n Q]
    P4 --> P5[Thrust Power PT equals T times VA]
    P4 --> P6[Rotative Loss 1 minus eta_O]
    P4 --> P7[Hull Loss 1 minus eta_H]
    P5 --> P8[Effective Towing Power T times VA minus RT]
    P6 --> P9[Propeller Efficiency eta_O]
    P7 --> P10[Hull Efficiency eta_H]
    P9 --> P11[Propulsive Efficiency eta_D]
    P10 --> P11
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Velocity Triangle at a Blade Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[Advance Velocity VA Axial] --> V1[Vector Sum at Radius r]
    R0[Rotational Velocity U equals pi n r] --> V1
    V1 --> V2[Resultant Velocity W Helical]
    V2 --> V3[Blade Chord Set at Pitch Angle theta]
    V3 --> V4[Angle of Attack alpha_i equals theta minus beta]
    V4 --> V5[Lift Force L Perpendicular to W]
    V4 --> V6[Drag Force D Parallel to W]
    V5 --> V7[Axial Component - Thrust dT]
    V5 --> V8[Tangential Component - Torque Arm]
    V6 --> V7
    V6 --> V8
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation Aid:** When you draw the velocity triangle in your answer script, label V_A as horizontal arrow pointing left (toward bow), U pointing upward (or downward for clockwise rotation), and W as the hypotenuse. The chord line is drawn at the geometric pitch angle θ to the rotation plane, and the angle of attack α_i is the small wedge between chord and W.

---

## 4.4 Geometric Nomenclature — Propeller Blade Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Propeller Hub Centre] --> B[Root Section r equals 0 point 18 R]
    A --> C[Mid Section r equals 0 point 6 R]
    A --> D[Tip Section r equals 0 point 95 R]
    B --> E[Highest Pitch - Strongest Section]
    C --> F[Mean Pitch - Reference Section]
    D --> G[Lowest Pitch - Tip to Avoid Cavitation]
    H[Pitch Distribution] --> B
    H --> C
    H --> D
    I[Chord Distribution] --> B
    I --> C
    I --> D
    J[Thickness Distribution] --> B
    J --> C
    J --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pitch decreases toward the tip?** The rotational speed πnr increases with r, so the inflow angle β grows. To keep the angle of attack nearly constant (and thus C_L optimum), the pitch angle θ must be reduced toward the tip. This is what makes a propeller blade visibly *twisted*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: thrust coefficient (K_T) and torque coefficient (K_Q) of a marine propeller. State their physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **thrust coefficient** is a non-dimensional number that expresses the thrust developed by a propeller in a form independent of operational scale:

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{4}}$$

where T = thrust (N), ρ = water density (kg/m³), n = rotational speed (rps) and D = diameter (m).

The **torque coefficient** is similarly non-dimensionalized:

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{5}}$$

where Q = torque (N·m).

**Physical significance:** Because K_T and K_Q depend only on the propeller geometry (P/D, blade area ratio, blade count, section shape) and the advance coefficient J = V_A/(nD), they allow **direct comparison of geometrically similar propellers at different sizes, speeds and densities** — a cornerstone of model-basin extrapolation.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the principle of operation of a marine propeller as a thrust producing mechanism. Mention the relevant physical law.**

**Model Answer:**

A marine propeller operates on **Newton's Third Law of Motion** (action and reaction are equal and opposite). The engine supplies torque to the propeller shaft; the rotating blades exert a tangential accelerating force on a large mass of water, increasing its axial (and rotational) momentum. The equal and opposite reaction force acts on the propeller blades and, transmitted through the shaft and thrust block to the hull, becomes the **useful thrust T** that overcomes the calm-water resistance R_T of the ship and propels it forward.

The thrust is given by the momentum expression:
$$T = \dot{m} (V_{\text{wake}} - V_A) = \rho A_O (V_A + v_a)(2v_a)$$

---

## 🅱️ Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**  
> • In derivation questions, students often *write the final formula without showing the Bernoulli or angular-momentum step*. This loses **2 of 7 marks** per sub-question.  
> • In numericals, **forgetting unit conversion (knots → m/s, rpm → rps)** is the #1 mark-deduction reason. Always write conversions explicitly.  
> • When asked "explain the assumptions", students list only 2 — you must state **at least four** (incompressibility, axisymmetry, uniform disc loading, pressure equal far upstream/downstream, no rotation at far upstream).  
> • Drawing the velocity triangle **without labelling angles β, α_i, θ** loses 1 mark immediately.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for thrust developed by a marine propeller using the **momentum theory (actuator disc)** approach. Clearly state all assumptions and explain each step of the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-bladed propeller of diameter **D = 4.5 m** rotates at **n = 130 rpm** in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³). The ship speed is **15 knots** and the open-water torque coefficient is **K_Q = 0.052**. If the thrust coefficient K_T = 0.30, calculate the **(i)** thrust developed, **(ii)** shaft power required, and **(iii)** open-water efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, axisymmetric flow.
2. Propeller modelled as an ideal frictionless actuator disc of area A_O = πD²/4.
3. Uniform pressure jump across the disc.
4. Far-upstream velocity = V_A; far-downstream velocity = V_w = V_A + 2v_a.
5. Velocity at the disc itself = V_A + v_a (Bernoulli across streamline boundary).
6. No rotational component far upstream (V_θ1 = 0).

**Derivation:**

Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem to a control volume that encloses the disc:

$$T = \dot{m} \, (V_w - V_A)$$

Continuity gives mass flow at the disc:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a)$$

Wake velocity (from Bernoulli applied across slipstream boundary):

$$V_w = V_A + 2 v_a$$

Substituting:

$$T = \rho \, A_O \, (V_A + v_a)(2 v_a)$$

$$\boxed{T = 2 \rho A_O v_a (V_A + v_a)}$$

Useful thrust power:

$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

Froude ideal efficiency:

$$\eta_F = \frac{V_A}{V_A + v_a}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Unit conversions:**
- n = 130/60 = 2.1667 rps
- V_A = 15 × 0.5144 = 7.716 m/s

**Step 2 — Thrust developed:**
$$T = K_T \cdot \rho \, n^2 D^4 = 0.30 \times 1025 \times (2.1667)^2 \times (4.5)^4$$

$$T = 0.30 \times 1025 \times 4.6944 \times 410.0625$$

$$T = 0.30 \times 1025 \times 1925.04 = 591{,}951 \text{ N} \approx 592 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft power:**
First compute torque:
$$Q = K_Q \cdot \rho \, n^2 D^5 = 0.052 \times 1025 \times 4.6944 \times (4.5)^5$$

$(4.5)^5 = 1845.28$, so:

$$Q = 0.052 \times 1025 \times 4.6944 \times 1845.28 = 462{,}684 \text{ N·m}$$

Shaft power:
$$P_D = 2\pi n Q = 2\pi \times 2.1667 \times 462{,}684 = 6{,}298{,}910 \text{ W} \approx 6.30 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Open-water efficiency:**
Advance coefficient:
$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D} = \frac{7.716}{2.1667 \times 4.5} = \frac{7.716}{9.750} = 0.7914$$

$$\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.7914}{6.2832} \times \frac{0.30}{0.052} = 0.1260 \times 5.769 = 0.727$$

$$\boxed{\eta_O \approx 72.7\%}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice for Q.A)*

`[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a marine propeller as a thrust producing mechanism using **Euler's turbomachinery equation**. Show that thrust power $P_T = T V_A = 2\pi n Q$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat velocity-triangle diagram at a typical blade section, derive the expressions for **dT** (elemental thrust) and **dQ** (elemental torque) using **blade-element theory**. State the role of lift and drag coefficients. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Euler's turbomachinery equation** for energy transfer per unit weight of fluid:

$$g H_E = U_2 V_{\theta 2} - U_1 V_{\theta 1}$$

For a propeller (energy is *added* to the fluid; rotor adds swirl):

- $U_1 = U_2 = U$ (same radius)
- $V_{\theta 1} \approx 0$ (free stream has no swirl upstream)
- $V_{\theta 2}$ = tangential velocity of wake at exit

Therefore:
$$g H_E = U V_{\theta 2}$$

Power transferred to the fluid per unit time:
$$P = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2}$$

Using angular momentum theorem, torque:
$$Q = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2}$$

Hence power:
$$P = Q \cdot \omega = Q \cdot (2\pi n)$$

But this same power also equals the useful thrust power:

$$P_T = T \cdot V_A$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{T \cdot V_A = 2\pi n \cdot Q = \dot{m} \, U \, V_{\theta 2}}$$

This is the **fundamental propeller energy identity** linking thrust, torque, advance velocity and rotational speed.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

Consider a small blade element at radius r, radial width dr, span-wise chord c. The element experiences a relative velocity W at inflow angle β.

**Velocity triangle at section:**
- Axial component: V_A
- Rotational component: U = 2π n r
- Resultant: W = √(V_A² + U²)
- Geometric pitch angle: θ
- Hydrodynamic angle of attack: α_i = θ − β

**Forces on the element (per blade):**
- Lift: $dL = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho W^{2} c \, C_L \, dr$ (perpendicular to W)
- Drag: $dD = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho W^{2} c \, C_D \, dr$ (parallel to W)

**Resolving into thrust and tangential-force directions:**

$$dT = dL \cos\beta - dD \sin\beta$$

$$dF_T = dL \sin\beta + dD \cos\beta$$

**Torque contribution of the element (per blade):**

$$dQ = r \, dF_T = r(dL \sin\beta + dD \cos\beta)$$

**Integrating over B blades and full radial extent (root r_h to tip R):**

$$T = B \int_{r_h}^{R} \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^{2} c (C_L \cos\beta - C_D \sin\beta)\, dr$$

$$Q = B \int_{r_h}^{R} \tfrac{1}{2}\rho W^{2} c r (C_L \sin\beta + C_D \cos\beta)\, dr$$

**Role of C_L and C_D:**
- **C_L (lift coefficient)** dominates thrust generation; higher C_L → more thrust (designed near stall margin).
- **C_D (drag coefficient)** contributes a small *positive* thrust (when α_i < 90° − β) but a *resistive* torque component; minimised by selecting thin, laminar-flow-friendly sections.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Sheet — Memorize Before Exam**

- ⚓ A marine propeller is a **rotating lifting-surface device** that converts shaft torque into **axial thrust** by accelerating water astern — operation rests on **Newton's Third Law**.

- ⚓ **Three theory levels**: *Momentum (actuator disc)* → global T, η_F; *Blade-Element* → dT and dQ distributions; *Vortex / Lifting-Line* → wake geometry and cavitation. Always mention the hierarchy in essay-type answers.

- ⚓ **Thrust from momentum theory** (memorize verbatim):
  $$T = 2 \rho A_O v_a (V_A + v_a)$$

- ⚓ **Euler's identity** (the golden link):
  $$T V_A = 2 \pi n Q = \dot{m} U V_{\theta 2}$$

- ⚓ **Standard non-dimensional coefficients**:
  - Advance coefficient: $J = V_A/(nD)$
  - Thrust coefficient: $K_T = T/(\rho n^2 D^4)$
  - Torque coefficient: $K_Q = Q/(\rho n^2 D^5)$
  - Power coefficient: $K_P = 2\pi K_Q$
  - Open-water efficiency: $\eta_O = (J/2\pi)(K_T/K_Q)$

- ⚓ **Standard seawater density** ρ = **1025 kg/m³**; conversion 1 knot = **0.5144 m/s**; rpm → rps by dividing by **60**.

- ⚓ **Froude ideal efficiency** $\eta_F = V_A/(V_A + v_a)$; maximum is 1 only as v_a → ∞ (unrealizable).

- ⚓ **Pitch decreases from root to tip** because the rotational velocity component U = π n r increases radially — this is why blades are visibly *twisted*.

- ⚓ **Right-hand rule:** marine propellers rotate **clockwise when viewed from astern** (port-side convention).

- ⚓ **Three forces on each blade element:** lift (L) perpendicular to W, drag (D) parallel to W, resolved into thrust (dT) and tangential (dF_T) which gives dQ = r · dF_T.

- ⚓ **Slip ratio** $S = (P - V_A/n)/P$ — a measure of how much the propeller "slips" relative to a solid medium.

- ⚓ **Hull, rotative and propulsive efficiencies**: $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O$ where $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ and $t$, $w$ are thrust deduction and wake fraction.

- ⚓ **Exam Pitfalls (losing 1–3 marks):**
  1. Forgetting **unit conversion** (knots → m/s, rpm → rps).
  2. Drawing velocity triangle **without labelling β, θ, α_i**.
  3. Writing the formula $T = \rho A_O v_a (V_A + v_a)$ (missing the factor 2) — **always T = 2 ρ A_O v_a (V_A + v_a)**.
  4. Confusing **rotative** efficiency η_O with **propulsive** efficiency η_D.
  5. Skipping the "since $V_{\theta 1} = 0$" line in Euler derivations.
  6. Using fresh-water density (1000) when problem states seawater (1025).
  7. Not stating the **assumption of incompressible / steady / axisymmetric** flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 scheme ESE, derivations are mandatory for full marks on 7-mark sub-questions. Always write the principle → assumptions → control volume → mathematical statement → solution → final boxed equation. Memorize the boxed equations from §3 above verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Propeller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screw Propeller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚓ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal Academic Definition

A **Screw Propeller** (also termed a **marine screw propeller** or **controllable pitch propeller** in its variable form) is the most widely used propulsion device in modern marine engineering. It is essentially a *rotating helical screw* — typically consisting of a **hub** and a finite number (commonly 3, 4, or 5) of **blades** — that converts the **rotational mechanical power** delivered by the main engine (via the shafting system) into a **thrust force**, which overcomes the hydrodynamic resistance of the ship hull and propels the vessel through water.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a screw propeller is a **facewise-mounted, sub-surface, fluid-reacting propulsion device** that operates on the principle of **Newton's Third Law of Motion** and the **momentum theory of propulsion**, generating thrust by accelerating a column of water in the **astern direction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The screw propeller is the *primary propulsion element* of almost all displacement-type and semi-displacement-type vessels. It is the single most important interface between the **propulsion plant** (engine + gearbox + shaft) and the **hull resistance**.

### 🔧 Geometric Anatomy of a Screw Propeller

| Component | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **Hub** | The central cylindrical boss that attaches the blades to the propeller shaft; typically 18%–22% of propeller diameter for merchant ships. |
| **Blades** | Helically twisted surfaces that generate thrust by imparting momentum to the fluid. |
| **Leading Edge** | The edge of the blade that first encounters the oncoming water during rotation. |
| **Trailing Edge** | The edge from which water exits after imparting thrust. |
| **Root** | The inner end of the blade where it joins the hub. |
| **Tip** | The outermost point of the blade (radius ≈ D/2). |
| **Boss Cap / Fairing** | A streamlined dome that reduces hub cavitation and drag. |

## 💡 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Rotating Screw" Intuition

Imagine you take a large **wood screw** (the kind used in carpentry) and mount it on a shaft. Now submerge this screw in water and spin it rapidly. Because the screw has a helical thread, as it rotates, each *turn* of the thread *pushes* a parcel of water backwards (astern). By Newton's Third Law, the reaction force pushes the screw — and the ship attached to it — *forward*.

This is exactly how a marine screw propeller works, but with two critical refinements:

1. Instead of a continuous thread, the marine propeller has **discrete blades** (typically 3–5), each blade acting like one *turn* of an imaginary screw thread.
2. The blades are **helically twisted** — the *pitch angle* is large near the hub (where rotational velocity is low) and small near the tip (where rotational velocity is high). This twist ensures that **every radial section of the blade operates at approximately the same hydrodynamic angle of attack**, producing uniform thrust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Helicopter Rotor:** A ship's propeller is conceptually identical to a helicopter rotor in *reverse* — instead of pushing air *down* to lift the helicopter *up*, the marine propeller pushes water *aft* to drive the ship *forward*.

---

## 🌊 Fundamental Operating Principle

When a propeller rotates at **rotational speed `n` (in revolutions per second, rps)**, every point on the blade traces a **helical path** through the water. The axial distance covered by one full revolution is called the **geometric pitch `P`**. 

If the water were *inviscid* and the propeller were operating in a *uniform inflow*, the ship would advance a distance equal to `P` per revolution. In reality, due to **slip**, the ship advances a smaller distance called the **advance per revolution `p_a`**.

$$\text{Geometric Pitch} \; P \;>\; \text{Advance per Revolution} \; p_a$$

The difference `P − p_a` is termed the **slip**, and the **slip ratio** is one of the most fundamental performance parameters used in propeller analysis.

## 📐 Key Geometric Parameters (KTU-Standard Notation)

- **D** = Propeller diameter (m)
- **P** = Geometric pitch (m), measured at the **blade reference line (0.7R)** for design purposes
- **P/D** = **Pitch ratio** (dimensionless; typically 0.6–1.4 for merchant ships)
- **BAR** = Blade Area Ratio = (Total expanded blade area) / (π D²/4) (dimensionless; 0.3–1.05)
- **Z** = Number of blades (typically 3, 4, or 5)
- **n** = Rotational speed (rps or rpm)
- **n_D** = Revolutions per second × D (used in Reynolds number scaling)
- **V_A** = **Advance velocity** of the propeller through water = V(1 − w), where V is ship speed and w is the **wake fraction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The mnemonic **"P-D-B-Z-N-V"** stands for *Pitch, Diameter, Blade Area Ratio, Number of blades, Rotational speed, Advance velocity* — the six master variables of any propeller problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## ⚙️ Theoretical Framework — How a Screw Propeller Generates Thrust

The propulsion process can be decomposed into **four sequential energy-transfer stages**, each governed by a distinct efficiency:

### Stage 1: Engine → Shaft (Mechanical Efficiency η_m)
The main engine delivers brake power **P_B** to the crankshaft. A fraction is lost to friction in the gearbox, thrust bearing, and stern tube. The remaining **delivered power P_D** = η_m · P_B reaches the propeller.

### Stage 2: Shaft → Propeller (Propeller Open-Water Efficiency η_O)
The propeller, rotating in the **wake field** of the hull, converts P_D into **thrust power T · V_A**, while losses occur due to rotational slip, frictional drag, and rotational (tip) losses. The **open-water efficiency** is defined as:

$$\eta_O = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q}$$

where **T** = thrust (N) and **Q** = torque (N·m).

### Stage 3: Propeller → Hull Interaction (Hull Efficiency η_H)
The propeller operates in the **suction field** of the hull. Two effects reduce the *effective* thrust available to overcome ship resistance:

- **Wake fraction w**: Ship speed at the propeller disc < ship speed at the stern (V_A = V(1−w)).
- **Thrust deduction t**: The propeller accelerates flow near the stern, *increasing* hull resistance by a fraction **t** of the thrust.

$$\eta_H = \frac{V(1-t)}{V_A} = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** η_H is *not* a propeller property — it depends on the **hull form**, particularly the fullness of the afterbody and the presence of a **deadwood** region. Tankers typically have high w (0.25–0.40) and low η_H; fine-lined vessels have low w (0.10–0.20) and high η_H.

### Stage 4: Useful Work Output (Propulsive Coefficient η_D or PC)
Combining all three stages:

$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_m \quad \text{(where } \eta_R \text{ is the relative rotative efficiency)}$$

For a typical single-screw merchant ship: η_D ≈ 0.55–0.65.

---

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula** required for KTU board examinations on Screw Propellers. Note that **no vertical pipe symbols (`|`) are used**; LaTeX `\vert` is used for absolute-value notation to preserve table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Advance velocity** | $V_A = V(1-w)$ | V = ship speed (m/s), w = wake fraction | Speed of water entering propeller disc |
| 2 | **Advance coefficient** | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | dimensionless | KTU's *master* similarity parameter |
| 3 | **Slip ratio** | $S_R = \dfrac{P - p_a}{P}$ | dimensionless | Measures geometric vs actual advance |
| 4 | **Apparent slip** | $S_A = \dfrac{P \cdot n - V_A}{P \cdot n}$ | dimensionless | Used with stock propellers |
| 5 | **Thrust coefficient** | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{4}}$ | dimensionless | ρ = water density (1025 kg/m³ seawater) |
| 6 | **Torque coefficient** | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{5}}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | **Open-water efficiency** | $\eta_O = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | dimensionless | Combines geometry & loading |
| 8 | **Hull efficiency** | $\eta_H = \dfrac{1-t}{1-w}$ | dimensionless | Pure hull-propeller interaction term |
| 9 | **Propulsive efficiency** | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | dimensionless | Overall ship-side efficiency |
| 10 | **Thrust power** | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | Watts | Useful work rate on water |
| 11 | **Delivered power** | $P_D = 2\pi n Q$ | Watts | Power delivered to propeller |
| 12 | **Reynolds number (prop)** | $Re = \dfrac{c_{0.75R} \sqrt{V_A^{2} + (0.75\pi n D)^{2}}}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | ν ≈ 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s seawater |
| 13 | **Cavitation number (sigma)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{0.7R}^{2}}$ | dimensionless | σ < σ_critical ⇒ cavitation |
| 14 | **Power absorption (EHP)** | $EHP = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V}{75}$ | metric HP (1 HP = 735.5 W) | R_T = total resistance (kgf) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Almost every numerical problem in the KTU board exam tests **at least one formula from Rows 1–9 of the above table**. Memorize the dimensionless coefficients (K_T, K_Q, J) — they are the foundation of the **Wageningen B-Series charts**.

## 🌐 Real-World Engineering Utility

The screw propeller is the workhorse of global shipping:

- **~90% of commercial tonnage** uses fixed-pitch screw propellers.
- **Container ships** (e.g., 24,000 TEU class) operate at P/D ≈ 0.6–0.8 with single 10 m+ diameter propellers.
- **Naval submarines** use **controllable-pitch propellers (CPP)** with 5–7 blades for silent operation.
- **Azimuth thrusters** (Voith Schneider, Z-drive) are screw propellers mounted in rotatable pods for superior maneuverability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **complete, exhaustive derivations** of the most important KTU-board-tested relations. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 📘 Derivation 1: The Thrust Coefficient K_T

### Starting Physical Statement
Thrust **T** generated by a propeller depends on the dynamic pressure of the flow it encounters, the disc area, and the loading density. By dimensional analysis using the Buckingham π-theorem with the governing variables (T, ρ, n, D), we obtain two independent dimensionless groups.

### Step 1 — Identify Governing Variables
The thrust T is a function of:
- Water density **ρ** [kg/m³]
- Rotational speed **n** [rev/s = s⁻¹]
- Propeller diameter **D** [m]

$$T = f(\rho, n, D)$$

### Step 2 — Dimensional Analysis (M, L, T System)

| Variable | M | L | T |
|---|---|---|---|
| T (thrust, force) | 1 | 1 | −2 |
| ρ | 1 | −3 | 0 |
| n | 0 | 0 | −1 |
| D | 0 | 1 | 0 |

We have **4 variables** and **3 fundamental dimensions** ⇒ **n − k = 1** dimensionless group from dimensional analysis, BUT in propeller theory we additionally know thrust scales as **ρ·(rotational velocity)²·(geometric scale)²** implicitly via fluid momentum transfer, which gives the canonical **K_T = T / (ρ n² D⁴)**.

### Step 3 — Construct the Coefficient
The thrust scales as ρ (density) × (n D)² (square of rotational velocity at the tip) × D² (disc area):

$$T \;\propto\; \rho \, (nD)^{2} \, D^{2} \;=\; \rho \, n^{2} \, D^{4}$$

Therefore, the dimensionless thrust coefficient is:

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{4}}$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation
- Numerator **T** = actual thrust produced.
- Denominator **ρ n² D⁴** = a *reference force* representing the momentum flux capacity of the disc.

Higher K_T at a given J means the propeller is **more heavily loaded** (more thrust per unit disc loading capacity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why n²D⁴?** Because thrust is force ∝ ρ × (velocity)² × (area), and for a rotating disc the relevant velocity is **π n D** (circumferential), while the relevant area is **π D²/4**. Their combination with ρ yields ρ n² D⁴ after constants are absorbed into K_T.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: The Torque Coefficient K_Q

### Step 1 — Torque from Rotational Equilibrium
The torque Q must overcome the moment of the hydrodynamic forces about the propeller axis. By analogy with thrust, it must scale with ρ × n² × D⁵ (one extra power of D compared to thrust, since torque = force × lever arm).

### Step 2 — Construct the Coefficient

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho \, n^{2} D^{5}}$$

### Step 3 — Units Check
- Numerator [Q] = N·m = kg·m²·s⁻²
- Denominator [ρ n² D⁵] = kg·m⁻³ · s⁻² · m⁵ = kg·m²·s⁻² ✓

The dimension cancels exactly.

---

## 📘 Derivation 3: Advance Coefficient J

### Step 1 — Definition
The advance coefficient is the ratio of the translational (axial) velocity of the propeller through water to the rotational (circumferential) tip velocity:

$$J = \frac{V_A}{n D}$$

### Step 2 — Why Use This Specific Form?
- It is **dimensionless** → enables comparison between model (towing tank) and full-scale propeller.
- For a given propeller geometry, **K_T and K_Q are functions of J alone** (in open water, ignoring Reynolds and cavitation effects).

### Step 3 — Inverse Relation to Slip
A low J (e.g., J = 0.2) means the propeller is rotating fast relative to its forward motion — equivalent to high geometric slip. A high J (e.g., J = 0.9) means efficient translation with low slip.

---

## 📘 Derivation 4: Open-Water Efficiency η_O

### Step 1 — Useful Power Output
The *useful* power delivered to the water as thrust is:

$$P_{thrust} = T \cdot V_A$$

### Step 2 — Power Input to Propeller
The mechanical power delivered to the propeller shaft is:

$$P_D = 2 \pi n Q$$

### Step 3 — Define Efficiency

$$\eta_O = \frac{P_{thrust}}{P_D} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Dimensionless Coefficients
Substituting $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$ and $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$:

$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T \rho n^{2} D^{4} \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n \cdot K_Q \rho n^{2} D^{5}}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify Algebraically
Cancel ρ, n², and D⁴:

$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n \cdot K_Q \cdot D}$$

### Step 6 — Introduce J
Recognizing that $J = V_A / (nD)$, so $V_A = J n D$:

$$\eta_O = \frac{K_T \cdot (J n D)}{2 \pi n \cdot K_Q \cdot D} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

### Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation appears in **almost every** board exam question on screw propellers. Practice writing it from memory.

---

## 📘 Derivation 5: Hull Efficiency η_H

### Step 1 — Effective Resistance vs Useful Thrust Work
- The **effective power (EHP)** is the work done against the *actual* hull resistance R_T at ship speed V:

$$EHP = R_T \cdot V$$

- The **thrust power (THP)** is the work done by the propeller's thrust T at the advance velocity V_A:

$$THP = T \cdot V_A$$

### Step 2 — Relate T to R_T Using Thrust Deduction
The thrust T produced by the propeller must overcome the *augmented* resistance due to the propeller's suction effect:

$$T = \frac{R_T}{1 - t}$$

Therefore:

$$THP = \frac{R_T \cdot V_A}{1 - t}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Efficiency Ratio

$$\eta_H = \frac{EHP}{THP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{R_T \cdot V_A / (1-t)} = \frac{V(1-t)}{V_A}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute V_A = V(1 − w)

$$\eta_H = \frac{V(1-t)}{V(1-w)} = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

### Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Since typically t > w (thrust deduction is usually slightly larger than wake fraction for single-screw ships), η_H is *less than 1*, often around **0.95–1.05**. Values above 1 are possible for fine ships where w > t.

---

## 📘 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem
A model propeller of diameter D = 0.25 m is tested in a towing tank at rotational speed n = 15 rps. The measured thrust is T = 95 N and torque is Q = 4.2 N·m. The advance velocity in the test is V_A = 2.5 m/s. Seawater density ρ = 1025 kg/m³. Compute J, K_T, K_Q, and η_O.

### Solution — Step 1: Advance Coefficient

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{2.5}{15 \times 0.25} = \frac{2.5}{3.75} = 0.6667$$

### Solution — Step 2: Thrust Coefficient

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^{2} D^{4}} = \frac{95}{1025 \times 225 \times (0.25)^{4}}$$

$$K_T = \frac{95}{1025 \times 225 \times 0.00390625} = \frac{95}{900.6} \approx 0.1055$$

### Solution — Step 3: Torque Coefficient

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^{2} D^{5}} = \frac{4.2}{1025 \times 225 \times (0.25)^{5}}$$

$$K_Q = \frac{4.2}{1025 \times 225 \times 0.0009766} = \frac{4.2}{225.16} \approx 0.01865$$

### Solution — Step 4: Open-Water Efficiency

$$\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.6667}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{0.1055}{0.01865}$$

$$\eta_O = 0.1061 \times 5.656 = 0.600 \;(\approx 60\%)$$

This result is realistic for a well-designed model propeller.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1 — Propeller Geometry & Helical Path

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional flow of geometric parameters** from raw blade profile to integrated hydrodynamic performance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Blade Geometry] --> B[Hub Diameter dH]
    A --> C[Blade Tip Diameter DT]
    A --> D[Mean Pitch P at 0.7R]
    A --> E[Expanded Blade Area AE]
    
    B --> F[Hub Ratio dH slash D = 0.18 to 0.22]
    C --> G[Diameter D]
    D --> H[Pitch Ratio P slash D = 0.6 to 1.4]
    E --> I[Blade Area Ratio BAR = AE / pi D squared / 4]
    
    F --> J[KTU Performance Block]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    
    K[Operating Conditions] --> L[Rotational Speed n]
    K --> M[Advance Velocity VA]
    
    L --> N[Advance Coefficient J = VA / nD]
    M --> N
    
    N --> O[Thrust Coefficient KT from B-Series]
    N --> P[Torque Coefficient KQ from B-Series]
    
    O --> Q[Thrust T = KT rho n squared D to the 4]
    P --> R[Torque Q = KQ rho n squared D to the 5]
    
    Q --> S[Open Water Efficiency eta_O]
    R --> S
    N --> S
    
    S --> T[Final KTU Output]
```

## 📊 Diagram 2 — Energy Flow Through the Propulsion System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Brake Power PB from Main Engine] -->|eta_m| P2[Delivered Power PD at Propeller]
    P2 -->|eta_O| P3[Thrust Power TP = T times VA]
    P3 -->|eta_H inverse| P4[Effective Power EHP = RT times V]
    P4 --> P5[Useful Work Towing the Ship]
    
    P2 -.-> P2a[Rotational Losses]
    P3 -.-> P3a[Slip and Rotational Losses]
    P4 -.-> P4a[Thrust Deduction Loss]
```

## 📊 Diagram 3 — Velocity Triangle at Propeller Blade Section

The velocity triangle is the **fundamental analytical tool** used to derive blade section geometry (camber, thickness, pitch angle) at any radius r. The following diagram represents the three-component velocity decomposition:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[Inflow Velocity VA Axial] --> V2[Velocity Triangle at Radius r]
    V3[Rotational Velocity 2 pi n r Tangential] --> V2
    
    V2 --> V4[Resultant Relative Velocity W]
    V4 --> V5[Angle of Attack alpha]
    V4 --> V6[Hidrodynamic Pitch Angle beta]
    
    V5 --> V7[Blade Section Design Pitch Angle theta]
    V6 --> V7
    
    V7 --> V8[Final Cambered Blade Profile]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The blade twist (camber variation from root to tip) is designed so that **every radial section sees approximately the same angle of attack α** at the design advance coefficient J, ensuring uniform thrust distribution and avoiding local cavitation.

## 📊 Diagram 4 — Propeller Slip Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ideal No Slip Case] --> B[Propeller Advances P per Revolution = P x n]
    C[Real Case with Slip] --> D[Ship Advances pA per Revolution = VA / n]
    
    B --> E[Slip = P minus pA]
    D --> E
    
    E --> F[Slip Ratio SR = P minus pA / P]
    
    F --> G{Is SR within 10 to 25 percent?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Optimal Design]
    G -->|No| I[Pitch or Diameter Adjustment Needed]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following with respect to a screw propeller:**
**(a)** Geometric pitch **P**    **(b)** Pitch ratio **P/D**    **(c)** Slip ratio **S_R**

#### ✅ Model Answer:
**(a)** **Geometric pitch (P):** The axial distance a propeller would advance in one complete revolution if it were operating in a *solid* (non-slipping) medium. It is measured at the **blade reference radius 0.7R** for design purposes. Unit: metres (m).

**(b)** **Pitch ratio (P/D):** The ratio of the geometric pitch to the propeller diameter. It is a *dimensionless* design parameter that determines the propeller's "screw-like" character. Typical range for merchant ships: **0.6 to 1.4**. Low P/D = high-thrust, low-speed propellers; high P/D = high-speed, low-thrust propellers.

**(c)** **Slip ratio (S_R):** Defined as

$$S_R = \frac{P - p_a}{P}$$

where **p_a = V_A / n** is the actual advance per revolution. It quantifies the *fractional loss* of geometric advance due to water-yielding during rotation. Typical values: **10% to 25%** for well-designed propellers.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between "apparent slip" and "real slip" of a screw propeller. Why does real slip differ from apparent slip?**

#### ✅ Model Answer:
- **Real slip (or true slip):** Computed using the *actual geometric pitch P* of the propeller. 
$$S_{real} = \frac{P \cdot n - V_A}{P \cdot n}$$

- **Apparent slip:** Computed using the *nominal pitch P_n* marked on a stock propeller (which may differ slightly from the true expanded pitch due to manufacturing tolerance and blade thickness effects).

The two differ because the **nominal pitch P_n** quoted in stock propeller tables is the *pitch of the screw helix traced by the blade centerline*, whereas the *effective* pitch that produces thrust is the **developed pitch at the 0.7R reference line**, which accounts for blade thickness distribution. Additionally, in **ship trials**, V_A is estimated using the wake fraction w, which itself contains measurement uncertainty, introducing further divergence.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🔵 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **geometric features** of a marine screw propeller. Define **diameter (D)**, **pitch (P)**, **blade area ratio (BAR)**, and **pitch ratio (P/D)**. Discuss how the choice of **P/D** influences the propeller's operating regime (high-thrust vs. high-speed). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 4-bladed propeller of diameter **D = 6 m** rotates at **n = 90 rpm** while the ship advances at **V = 15 knots**. The wake fraction is **w = 0.30** and thrust deduction is **t = 0.15**. If the thrust coefficient is **K_T = 0.18** and torque coefficient is **K_Q = 0.030**, compute:
  - (i) The advance velocity V_A
  - (ii) The advance coefficient J
  - (iii) The thrust T (use ρ = 1025 kg/m³)
  - (iv) The torque Q
  - (v) The open-water efficiency η_O
  - (vi) The hull efficiency η_H
  - (vii) The propulsive efficiency η_D = η_O · η_H **[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

**Geometric Features of a Marine Screw Propeller** (Refer to Section 1 diagram):

A screw propeller consists of:
1. **Hub**: Central cylindrical boss of diameter d_H, typically d_H/D = 0.18 to 0.22.
2. **Blades**: Helically twisted airfoil-shaped surfaces. Each blade has a **leading edge**, **trailing edge**, **root**, and **tip**.
3. **Expanded blade area A_E**: The actual surface area of one blade (when developed flat) multiplied by Z (number of blades).

**Definitions:**
- **Diameter D**: Tip-to-tip distance through the propeller axis. Determines the disc area A_0 = πD²/4.
- **Pitch P**: Axial advance per revolution in a solid medium, measured at 0.7R.
- **BAR (Blade Area Ratio)**: A_E / (πD²/4). Indicates blade "solidity".
- **Pitch ratio P/D**: P / D, dimensionless design parameter.

**Influence of P/D on Operating Regime:**
- **Low P/D (0.5–0.7)**: High blade area loading → high thrust at low speed → used for **tugboats, fishing vessels, icebreakers**.
- **Medium P/D (0.7–1.0)**: Balanced design → used for **bulk carriers, tankers**.
- **High P/D (1.0–1.4)**: High-speed, lightly loaded → used for **container ships, naval frigates**.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- D = 6 m, n = 90 rpm = 1.5 rps, V = 15 knots
- w = 0.30, t = 0.15
- K_T = 0.18, K_Q = 0.030, ρ = 1025 kg/m³

**Convert ship speed:**
V = 15 knots × 0.5144 m/s per knot = **7.716 m/s**

**(i) Advance velocity:**

$$V_A = V(1-w) = 7.716 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7.716 \times 0.70 = 5.401 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Advance coefficient:**

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{5.401}{1.5 \times 6} = \frac{5.401}{9.0} = 0.6001$$

**(iii) Thrust T:**

$$T = K_T \, \rho \, n^{2} \, D^{4} = 0.18 \times 1025 \times (1.5)^{2} \times (6)^{4}$$

$$T = 0.18 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 1296 = 0.18 \times 2{,}988{,}360 = 5.379 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N}$$

**(iv) Torque Q:**

$$Q = K_Q \, \rho \, n^{2} \, D^{5} = 0.030 \times 1025 \times 2.25 \times 7776 = 5.381 \times 10^{5} \; \text{N·m}$$

**(v) Open-water efficiency:**

$$\eta_O = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.6001}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{0.18}{0.030} = 0.0955 \times 6.0 = 0.573$$

**(vi) Hull efficiency:**

$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{1-0.15}{1-0.30} = \frac{0.85}{0.70} = 1.214$$

**(vii) Propulsive efficiency:**

$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.573 \times 1.214 = 0.696 \;(\approx 69.6\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rps** — dividing by 60 is mandatory (1.5 rps, not 90 rps).
> 2. **Mixing up knots and m/s** — 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s; writing V = 15 m/s directly is a common error worth −1 mark.
> 3. **Reporting η_H > 1 without comment** — KTU accepts this for fine ships but penalizes students who don't *state* that η_H > 1 is physically valid here.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — always state N for thrust, N·m for torque, dimensionless for efficiencies.

---

### 🟢 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Wageningen B-Series method** of propeller design. Why are B-Series charts preferred over analytical methods for predicting open-water performance? Mention the key parameters that B-Series charts require as input. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A single-screw cargo vessel has a **total resistance R_T = 200 kN** at a service speed of **V = 12 knots**. The propeller operates with:
- Diameter D = 4.5 m, P = 3.6 m
- Rotational speed n = 130 rpm
- Open-water efficiency η_O = 0.62
- Wake fraction w = 0.28, thrust deduction t = 0.14

Determine:
- (i) The slip ratio S_R
- (ii) The thrust T produced by the propeller
- (iii) The torque Q absorbed
- (iv) The delivered power P_D
- (v) The hull efficiency η_H
- (vi) The propulsive coefficient η_D **[7 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

**Wageningen B-Series Method:**

The **B-Series** (also called the **Wageningen B-Series**) is a family of systematically tested propeller series developed between **1968 and 1975** at **Maritime Research Institute Netherlands (MARIN)** in Wageningen, Netherlands, under the leadership of Professor W.P.A. van Lammeren.

**Key Features:**
- Over **120 model propellers** were tested in the MARIN towing tank across a wide range of:
  - Pitch ratios (P/D = 0.5 to 1.4)
  - Blade area ratios (BAR = 0.3 to 1.05)
  - Number of blades (Z = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7)
- Results are presented as **polynomial regression curves** for K_T, K_Q, and η_O as functions of **J, P/D, BAR, Z**.

**Why Preferred over Analytical Methods:**
1. **Empirical validation** — Based on extensive model tests, not simplified theory.
2. **Wide applicability** — Covers the design space of virtually all merchant ship propellers.
3. **Cavitation-ready** — Includes cavitation bucket boundaries for tip-vortex and sheet cavitation.
4. **Reynolds-scaling** — Empirical corrections allow extrapolation to full scale.

**Required Input Parameters:**
- Advance coefficient **J**
- Pitch ratio **P/D**
- Blade area ratio **EAR** (or BAR)
- Number of blades **Z**

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:**
- R_T = 200 kN, V = 12 knots
- D = 4.5 m, P = 3.6 m, n = 130 rpm
- η_O = 0.62, w = 0.28, t = 0.14

**Convert:**
V = 12 × 0.5144 = **6.173 m/s**
n = 130 / 60 = **2.167 rps**

**(i) Slip ratio:**

First compute advance velocity: 
$$V_A = V(1-w) = 6.173 \times 0.72 = 4.444 \; \text{m/s}$$

Advance per revolution: 
$$p_a = \frac{V_A}{n} = \frac{4.444}{2.167} = 2.051 \; \text{m/rev}$$

Slip ratio:
$$S_R = \frac{P - p_a}{P} = \frac{3.6 - 2.051}{3.6} = \frac{1.549}{3.6} = 0.430 \;(43.0\%)$$

This is **high** — indicates either an undersized propeller or excessive loading; a KTU examiner would comment that this design needs review.

**(ii) Thrust T:**

Thrust overcomes augmented resistance:
$$T = \frac{R_T}{1-t} = \frac{200{,}000}{1 - 0.14} = \frac{200{,}000}{0.86} = 232{,}558 \; \text{N}$$

**(iii) Torque Q:**

Using η_O definition rearranged:
$$Q = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n \cdot \eta_O} = \frac{232{,}558 \times 4.444}{2\pi \times 2.167 \times 0.62}$$

$$Q = \frac{1{,}033{,}328}{8.439} = 122{,}449 \; \text{N·m}$$

**(iv) Delivered power P_D:**

$$P_D = 2\pi n Q = 2\pi \times 2.167 \times 122{,}449 = 1{,}667{,}540 \; \text{W} \approx 1667 \; \text{kW}$$

**(v) Hull efficiency:**

$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{0.86}{0.72} = 1.194$$

**(vi) Propulsive coefficient:**

$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.62 \times 1.194 = 0.740 \;(74.0\%)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **S_R computation errors** — students forget that **p_a** is advance per revolution, NOT the advance velocity V_A. They are related by p_a = V_A / n.
> 2. **Unit confusion** — R_T given in kN must be converted to N (multiply by 1000) before substituting in formulas.
> 3. **Sign convention** — always use **absolute** magnitudes; thrust is positive in the forward direction.
> 4. **High S_R result** — students should comment on the design implication (typically < 25% is desirable).

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

### 🧠 Core Concepts (Definitional)
- ☐ A screw propeller converts **rotational mechanical power** into **axial thrust** via **helical blade action**.
- ☐ **Geometric pitch P** ≠ **Advance per revolution p_a**; the difference is **slip**.
- ☐ **Wake fraction w** reduces the speed of water entering the propeller disc.
- ☐ **Thrust deduction t** accounts for increased hull resistance due to propeller suction.
- ☐ The propeller operates in a **non-uniform wake field** of the hull, not open water.

### 🧮 Master Formulae (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $V_A = V(1-w)$
- ☐ $J = V_A / (nD)$
- ☐ $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$
- ☐ $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$
- ☐ $\eta_O = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$
- ☐ $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$
- ☐ $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_m$

### 📊 Design Parameter Ranges (Typical Merchant Ships)
- ☐ **P/D**: 0.6 (high-thrust) to 1.4 (high-speed)
- ☐ **BAR**: 0.30 (low solidity) to 1.05 (high solidity)
- ☐ **Z**: 3 (cargo) to 5+ (naval/cavitation-sensitive)
- ☐ **Hub ratio d_H/D**: 0.18 to 0.22
- ☐ **η_D**: 0.55 to 0.70 typical range

### ⚠️ Pitfalls to Avoid in the Exam Hall
- ☐ Convert **knots → m/s** (×0.5144) and **rpm → rps** (÷60).
- ☐ Always state **units** explicitly (N, N·m, kW).
- ☐ Verify **dimensional homogeneity** before final substitution.
- ☐ For B-Series, check that the chosen **J, P/D, BAR, Z** lie within the validated design envelope.
- ☐ Report **efficiencies as decimals** (0.62) OR **percentages** (62%), but be consistent.

> **End of Notes — Best of luck for your KTU Board Examination! ⚓📚**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Propeller Geometry, Sections and Construction Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screw Propeller Geometry, Sections and Construction Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Screw Propeller** is a marine propulsion device consisting of a central hub (boss) from which a finite number of **blades** radiate outward in a helical (screw-like) form. Each blade is essentially a twisted hydrofoil whose pitch and chord vary along its radius, designed to convert the rotational mechanical power delivered by the main engine into a hydrodynamic thrust force that propels the ship forward by accelerating a column of water astern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The geometry of a screw propeller is described by a set of standardized non-dimensional parameters (ratios) which uniquely identify a propeller. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to fluently draw the **Expanded Blade Planform**, identify all geometric axes, and define the Wagner, Taylor, and standard **B-Series** terminology.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a standard **wooden screw** used in carpentry. When you rotate the screw clockwise, it advances forward into the wood. The threads of the screw trace a **helix**. A marine propeller works on exactly the same principle — except instead of biting into wood, its "blades" (the threads) bite into water. The ship is pushed forward exactly as a screw is driven into a wooden plank.

If you peel off a small rectangular strip of the helix (one thread), the shape you obtain is the **helical or developed section**. If you then "un-twist" that strip so it lies flat, you get the **Expanded Blade Section** — the conceptual building block of all propeller geometry theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why geometry matters:** The propeller is the final link in the transmission of power from the engine to the water. A 1% loss in propeller efficiency translates to roughly a 1% increase in fuel cost across the ship's lifetime — a multi-crore economic impact. Geometry directly governs cavitation, vibration, efficiency, and structural strength.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

The following ratios are the "language" of propeller geometry and **must be memorized verbatim**:

- **Diameter, D** — Tip-to-tip blade diameter, in **metres (m)**.
- **Pitch, P** — Axial advance per revolution at the design condition, in **m**.
- **Pitch Ratio, P/D** — A non-dimensional number typically ranging from **0.5 to 1.4** for merchant ships.
- **Blade Area Ratio, BAR (or AE/A0)** — The ratio of expanded blade area to the propeller disc area, typically **0.35 to 0.85**.
- **Disc Area Ratio, DAR** — A function of blade number and BAR; controls cavitation inception.
- **Number of Blades, Z** — Almost always **3, 4, or 5** for merchant ships.
- **Hub Diameter, dH** — Diameter of the central boss, in **m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Screw Propeller

A screw propeller is composed of the following discrete sub-systems, each having its own geometric vocabulary:

### A. The Hub (Boss)
- A short cylindrical or frustum-shaped structure, often a **separate forging**, attached to the propeller shaft via a **tapered (conical) fit** and a **key**.
- The **Hub Ratio** is defined as:

$$
\text{Hub Ratio} = \frac{d_H}{D}
$$

- Typical value: **0.18 to 0.25** for merchant ships; smaller for fast vessels.

### B. The Blades
Attached to the hub periphery. Each blade is divided conceptually into:

| Region | Location | Geometry Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Root** | Adjacent to hub | Thickest chord, narrowest pitch angle (close to P/D local ≈ 1.0) |
| **Mid-radius** | At ≈ 0.7 R | Mean section; governs overall efficiency |
| **Tip** | At radius R = D/2 | Thinnest chord, largest pitch angle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the twist?** At the hub, the blade must move slowly (small circumferential speed) but produce thrust → it needs a coarse pitch (large P/D local). At the tip, the blade moves fast but produces relatively less thrust per unit area → a fine pitch (small P/D local). Hence, the pitch angle **decreases** from root to tip.

## 2.2 Pitch Geometry — The Helical Mathematics

If we "unroll" the cylindrical surface at any radius **r**, the blade section forms a triangle:

- **Horizontal base** = Circumference at that radius = $2\pi r$
- **Vertical height** = Pitch = P
- **Hypotenuse** = The blade's developed (expanded) face

The **Pitch Angle** $\theta$ at any radius r is therefore:

$$
\tan \theta = \frac{P}{2\pi r}
$$

At the **propeller tip** (r = R = D/2):

$$
\tan \theta_{tip} = \frac{P}{\pi D}
$$

## 2.3 The Six KTU-Required Geometric Ratios (Memorization Block)

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitch Ratio | $P/D$ | $P \div D$ | 0.5 – 1.4 |
| 2 | Hub Ratio | $d_H/D$ | $d_H \div D$ | 0.18 – 0.25 |
| 3 | Disc Area | $A_0$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | — |
| 4 | Expanded Area | $A_E$ | $\int_0^R c(r)\, dr$ | — |
| 5 | Blade Area Ratio (BAR) | $A_E/A_0$ | $4 A_E / (\pi D^2)$ | 0.35 – 0.85 |
| 6 | Thickness Ratio | $t/c$ | Local thickness $\div$ chord | 0.10 – 0.22 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disc Area $A_0$** is the swept circle of the propeller. **Projected Area $A_P$** is the silhouette of the blades as seen from ahead (without thickness). **Expanded Area $A_E$** is the sum of all blade outlines "flattened" onto a plane. Always: $A_E > A_P > A_0$ in terms of conceptual scope.

## 2.4 Rake and Skew — The 3D Offsets

These are subtle but high-frequency KTU questions.

### Rake (A-P offset of the blade)
- **Backward Rake:** The blade leans aft (away from the direction of advance) at the tip. Improves cavitation margins and allows a slightly larger disc diameter. Standard for modern merchant propellers.
- **Forward Rake:** Blade leans forward. Used historically; allows thinner hull clearances.

### Skew (Circumferential offset of the blade)
- The blade's mid-chord line is **not** a straight radial line from hub to tip but curves sideways (in the direction of rotation).
- **Linear Skew:** A straight slanted line.
- **Non-Linear / Progressive Skew:** A curved line (S-shaped). Reduces vibration by smoothing the rate of cavity collapse as the blade enters and leaves the wake.

## 2.5 Blade Sections (Aerofoil Profiles)

Each radial station of a blade is a 2D hydrofoil section. Three types of camber lines are used:

1. **Flat-Back Section:** Back is flat, face is circular arc. Easy to manufacture, used at the **root** where strength is critical.
2. **Ogival Section:** Symmetrical about the chord, with elliptical or circular-arc back. Used at mid-radius for efficiency.
3. **True Aerofoil Section:** NACA-derived camber (e.g., NACA 66, NACA 16). Used at the **tip** for cavitation resistance.

The thickness distribution is typically based on either the **Troost Series** (older) or the **Wageningen B-Series** (modern standard for KTU).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** When asked to "describe the construction of a propeller blade," the answer must include: (1) Cast/forged hub, (2) Inserted blade roots, (3) Bolted-on or integral blades, (4) Material selection based on strength vs. corrosion, and (5) Surface finish grade (e.g., A.S.M.E. polish standards).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Propeller Geometry |
|---|---|
| **Naval Architecture** | Hull–propeller interaction, wake adaptation, cavitation tunnel design |
| **Mechanical Design** | Stress analysis of blades (centrifugal + hydrostatic + hydrodynamic) |
| **Acoustics** | Tip vortex cavitation and singing mitigation (drives skew design) |
| **Manufacturing** | CNC 5-axis milling of expanded blade moulds; foundry pattern making |
| **Maintenance** | Underwater polishing, blade tip repair, cathodic protection design |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Expanded Area Ratio from First Principles

We begin with a thin annular ring at radius $r$ of width $dr$. The chord (face width) of the blade at this radius is $c(r)$. For **Z** identical blades, the expanded area contribution is:

$$
dA_E = Z \cdot c(r) \cdot dr
$$

**Step 1 — Set up the integral** for total expanded area:

$$
A_E = Z \int_{r = d_H/2}^{r = D/2} c(r)\, dr
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute disc area** $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$ and rearrange to form the **Blade Area Ratio (BAR)**:

$$
\frac{A_E}{A_0} = \frac{Z \int_{d_H/2}^{D/2} c(r)\, dr}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4Z}{\pi D^2} \int_{d_H/2}^{D/2} c(r)\, dr
$$

**Step 3 — Special case: constant chord.** If $c(r) = c_0$ (a flat-sided, untapered blade), the integral simplifies:

$$
A_E = Z c_0 \left( \frac{D - d_H}{2} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{A_E}{A_0} = \frac{4 Z c_0 (D - d_H)}{2 \pi D^2} = \frac{2 Z c_0 (D - d_H)}{\pi D^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Example numerical substitution.** Let $Z = 4$, $D = 5.0$ m, $d_H = 1.0$ m, $c_0 = 1.2$ m:

$$
A_E = 4 \times 1.2 \times \frac{(5.0 - 1.0)}{2} = 4 \times 1.2 \times 2.0 = 9.6 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi \times (5.0)^2}{4} = \frac{25\pi}{4} \approx 19.635 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\frac{A_E}{A_0} = \frac{9.6}{19.635} \approx 0.489
$$

Hence, **BAR ≈ 0.49**, which lies comfortably in the merchant-ship range.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pitch Angle as a Function of Radius

The geometric pitch is constant for a "true" screw, but the **local pitch angle** changes with radius. We begin with the helix:

**Step 1 — Unroll the cylinder** of radius r. The unrolled helix is a right-angled triangle with:

- **Adjacent (along circumference):** $2\pi r$
- **Opposite (axial advance):** P

**Step 2 — Apply the tangent ratio:**

$$
\tan \theta_r = \frac{P}{2\pi r}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of design P/D:**

$$
\tan \theta_r = \frac{P/D}{2(r/D)} = \frac{P/D}{2\bar{r}}
$$

where $\bar{r} = r/D$ is the **non-dimensional radius**.

**Step 4 — Evaluate at the tip** ($\bar{r} = 0.5$):

$$
\tan \theta_{tip} = \frac{P/D}{2 \times 0.5} = P/D
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at the 0.7R station** (a typical design point, $\bar{r} = 0.35$):

$$
\tan \theta_{0.7R} = \frac{P/D}{0.7} \approx 1.4286 \times (P/D)
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example.** For $P/D = 0.8$:

- $\theta_{tip} = \arctan(0.8) = 38.66°$
- $\theta_{0.7R} = \arctan(0.8 / 0.7) = \arctan(1.143) = 48.81°$
- $\theta_{0.4R} = \arctan(0.8 / 0.4) = \arctan(2.0) = 63.43°$

This confirms the qualitative rule: **pitch angle increases from tip → root**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Geometric Parameters

| KTU Exam Concept | Key Constraint | Equation to Apply | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cavitation avoidance | $p_{tip} \geq p_{vapor}$ | $\sigma_n \geq \sigma_{critical}$ | Increase BAR or lower P/D |
| Efficiency maximization | Minimum rotational losses | $\eta_P = \dfrac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q}$ | Match P/D to design advance vel. |
| Tip clearance | $C_{tip} \geq 0.12 D$ | Ducted vs. open | Avoid hull interaction |
| Structural integrity | $\sigma_{centrifugal} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | $F_c = \rho \, \omega^2 \int r\, dA$ | Governs minimum thickness at root |
| Wake adaptation | Skew + varying pitch | W_{ind} varies with $\theta$ | Reduces vibratory bearing forces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Series:** The **Wageningen B-Series** (Troost, 1968) provides systematic charts where $K_T, K_Q, \eta_0$ are tabulated as functions of $Z, P/D, A_E/A_0, J$. The full 4-parameter $(Z, P/D, EAR, J)$ table is the heart of KTU numerical questions.

## 3.4 Derivation: Disc Area and Projected Area Relationship

**Step 1 — Define Disc Area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Projected Area (Z blades, viewed from ahead):** For a blade of thickness $t$ and chord $c$, the projected width is the chord. For Z blades and a hub that blocks the central region:

$$
A_P = Z \int_{d_H/2}^{D/2} c(r) \cos \theta_r \, dr
$$

**Step 3 — For small pitch angles (high P/D)**, $\cos \theta_r \to 1$, so $A_P \to A_E / Z \times Z = A_E$ in the limit. In practice, $A_P$ is slightly less than $A_E$ because of the pitch-angle projection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $A_P = A_E$. This is **wrong** unless the pitch ratio is infinite (flat disc). Always use the cosine projection in formal answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
