## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define dimensional homogeneity. Why is it considered the foundation of dimensional analysis in ship model testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dimensional homogeneity is the principle that every term in a physically meaningful equation must have the **same fundamental dimensions**. For example, in the force balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= ma \rightarrow [M L T^{-2}] = [M] \cdot [L T^{-2}] \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

(½ mark for definition)

In ship model testing, dimensional homogeneity guarantees that the dimensionless π-groups (Froude, Reynolds, etc.) computed from the model are **numerically equal** to those of the full-scale ship when similitude is enforced. (1 mark)

This enables naval architects to **extrapolate** model-scale resistance data to full-scale ships with mathematical confidence, rather than empirical guesswork. (1 mark)

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State Buckingham's π-theorem. A ship model test involves 6 variables governed by 3 fundamental dimensions. How many dimensionless groups result?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):**
*"If a physical phenomenon involves **n** physical variables expressible in terms of **k** fundamental dimensions, then the relationship between the variables can be expressed in terms of **(n − k)** independent dimensionless groups called π-groups."*

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 6, \quad k = 3 \\
m &= n - k = 6 - 3 = 3 \text{ dimensionless groups}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Using **Rayleigh's method**, derive an expression for the **pressure drop Δp** in a fully developed turbulent flow through a smooth horizontal pipe of diameter **D**, given that Δp depends on the pipe diameter **D**, the mean flow velocity **V**, the fluid density **ρ**, and the dynamic viscosity **μ**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Buckingham's π-theorem**, obtain the relevant dimensionless groups governing the **drag force F_D** on a fully submerged marine hydrofoil, given that F_D depends on the chord length **c**, the velocity **V**, the fluid density **ρ**, the dynamic viscosity **μ**, and the pressure **p_∞** far from the body. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — List variables and write Rayleigh form (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta p = K \cdot D^{a} \cdot V^{b} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \mu^{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute dimensions (1 mark):**

$$
[M L^{-1} T^{-2}] = [L]^{a} \cdot [L T^{-1}]^{b} \cdot [M L^{-3}]^{c} \cdot [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^{d}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand RHS (1 mark):**

$$
[M L^{-1} T^{-2}] = [M]^{c+d} \cdot [L]^{a+b-3c-d} \cdot [T]^{-b-d}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate exponents (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{[M]:} \quad c + d &= 1 \\
\text{[T]:} \quad -b - d &= -2 \implies b + d = 2 \\
\text{[L]:} \quad a + b - 3c - d &= -1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve (1 mark):** Choosing c as free parameter → d = 1 − c; b = 1 + c; substituting → a = −1 − c

**Step 6 — Reconstruct (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= K \cdot D^{-1-c} \cdot V^{1+c} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \mu^{1-c} \\
&= K \cdot \frac{\mu V}{D} \cdot \left(\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}\right)^{c} \\
&= K \cdot \frac{\mu V}{D} \cdot R_e^{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the Darcy-Weisbach form where pressure drop is proportional to (μV/D) × Re^c. (Conclusion statement)

---

**Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions (1 mark):**

| Variable | Symbol | Dimension |
|---|---|---|
| Drag Force | F_D | [M L T⁻²] |
| Chord length | c | [L] |
| Velocity | V | [L T⁻¹] |
| Density | ρ | [M L⁻³] |
| Viscosity | μ | [M L⁻¹ T⁻¹] |
| Pressure | p_∞ | [M L⁻¹ T⁻²] |

n = 6, k = 3 → m = 6 − 3 = **3 π-groups**.

**Step 2 — Repeating variables (1 mark):** Choose ρ, V, c (covers M, L, T, none dimensionless alone).

**Step 3 — Form π-groups (3 marks):**

**π₁ with F_D:**

$$
\pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 c^2}
$$

**π₂ with μ:**

$$
\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho V c} = \frac{1}{R_e}
$$

**π₃ with p_∞:**

$$
\pi_3 = \frac{p_\infty}{\rho V^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Final correlation (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{F_D}{\rho V^2 c^2} = \phi\left(\frac{1}{R_e}, \frac{p_\infty}{\rho V^2}\right)
$$

The drag coefficient is a function of Reynolds number and a pressure coefficient — this is the standard lift-drag polar format used in hydrofoil design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **principle of geometric, kinematic, and dynamic similarity** between a ship model and its prototype. State the role of the Froude number in dynamic similarity for free-surface flows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1:25 scale model of a cargo ship is tested in a freshwater towing tank. The model is towed at **V_m = 2.5 m/s**. The measured total resistance is **R_m = 12 N**. Using Froude's law of comparison (neglecting Reynolds-scale effects), compute:
   (i) The corresponding full-scale ship speed **V_s**.
   (ii) The full-scale total resistance **R_s**, assuming both model and ship operate in seawater of similar density (ρ_s ≈ ρ_m). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Geometric Similarity (2 marks):** Every linear dimension of the model is a fixed ratio λ = L_m / L_s of the corresponding dimension of the prototype. All angles are preserved. The model is a **scaled replica** of the ship — including hull form, appendages, and propeller geometry.

**Kinematic Similarity (2 marks):** The ratio of velocities at corresponding points is the same throughout the flow field. Streamlines around the model are geometrically similar to those around the ship, and the velocity ratios V_m/V_s are constant at homologous points.

**Dynamic Similarity (2 marks):** The ratio of all forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational, surface tension) at corresponding points is the same. This is enforced by equating the relevant dimensionless numbers (π-groups) between model and prototype.

**Role of Froude Number (1 mark):** In free-surface flows dominated by gravity and inertia (wave-making), **Froude similarity** is the governing law:

$$
F_{n,m} = F_{n,s} \implies \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

This determines the model towing speed for a given ship speed.

---

**Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** λ = 1/25, V_m = 2.5 m/s, R_m = 12 N, ρ_m ≈ ρ_s.

**Part (i) — Full-scale speed (3 marks):**

Froude similarity requires:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g L_m}} = \frac{V_s}{\sqrt{g L_s}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= V_m \sqrt{\frac{L_s}{L_m}} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\lambda}} \\
&= 2.5 \times \sqrt{25} \\
&= 2.5 \times 5 \\
&= 12.5 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Full-scale resistance (4 marks):**

Froude's law of comparison for resistance (when ρ_m = ρ_s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_s}{R_m} &= \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_m} \cdot \left(\frac{V_s}{V_m}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{L_s}{L_m}\right)^2 \\
&= 1 \cdot (5)^2 \cdot (25)^2 \\
&= 25 \cdot 625 \\
&= 15625
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R_s = 15625 \times 12 = 1{,}87{,}500 \text{ N} = 187.5 \text{ kN}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the k = 3 vs. k = 4 distinction.** If a problem involves temperature (e.g., heat transfer in steam turbines on board), k = 4 and the number of π-groups changes. Always count dimensions carefully. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Choosing wrong repeating variables.** A common error is choosing variables that themselves form a dimensionless group — for instance, picking V, g, ρ where (V²/g) might accidentally collapse dimensions. Always verify the repeating set. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**
> 3. **Sign errors in exponent equations.** The Froude derivation's [L] equation often has sign slips. Re-derive and check each coefficient carefully. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing up model-to-ship vs. ship-to-model ratios.** Always write λ = L_m / L_s and ensure V_s = V_m / √λ is consistent with your assumption. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 5. **Ignoring Reynolds-scale friction correction.** Full-scale resistance = R_F (model) + R_R (residuary). Skipping the friction-line correction (ITTC 1978) costs full marks on real-world problems. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dimensional Analysis (Module 1):**

- ⭐ **Primary Dimensions:** Mass [M], Length [L], Time [T] (add Temperature [θ] for thermal problems).
- ⭐ **Rayleigh's Method:** Best for ≤ 4 variables; uses power-product assumption x = K·∏Qᵢ^aᵢ.
- ⭐ **Buckingham's π-Theorem:** General method; yields (n − k) dimensionless groups.
- ⭐ **Repeating Variables:** Must contain all k fundamental dimensions, must not be dimensionless, must not be the dependent variable.
- ⭐ **Froude Number Fn = V/√(gL):** Dominant in free-surface ship hydrodynamics.
- ⭐ **Reynolds Number Re = VL/ν:** Dominant in viscous-dominated flows (boundary layers, pipes).
- ⭐ **Weber Number We = ρV²L/σ:** Dominant in spray and cavitation problems.
- ⭐ **Euler Number Eu = Δp/(ρV²):** Pressure coefficient form for compressible/inviscid flow.
- ⭐ **Scale Ratio:** λ = L_m/L_s; Froude-scaled speed V_s = V_m/√λ; resistance scaling R_s/R_m = ρ_s/ρ_m · (V_s/V_m)² · (L_s/L_m)².
- ⭐ **ITTC 1978 Friction Line:** C_F = 0.075 / (log₁₀ R_e − 2)² — must be applied for full-scale resistance extrapolation.
- ⭐ **KTU Hot Tip:** Always state the **number of π-groups explicitly** before forming them; examiners award marks for that initial step.
- ⭐ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Mixing up **frictional resistance scaling** (Re-based) and **wave-making resistance scaling** (Fn-based). They are **different** and must be applied to the correct component of total resistance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction Between Hull and Propeller - Wake and Wake Fraction, Resistance Augmentation and Thrust Deduction, Propulsive Efficiency in Open Water and Behind Conditions, Hull Efficiency, Quasi Propulsive Coefficient, Transmission Efficiency
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interaction Between Hull and Propeller - Wake and Wake Fraction, Resistance Augmentation and Thrust Deduction, Propulsive Efficiency in Open Water and Behind Conditions, Hull Efficiency, Quasi Propulsive Coefficient, Transmission Efficiency

# Module 2 — Interaction Between Hull and Propeller

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fundamental Premise: Hull–Propeller Coupling

In ship hydrodynamics, a vessel and its propeller do **NOT** operate as two independent mechanical systems. The hull creates a non-uniform, low-pressure wake field at the propeller disc, and the propeller, in turn, alters the pressure distribution on the hull's afterbody. This two-way coupling is the essence of the **hull–propeller interaction problem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Students must clearly distinguish between **Open Water (OW)** conditions (propeller tested in free, undisturbed water — e.g., in a towing tank) and **Behind Condition (BC)** or **Behind Ship (BS)** conditions (propeller working in the actual wake of the moving ship). All efficiencies in Module 2 are defined by this distinction.

---

### 1.2 Wake and Wake Fraction (KTU High-Yield Concept)

**Formal Definition:**
The **wake** of a ship is defined as the flow field behind the hull caused by the frictional and pressure boundary layers, by the wave-making system, and by the displacement effect of the hull. The **wake velocity** $V_w$ is the difference between the ship's speed $V$ and the speed of the water actually entering the propeller disc $V_A$ (often called the speed of advance).

$$V_w = V - V_A$$

**Wake Fraction (Taylor's Wake Fraction):**
The non-dimensional form of wake is the **wake fraction**, denoted $w$:

$$w = \frac{V_w}{V} = \frac{V - V_A}{V}$$

**Components of Wake** (must be remembered for KTU ESE):

| Component | Symbol | Physical Origin | Approx. Share |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frictional Wake** | $w_F$ | Viscous boundary layer on hull | ~30–40% |
| **Wave Wake** | $w_W$ | Wave system generated by hull | ~10–20% |
| **Potential Wake** (Displacement) | $w_P$ | Steady potential flow thickening at stern | ~40–50% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Hand in a Swimming Pool":** Imagine pushing your open palm forward through still water. The water directly behind your palm is *not* still — it is dragged along with your hand. A small propeller placed in this "following water" sees a lower approach velocity than the ship's actual speed. This velocity deficit is the **wake**, and the fractional deficit is the **wake fraction**.

---

### 1.3 Resistance Augmentation

When the propeller is operating behind the ship, it sucks the hull forward (the **slipstream** accelerates the boundary layer) but also creates a low-pressure field on the stern that **increases** the hull's resistance. This additional resistance is called **resistance augmentation** (or **rotational augmentation**).

**Formal Definition:**
$$\Delta R = R_B - R_T$$

where $R_T$ = total resistance of the *bare hull* (towed, no propeller) and $R_B$ = resistance of the hull *with propeller working* (self-propulsion condition).

The augmented resistance is often expressed as a fraction:
$$\Delta C = \frac{\Delta R}{\frac{1}{2}\rho S V^2}$$

---

### 1.4 Thrust Deduction

Because of resistance augmentation, the propeller must deliver **more thrust** than the ship's tow resistance $R_T$ to keep the vessel moving at constant speed $V$.

**Formal Definition:**
**Thrust deduction** $T$ is the difference between the total thrust developed by the propeller $T_P$ and the ship's tow resistance $R_T$:

$$T = T_P - R_T$$

**Thrust Deduction Fraction:**
$$t = \frac{T}{T_P} = \frac{T_P - R_T}{T_P} = 1 - \frac{R_T}{T_P}$$

Typical values:
- Single-screw ships: $t \approx 0.12$ to $0.20$
- Twin-screw ships: $t \approx 0.05$ to $0.10$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Pedaling a Bicycle Uphill":** When you pedal a bicycle on flat ground, your leg power goes mostly into forward motion. But if a strong headwind blows (representing resistance augmentation), you must push harder (extra thrust) just to maintain speed. The "extra push" required beyond the basic resistance is the **thrust deduction**. The propeller's slipstream sucks the stern forward, partly compensating — but the net loss is what we call $t$.

---

### 1.5 Propulsive Efficiency — The Efficiency Cascade

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cascade (Memorize this for KTU 2024 ESE):**
> $$\eta_D = \eta_O \times \eta_H = \eta_O \times \eta_R \times \eta_T$$
> where the symbols are defined in Section 2 below.

The power delivered by the engine is transformed into useful tow power through a chain of efficiency losses. Understanding each link is the central theme of Module 2.

---

### 1.6 Hull Efficiency ($\eta_H$)

**Definition:**
Hull efficiency measures the effectiveness of converting the thrust produced at the propeller into useful work overcoming the ship's resistance, **accounting for the wake and thrust deduction phenomena**.

$$\eta_H = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T_P \cdot V_A} = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w}$$

It is also called the **Holtrop–Mennen hull efficiency** when expressed in this form.

---

### 1.7 Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient (QPC)

**Definition:**
The QPC accounts for the propeller's open-water efficiency and the hull efficiency, but **excludes** mechanical transmission losses (shaft, gearbox, bearings).

$$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \times \eta_H = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{P_B}$$

where $P_B$ is the **brake power** delivered to the propeller shaft (sometimes also called $P_D$, the delivered power).

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** QPC is the "ideal" propulsive efficiency of the system if transmission were perfect. It tells the naval architect how well the hull and propeller are matched.

---

### 1.8 Transmission Efficiency ($\eta_T$ or $\eta_S$)

**Definition:**
Transmission efficiency accounts for mechanical losses between the engine output flange (or thrust-bearing power take-off) and the propeller shaft input:

$$\eta_T = \frac{P_D}{P_B} = \frac{P_{\text{Delivered to Propeller}}}{P_{\text{Brake (Engine Output)}}}$$

These losses include:
- Gearbox meshing losses
- Shaft bearing friction
- Stern-tube bearing losses
- Seal friction
- Power-take-off losses (if applicable)

Typical values: $\eta_T \approx 0.97$ to $0.99$ (very high for modern ships).

---

### 1.9 Overall Propulsive Coefficient ($\eta_D$)

The product of all efficiencies in the chain:

$$\eta_D = \eta_O \times \eta_H \times \eta_T = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{P_B}$$

This is the figure-of-merit for the entire propulsion plant.

---

### 1.10 Relative Rotative Efficiency ($\eta_R$)

An alternative, empirically-based decomposition is sometimes used:

$$\eta_H = \eta_R \times \eta_T$$

but more commonly in modern KTU texts:
$$\eta_D = \eta_O \times \eta_R \times \eta_T$$
where $\eta_R$ = **relative rotative efficiency** ≈ ratio of behind-condition propeller efficiency to open-water propeller efficiency at the same thrust and advance coefficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wake — The Velocity Deficit Mechanism

The wake fraction $w$ is influenced by:
1. **Hull form** (fuller forms → higher wake).
2. **Block coefficient $C_B$** (larger $C_B$ → higher wake).
3. **Propeller location** (closer to hull → higher wake).
4. **Reynolds number** (viscous scaling effect).
5. **Speed/Froude number** (wave wake increases with $F_n$).

**Empirical Taylor Wake Formula** (for single-screw ships):
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05 \quad \text{(for fine sterns)}$$
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.10 \quad \text{(for full sterns)}$$

**Harvald's formula** (more modern):
$$w = 0.3095 C_B + 0.23 \frac{B}{\nabla^{1/3}} - 0.0001328 F_n^2 \cdot 10^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> These empirical relations are exam-favourites in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.2 Wake Components — Decomposition Logic

The total wake can be split as:
$$w = w_F + w_W + w_P$$

- **Frictional wake** $w_F$ is computed from boundary layer momentum thickness $\theta$ at the propeller plane:
$$w_F = \frac{2\theta}{D_p + h} \quad \text{(in disc-averaged sense)}$$

- **Wave wake** $w_W$ decays rapidly with depth but is non-zero at the propeller disc.

- **Potential wake** $w_P$ arises from the asymmetric pressure field — often the dominant component (~50%).

---

## 2.3 Resistance Augmentation — Detailed Mechanism

The propeller induces two counteracting effects on the hull:
1. **Slipstream effect (favourable):** Increases velocity over the afterbody → reduces pressure resistance. (Small effect, ~3–5% reduction).
2. **Suction effect (adverse):** The low pressure on the stern of the hull (caused by propeller suction) increases the *pressure resistance* of the hull. (Dominant, 5–15% increase).

**Net result:** Resistance augmentation $\Delta R$ is the difference, and it is always **positive** (resistance increases when propeller works).

Empirical correlation (from model tests):
$$\frac{\Delta R}{R_T} \approx (0.003 \text{ to } 0.012) \cdot \frac{C_B}{w^2}$$

---

## 2.4 Thrust Deduction — Why It Exists

The thrust deduction fraction $t$ is **not** a free parameter — it is a consequence of the suction force $F_s$ acting on the hull's afterbody due to the accelerated flow past the stern.

$$t = \frac{F_s}{T_P}$$

For practical naval architecture:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

This is the **single most important formula in Module 2** (KTU 2024 high-priority).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — KTU Module 2** (Use this for last-minute revision.)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range / Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wake velocity | $V_w$ | $V - V_A$ | m/s |
| 2 | Wake fraction | $w$ | $V_w / V$ | 0.20 – 0.40 (single screw) |
| 3 | Taylor's wake | $w$ | $0.5C_B - 0.05$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Speed of advance | $V_A$ | $V(1-w)$ | m/s |
| 5 | Resistance augmentation | $\Delta R$ | $R_B - R_T$ | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | Thrust deduction | $T$ | $T_P - R_T$ | Newtons (N) |
| 7 | Thrust deduction fraction | $t$ | $T / T_P$ | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| 8 | Hull efficiency | $\eta_H$ | $(1-t)/(1-w)$ | 0.60 – 0.85 |
| 9 | Open-water efficiency | $\eta_O$ | $T_P V_A / 2\pi n Q_O$ | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| 10 | Behind-condition efficiency | $\eta_B$ | $T_P V_A / 2\pi n Q_B$ | similar to $\eta_O$ |
| 11 | Relative rotative efficiency | $\eta_R$ | $\eta_B / \eta_O$ (at matched $J, K_T$) | 0.95 – 1.05 |
| 12 | Quasi-propulsive coefficient | $\eta_{QPC}$ | $\eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ | 0.40 – 0.65 |
| 13 | Transmission efficiency | $\eta_T$ | $P_D / P_B$ | 0.97 – 0.99 |
| 14 | Overall propulsive coefficient | $\eta_D$ | $R_T V / P_B$ | 0.50 – 0.70 |
| 15 | Propulsive efficiency (KTU text) | $\eta_P$ | $\eta_D = \eta_O \eta_H \eta_T$ | same as $\eta_D$ |
| 16 | Engine Power → Tow Power | $P_E \to P_{Tow}$ | $\eta_D = P_{Tow}/P_E$ | — |
| 17 | Effective Power | $P_E$ | $R_T \cdot V$ | Watts (W) |
| 18 | Delivered Power | $P_D$ | $2\pi n Q$ | Watts (W) |
| 19 | Brake Power | $P_B$ | $P_D / \eta_T$ | Watts (W) |
| 20 | Thrust Power | $P_T$ | $T_P \cdot V_A$ | Watts (W) |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical relationship for KTU 2024:**
> $$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_D \cdot \eta_T \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad \eta_D = \eta_O \eta_H \eta_T$$

---

## 2.6 Why These Efficiencies Matter in Real Engineering

1. **Hull efficiency** $\eta_H$ tells the designer how well the hull "feeds" water to the propeller. A poorly designed afterbody has high wake (which is good for propulsive efficiency numerically), but it usually comes with high thrust deduction (which is bad). The trade-off is delicate.
2. **Quasi-propulsive coefficient** is used to compare hull–propeller designs before committing to a full mechanical transmission system.
3. **Transmission efficiency** matters most in:
   - Geared steam turbines ($\eta_T \approx 0.98$).
   - Diesel-electric drives ($\eta_T \approx 0.92$ to $0.96$ — including generators and motors).
   - Direct-drive slow-speed diesels ($\eta_T \approx 0.99$).
4. **Overall propulsive coefficient** $\eta_D$ is the metric used in the **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** regulations by IMO.

---

## 2.7 The Two Schools of Decomposition

| School A (KTU Preferred) | School B (Some texts) |
|---|---|
| $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_T$ | $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_T$ |
| where $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ | where $\eta_R = \eta_B / \eta_O$ |

Both are mathematically equivalent when $\eta_R = \eta_H$ is forced, but physically they differ:
- School A: $\eta_H$ is **measured directly** from model self-propulsion tests.
- School B: $\eta_R$ separates the "rotative" effect of wake non-uniformity from the "wake-fraction" effect.

For KTU 2024, the **School A formulation** is the one most commonly tested.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hull Efficiency $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$

**Starting Point (Power Definitions):**

Step 1: **Effective Power (Tow Power)** — power required to tow the ship at speed $V$ against resistance $R_T$:
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V$$

Step 2: **Thrust Power** — useful power developed by the propeller at the disc:
$$P_T = T_P \cdot V_A$$

Step 3: **By definition**, hull efficiency is the ratio of effective power to thrust power:
$$\eta_H = \frac{P_E}{P_T} = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T_P \cdot V_A}$$

Step 4: **Substitute wake fraction definition** $V_A = V(1-w)$:
$$\eta_H = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T_P \cdot V(1-w)} = \frac{R_T}{T_P (1-w)}$$

Step 5: **Substitute thrust deduction fraction** $t = (T_P - R_T)/T_P$, so $R_T/T_P = 1-t$:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

This is the celebrated result. $\blacksquare$

**Explanatory Note:** Each step is justified by either definition (steps 1, 2, 3) or by direct substitution of the dimensionless ratios $w$ and $t$ (steps 4, 5). The cancellation of $V$ in step 4 is the key insight — it shows that $\eta_H$ is a *pure* ratio of forces, independent of ship speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient

**Starting Point:** The propulsive coefficient chain:
$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_T$$

**Open-Water Efficiency:**
By definition:
$$\eta_O = \frac{T_P \cdot V_A}{2\pi n \cdot Q_O} = \frac{P_T}{P_{D,OW}}$$

where $Q_O$ is the torque in open water at the same $T_P$ and $V_A$.

**Multiply:**
$$\eta_O \cdot \eta_H = \frac{T_P V_A}{2\pi n Q_O} \cdot \frac{R_T V}{T_P V_A} = \frac{R_T V}{2\pi n Q_O}$$

**The QPC is defined as** (using $P_B$ as brake power, which for *ideal* transmission equals the delivered power to the shaft):
$$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{P_B}$$

This is the propulsive efficiency **without** transmission losses. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Overall Propulsive Coefficient

Combining QPC with transmission:
$$\eta_D = \eta_{QPC} \cdot \eta_T = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{P_B} = \frac{P_E}{P_B}$$

This shows that $\eta_D$ is the ratio of useful tow power to brake power — the master efficiency of the entire ship. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Resistance Augmentation in Terms of $t$ and $w$

A subtle but exam-frequent derivation:

**Step 1:** The thrust required at the propeller equals tow resistance plus thrust deduction:
$$T_P = R_T + T = R_T(1 + \frac{T}{R_T})$$

**Step 2:** Define $\Delta R = R_B - R_T$ as resistance augmentation.

**Step 3:** The thrust deduction fraction is sometimes approximated as:
$$t \approx \frac{\Delta R}{T_P} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta_O} \quad \text{(rough empirical link)}$$

**Step 4:** Alternative form — the relation between bare-hull and self-propulsion resistance:
$$R_B = R_T + \Delta R$$
$$T_P = R_B \quad \text{(steady-state, ship moving at constant speed)}$$

So:
$$t = \frac{T_P - R_T}{T_P} = \frac{\Delta R}{T_P} = \frac{\Delta R}{R_T + \Delta R}$$

**Solving for $\Delta R$:**
$$\Delta R = \frac{t \cdot R_T}{1-t}$$

This gives the resistance augmentation directly in terms of the tow resistance and the thrust deduction fraction. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Pattern (KTU-Exam-Style)

**Worked Example 1: Hull Efficiency Calculation**

**Given:**
- Ship speed $V = 15$ knots = $7.716$ m/s
- Wake fraction $w = 0.30$
- Thrust deduction fraction $t = 0.15$
- Tow resistance $R_T = 250$ kN

**Find:** (a) Speed of advance, (b) Hull efficiency, (c) Effective power, (d) Thrust developed, (e) Thrust power.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Speed of advance:
$$V_A = V(1-w) = 7.716 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7.716 \times 0.70 = 5.4012 \text{ m/s}$$

Step (b) — Hull efficiency:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{1 - 0.15}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.85}{0.70} = 1.2143$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Hull efficiency *can exceed unity* (1.0) when $t < w$. This is **NOT** an error — it reflects the fact that the propeller works in slower water ($V_A < V$) and pushes against higher resistance ($T_P > R_T$). The "gain" from working in the wake more than compensates the "loss" from thrust deduction. However, $\eta_H > 1.05$ is rare in real ships.

Step (c) — Effective power:
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 250{,}000 \times 7.716 = 1{,}929{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 1929 \text{ kW}$$

Step (d) — Thrust developed:
$$T_P = \frac{R_T}{1-t} = \frac{250{,}000}{0.85} = 294{,}118 \text{ N} \approx 294.1 \text{ kN}$$

Step (e) — Thrust power:
$$P_T = T_P \cdot V_A = 294{,}118 \times 5.4012 = 1{,}588{,}500 \text{ W} \approx 1588.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification:** $P_E / P_T = 1929 / 1588.5 = 1.2143$ = $\eta_H$ ✓

---

**Worked Example 2: Full Power Chain**

**Given (continuing Example 1):**
- Open-water efficiency $\eta_O = 0.60$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_T = 0.98$

**Find:** (a) QPC, (b) Overall propulsive coefficient, (c) Delivered power, (d) Brake power.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Quasi-propulsive coefficient:
$$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.60 \times 1.2143 = 0.7286$$

Step (b) — Overall propulsive coefficient:
$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_T = 0.7286 \times 0.98 = 0.7140$$

Step (c) — Delivered power (shaft input to propeller):
$$P_D = \frac{P_T}{\eta_O} = \frac{1{,}588{,}500}{0.60} = 2{,}647{,}500 \text{ W} \approx 2647.5 \text{ kW}$$

Or equivalently:
$$P_D = \frac{P_E}{\eta_{QPC}} = \frac{1{,}929{,}000}{0.7286} = 2{,}647{,}500 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

Step (d) — Brake power:
$$P_B = \frac{P_D}{\eta_T} = \frac{2{,}647{,}500}{0.98} = 2{,}701{,}531 \text{ W} \approx 2701.5 \text{ kW}$$

Or equivalently:
$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{1{,}929{,}000}{0.7140} = 2{,}701{,}681 \text{ W} \approx 2701.7 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$

(Small rounding difference.)

---

**Worked Example 3: Finding Wake and Thrust Deduction from Resistance Augmentation**

**Given:**
- $R_T = 500$ kN
- $R_B = 540$ kN (bare hull + propeller working)
- Wake fraction $w = 0.25$

**Find:** (a) Resistance augmentation $\Delta R$, (b) Thrust deduction fraction $t$, (c) Hull efficiency.

**Solution:**

Step (a):
$$\Delta R = R_B - R_T = 540 - 500 = 40 \text{ kN}$$

Step (b):
Since steady state: $T_P = R_B = 540$ kN
$$t = \frac{T_P - R_T}{T_P} = \frac{540 - 500}{540} = \frac{40}{540} = 0.0741$$

Step (c):
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - 0.0741}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{0.9259}{0.75} = 1.2345$$

---

## 3.6 Relating Wake Fraction to Block Coefficient (Empirical Derivation)

**Taylor's formula** (1883, derived from extensive tank tests on single-screw merchant ships):
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$$

**Derivation logic:** Taylor correlated measured wake fraction with the longitudinal prismatic coefficient and block coefficient. For slender, fine-sterned ships, the wake is dominated by frictional effects. For full ships, displacement wake dominates. The coefficient 0.5 reflects the average slope, and 0.05 is the intercept correction.

**For full-stern ships** (Taylor's second approximation):
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.10$$

**Numerical:** If $C_B = 0.65$, then:
$$w = 0.5(0.65) - 0.05 = 0.325 - 0.05 = 0.275$$

This is a typical value for a single-screw bulk carrier.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hull–Propeller Interaction — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **power flow** through a ship propulsion system, identifying every efficiency node in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ENGINE FUEL ENERGY Qin] --> B[BRAKE POWER PB]
    B --> C[TRANSMISSION SYSTEM Gearbox Shaft Bearings]
    C --> D[DELIVERED POWER PD]
    D --> E[PROPELLER Open Water]
    E --> F[THRUST POWER PT equals TP times VA]
    F --> G[HULL INTERACTION Wake and Resistance Augmentation]
    G --> H[EFFECTIVE POWER PE equals RT times V]
    H --> I[USEFUL TOW WORK RT times V]

    B -.etaT.-> C
    C -.etaT.-> D
    D -.etaO.-> E
    E -.etaO.-> F
    F -.etaH.-> H
    F -.1.-> G
    H -.etaD.-> I
```

---

## 4.2 Wake Generation Mechanism — Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HULL MOVES AT V] --> B[Frictional Boundary Layer on Hull Surface]
    A --> C[Wave System at Hull Surface]
    A --> D[Displacement of Water Around Hull]

    B --> E[Velocity Deficit at Stern]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    E --> F[Total Wake Vw at Propeller Disc]
    F --> G[Wake Fraction w equals Vw over V]
    G --> H[Speed of Advance VA equals V times 1 minus w]

    H --> I[PROPELLER SEES SLOWER WATER]
    I --> J[Modified Thrust and Torque]
```

---

## 4.3 Resistance Augmentation — Two Competing Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROPELLER WORKING IN WAKE] --> B[Favourable Slipstream Effect]
    A --> C[Adverse Suction Effect]

    B --> B1[Increases flow velocity over afterbody]
    B1 --> B2[Reduces pressure resistance by 3 to 5 percent]

    C --> C1[Low pressure on stern pulls hull backward]
    C1 --> C2[Increases pressure resistance by 5 to 15 percent]

    B2 --> D[NET DELTA R equals RB minus RT]
    C2 --> D

    D --> E[Net effect is POSITIVE resistance increase]
    E --> F[Thrust Deduction Fraction t]
```

---

## 4.4 Efficiency Cascade — Module 2 Master Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER_INPUT
        A[PE Effective Power]
    end

    subgraph HULL_BLOCK
        B[etaH equals 1 minus t over 1 minus w]
    end

    subgraph PROPELLER_BLOCK
        C[etaO Open Water Efficiency]
    end

    subgraph TRANSMISSION_BLOCK
        D[etaT Mechanical Efficiency]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE_BLOCK
        E[PB Brake Power]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

    E --> F[Ship Moves]

    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style E fill:#cce5ff
    style F fill:#ccffcc
```

---

## 4.5 Relationship Between Efficiencies — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OVERALL PROPULSIVE COEFFICIENT etaD] --> B[QUASI PROPULSIVE etaQPC]
    A --> C[TRANSMISSION etaT]

    B --> D[OPEN WATER etaO]
    B --> E[HULL etaH]

    E --> F[WAKE FRACTION w]
    E --> G[THRUST DEDUCTION t]

    F --> F1[Taylor 0.5 CB minus 0.05]
    F --> F2[Harvald formula]
    F --> F3[Direct model test]

    G --> G1[Resistance Augmentation dR]
    G --> G2[T equals TP minus RT]
    G1 --> G2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define wake fraction. State Taylor's formula for wake fraction in terms of block coefficient and explain its physical significance.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **wake fraction** $w$ is defined as the ratio of the wake velocity $V_w$ (deficit between ship speed and the velocity of water at the propeller disc) to the ship speed $V$:
$$w = \frac{V_w}{V} = \frac{V - V_A}{V}$$

**Taylor's empirical formula** (for single-screw ships with fine sterns):
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$$

where $C_B$ is the block coefficient. For full-stern ships, the formula is $w = 0.5 C_B - 0.10$.

**Physical Significance:** A higher $C_B$ (fuller hull form) produces a thicker boundary layer and stronger displacement effect at the stern, leading to a higher wake fraction. This means the propeller operates in slower water, which raises the thrust power available per unit torque, generally improving propulsive efficiency — but excessive wake can cause propeller racing and vibration.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Distinguish clearly between resistance augmentation and thrust deduction. How are they related?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Resistance Augmentation | Thrust Deduction |
|---|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Increase in hull resistance due to propeller action | Difference between propeller thrust and ship resistance |
| **Symbol** | $\Delta R = R_B - R_T$ | $T = T_P - R_T$ |
| **Fraction** | $\Delta R / R_T$ | $t = T / T_P$ |
| **Physical cause** | Low pressure on hull afterbody due to propeller suction | Same suction force, viewed from propeller side |
| **Measurement** | From model self-propulsion test (with/without propeller) | Computed from $R_T$ and $T_P$ |

**Relationship:** Both phenomena have the **same physical origin** — the suction effect of the propeller on the hull's afterbody. The augmented resistance $\Delta R$ and the thrust deduction $T$ are connected by:
$$T = \Delta R$$
at steady-state self-propulsion (where $T_P = R_B = R_T + \Delta R$).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, with TWO sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** is provided — students answer EITHER the full Q-A OR the full Q-B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**Q-A:** (a) With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the concept of wake, wake fraction, and the three main components of wake. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A single-screw ship has the following data:**
- Ship speed: $V = 18$ knots
- Wake fraction: $w = 0.28$
- Thrust deduction fraction: $t = 0.16$
- Total resistance (bare hull): $R_T = 350$ kN
- Propeller open-water efficiency: $\eta_O = 0.58$
- Shaft efficiency: $\eta_S = 0.97$

**Calculate:**
1. Speed of advance $V_A$
2. Thrust developed $T_P$
3. Hull efficiency $\eta_H$
4. Effective power $P_E$
5. Quasi-propulsive coefficient $\eta_{QPC}$
6. Overall propulsive coefficient $\eta_D$
7. Brake power $P_B$ in kW

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A

##### Part (a) — Conceptual Answer

**Wake:** The wake is the non-uniform, low-velocity flow field that exists behind a ship's hull, arising from viscous, wave, and potential flow effects. The propeller, mounted in this wake, experiences a reduced approach velocity $V_A$ compared to the ship's speed $V$.

**Wake Fraction:**
$$w = \frac{V_w}{V} = \frac{V - V_A}{V}$$

[Sketch: a side view of a ship with streamlines showing the velocity deficit behind the stern and the propeller disc with $V_A < V$.]

**Three Components of Wake:**

1. **Frictional Wake ($w_F$):** Caused by the viscous boundary layer on the hull. The boundary layer momentum deficit is largest near the stern where the propeller is located. Typical contribution: 30–40% of total wake.

2. **Wave Wake ($w_W$):** Caused by the wave system generated by the hull. Wave-induced orbital velocities and the divergent wave system create additional velocity fluctuations. Typical contribution: 10–20%.

3. **Potential (Displacement) Wake ($w_P$):** Caused by the steady asymmetric flow around the hull — the displacement effect of the hull's volume. This is the dominant component for most ships (~40–50%).

**Total:**
$$w = w_F + w_W + w_P$$

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $V = 18$ knots $= 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.2592$ m/s
- $w = 0.28$, $t = 0.16$
- $R_T = 350$ kN $= 350{,}000$ N
- $\eta_O = 0.58$, $\eta_S = 0.97$

**Step 1 — Speed of advance:**
$$V_A = V(1-w) = 9.2592 \times (1 - 0.28) = 9.2592 \times 0.72 = 6.6666 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Thrust developed:**
$$T_P = \frac{R_T}{1-t} = \frac{350{,}000}{1 - 0.16} = \frac{350{,}000}{0.84} = 416{,}666.7 \text{ N} \approx 416.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Hull efficiency:**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{0.84}{0.72} = 1.1667$$

**Step 4 — Effective power:**
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 350{,}000 \times 9.2592 = 3{,}240{,}720 \text{ W} \approx 3240.72 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Quasi-propulsive coefficient:**
$$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.58 \times 1.1667 = 0.6767$$

**Step 6 — Overall propulsive coefficient:**
$$\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_S = 0.6767 \times 0.97 = 0.6564$$

**Step 7 — Brake power:**
$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{3{,}240{,}720}{0.6564} = 4{,}937{,}600 \text{ W} \approx 4937.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification (cross-check):**
$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_{QPC} \cdot \eta_S} = \frac{3{,}240{,}720}{0.6767 \times 0.97} = \frac{3{,}240{,}720}{0.6564} = 4{,}937{,}600 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q-B:** (a) Derive the expression for hull efficiency $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ starting from fundamental definitions. Explain why $\eta_H$ can be greater than 1.0. **(7 marks)**

**(b) For a twin-screw ship:**
- Ship speed: $V = 20$ knots
- Block coefficient: $C_B = 0.62$
- Resistance: $R_T = 800$ kN
- Open-water efficiency (each propeller): $\eta_O = 0.62$
- Shaft efficiency: $\eta_S = 0.98$
- Taylor's formula: $w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$
- Thrust deduction fraction (each shaft): $t = 0.08$

**Calculate:**
1. Wake fraction using Taylor's formula
2. Speed of advance
3. Thrust per propeller
4. Hull efficiency
5. Effective power
6. Quasi-propulsive coefficient
7. Brake power required (total for both shafts)

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B

##### Part (a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Effective (Tow) Power:** The useful work done per unit time in towing the ship:
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Thrust Power:** The work done by the propeller per unit time at the disc plane:
$$P_T = T_P \cdot V_A$$

**Step 3 — Define Hull Efficiency:**
$$\eta_H = \frac{P_E}{P_T} = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T_P \cdot V_A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_A = V(1-w)$:**
$$\eta_H = \frac{R_T \cdot V}{T_P \cdot V(1-w)} = \frac{R_T}{T_P(1-w)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $R_T / T_P = 1-t$:**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w}$$

**Why $\eta_H > 1$?** Hull efficiency is the ratio of effective power to thrust power, not a thermodynamic efficiency. Since $V_A < V$ (the propeller works in slower water), the thrust power can be less than the effective power even when $T_P > R_T$. The "gain" from operating in the wake (numerator in $\eta_H$ formulation) can exceed the "loss" from thrust deduction. The condition is:
$$\eta_H > 1 \iff t < w$$

This is common in single-screw ships where $w \approx 0.20$ to $0.40$ and $t \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $V = 20$ knots $= 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288$ m/s
- $C_B = 0.62$, $R_T = 800$ kN
- $\eta_O = 0.62$, $\eta_S = 0.98$
- $t = 0.08$ (each)

**Step 1 — Wake fraction (Taylor's formula):**
$$w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05 = 0.5(0.62) - 0.05 = 0.31 - 0.05 = 0.26$$

**Step 2 — Speed of advance:**
$$V_A = V(1-w) = 10.288 \times 0.74 = 7.6131 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Thrust per propeller:**
For a twin-screw ship, each propeller carries half the total resistance plus thrust deduction:
$$T_{P,\text{total}} = \frac{R_T}{1-t} = \frac{800{,}000}{0.92} = 869{,}565 \text{ N} \approx 869.57 \text{ kN}$$
$$T_{P,\text{each}} = \frac{T_{P,\text{total}}}{2} = 434.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Hull efficiency:**
$$\eta_H = \frac{1-t}{1-w} = \frac{0.92}{0.74} = 1.2432$$

**Step 5 — Effective power:**
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 800{,}000 \times 10.288 = 8{,}230{,}400 \text{ W} = 8230.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Quasi-propulsive coefficient:**
$$\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H = 0.62 \times 1.2432 = 0.7708$$

**Step 7 — Total brake power (both shafts):**
$$\eta_D = \eta_{QPC} \cdot \eta_S = 0.7708 \times 0.98 = 0.7554$$
$$P_B = \frac{P_E}{\eta_D} = \frac{8{,}230{,}400}{0.7554} = 10{,}895{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 10{,}895 \text{ kW}$$

**Per shaft:** $P_{B,\text{each}} \approx 5447.5$ kW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Knots → m/s requires multiplication by 0.5144. Marks lost if forgotten.
> 2. **Hull efficiency > 1.0:** Students often "correct" it to 1.0 thinking it is wrong. It is NOT — it is mathematically valid.
> 3. **Sign errors in $1-t$:** Thrust deduction is *subtracted* from unity. Writing $1+t$ is a 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing $\eta_S$ and $\eta_T$:** They are the same in KTU Module 2 — both denote transmission efficiency.
> 5. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 for twin-screw:** A common 3-mark error. The total resistance is shared between two propellers.
> 6. **Incorrect QPC formula:** Some students write $\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \eta_T$ — WRONG. It excludes transmission by definition.
> 7. **Mixing up $P_D$ and $P_B$:** $P_B$ is engine brake power (output), $P_D$ is delivered to propeller. They differ by $\eta_T$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Hull–Propeller Interaction)**

- **Wake Fraction** $w = V_w / V$ — the velocity deficit at the propeller disc relative to ship speed.
- **Components of Wake:** Frictional ($w_F$), Wave ($w_W$), Potential ($w_P$); $w = w_F + w_W + w_P$.
- **Taylor's Wake Formula:** $w = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$ (fine stern) or $w = 0.5 C_B - 0.10$ (full stern).
- **Speed of Advance:** $V_A = V(1-w)$.
- **Resistance Augmentation:** $\Delta R = R_B - R_T$ (positive; hull resistance rises with propeller working).
- **Thrust Deduction:** $T = T_P - R_T$ (propeller must produce more than bare-hull resistance).
- **Thrust Deduction Fraction:** $t = T / T_P = (T_P - R_T)/T_P$.
- **Hull Efficiency:** $\eta_H = (1-t)/(1-w)$ — *the* Module 2 formula.
- **$\eta_H$ can exceed 1.0** when $t < w$ — physically valid.
- **Quasi-Propulsive Coefficient:** $\eta_{QPC} = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H$ — *excludes* transmission losses.
- **Transmission Efficiency:** $\eta_T = P_D / P_B$ — typically 0.97 to 0.99.
- **Relative Rotative Efficiency:** $\eta_R = \eta_B / \eta_O$ — empirical, near unity (0.95–1.05).
- **Overall Propulsive Coefficient:** $\eta_D = \eta_O \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_T = P_E / P_B$.
- **Master Power Chain:** $P_B \xrightarrow{\eta_T} P_D \xrightarrow{\eta_O} P_T \xrightarrow{\eta_H} P_E$.
- **Effective Power** $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ — power needed to tow the ship.
- **Typical Values (Single-Screw Merchant Ship):** $w \approx 0.25$–$0.35$, $t \approx 0.12$–$0.18$, $\eta_H \approx 1.10$–$1.25$, $\eta_O \approx 0.55$–$0.65$, $\eta_D \approx 0.55$–$0.70$.
- **Twin-screw ships** have lower $t$ and lower $w$ than single-screw ships.
- **Wake and thrust deduction are not independent** — both originate from the propeller-induced suction on the hull's afterbody.
- **For KTU numericals:** Always convert knots to m/s; always state units; always cross-verify with $P_E / P_B = \eta_D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powering Cavitation - Types, Cavitation Number, Effects of Cavitation, Prevention of Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powering Cavitation - Types, Cavitation Number, Effects of Cavitation, Prevention of Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Cavitation in a Ship Propeller?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of vapor bubbles (cavities) in a liquid when the local static pressure at any point on the propeller blade surface falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

In a marine propeller, water flows over the blade surfaces. The pressure on the **suction (back) side** of the blade can drop substantially as the blade imparts momentum to the water. When this local pressure drops below the vapor pressure of seawater, the water "boils" locally, even at ambient temperature, creating cavities of vapor. As these cavities are swept by the rotating blade into regions of higher pressure, they collapse with tremendous force.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Bends" for Propellers

Imagine a scuba diver ascending too quickly — dissolved gas in their blood forms bubbles. Cavitation is the propeller's equivalent:
- The **diver (water)** experiences a **pressure drop (moving over a curved blade)**.
- **Bubbles (vapor cavities)** form.
- When the diver surfaces, the bubbles collapse — analogous to a cavity entering a high-pressure region behind the blade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in Ship Propulsion:** Cavitation limits the maximum power a propeller can absorb, erodes blade surfaces, generates noise and vibration, and reduces propulsive efficiency. It is a primary design constraint for high-speed vessels and naval submarines.

## 1.2 Why Cavitation Occurs in Propellers

A propeller works by creating a **pressure difference** between its suction and pressure sides:

$$\Delta p = p_{pressure\,side} - p_{suction\,side}$$

This pressure difference generates thrust. The **suction side** experiences a low-pressure region. If this low pressure drops below the saturation vapor pressure, water vaporizes locally, forming the **cavity**.

## 1.3 Conditions Required for Cavitation Onset

Cavitation occurs when three conditions are simultaneously met:
1. **Local pressure** $p_{local} < p_{vapor}$ (vapor pressure)
2. **Sufficient flow velocity** to create pressure differentials
3. **Presence of nucleation sites** (microscopic impurities, dissolved gas, surface roughness)

## 1.4 The Two Reference Pressures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atmospheric Pressure ($p_{atm}$):** $\mathbf{101.325\ kPa}$ (standard sea-level).
> **Vapor Pressure of Seawater ($p_v$):** typically $\mathbf{1.7\text{–}2.5\ kPa}$ at 20°C, depending on salinity and temperature.

**Density of Seawater ($\rho$):** $\mathbf{1025\ kg/m^3}$ (standard for naval architecture calculations).
**Kinematic Viscosity of Seawater ($\nu$):** approximately $\mathbf{1.05 \times 10^{-6}\ m^2/s}$.

---

## 1.5 Module Learning Outcomes Alignment (KTU 2024 CO Mapping)

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Topic Mapping |
|---|---|
| **CO2** – Apply the principles of ship powering to estimate resistance and propulsive coefficients | Define cavitation number and its threshold limits |
| **CO3** – Analyze the operating characteristics of marine propellers | Classify cavitation types and their formation mechanisms |
| **CO4** – Evaluate the effect of cavitation on propeller performance and hull efficiency | Justify mitigation strategies and predict cavitation onset |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cavitation Types in Propellers

> [!NOTE]
> Cavitation is classified based on the **location of bubble formation** and the **shape of the cavity** on the blade.

### 2.1.1 Sheet / Cloud Cavitation (Lamellar Cavitation)
- **Location:** Primarily on the **suction side** of the blade, near the leading edge.
- **Mechanism:** Forms a thin, transparent sheet that periodically sheds into a "cloud" of bubbles.
- **Visual:** Looks like a milky, irregular region covering 30–60% of the blade chord.
- **Cause:** High local velocities at off-design angles of attack; most common type in service.
- **Severity:** Moderate — can lead to erosion and efficiency loss if persistent.

### 2.1.2 Bubble (Travelling) Cavitation
- **Location:** Distributed along the blade, often from the **tip region inward**.
- **Mechanism:** Discrete bubbles form, travel with the flow, and collapse downstream.
- **Cause:** Insufficient blade area to absorb thrust — overload condition.
- **Severity:** **Most damaging** type; each bubble collapse creates micro-jets of up to 700 m/s, causing pitting erosion.

### 2.1.3 Vortex / Hub / Tip Vortex Cavitation
- **Location:** At the **blade tip** (tip vortex) and at the **hub** (hub vortex / boss cap vortex).
- **Mechanism:** Centrifugal force in the rotating tip vortex depresses the core pressure to below vapor pressure.
- **Cause:** Strong rotational flow at blade extremities.
- **Severity:** Significant for noise, but minimal erosion; highly visible in cavitation tunnel tests.

### 2.1.4 Leading Edge / Suction Side Cavitation
- **Location:** Just behind the leading edge on the suction face.
- **Mechanism:** Flow separation at the leading edge when angle of attack is too high; the separated region has low pressure.
- **Cause:** Overloaded blade section.

### 2.1.5 Trailing Edge / Pressure Side Cavitation
- **Location:** Near the trailing edge.
- **Mechanism:** Local pressure drop caused by accelerated flow between the trailing edge and adjacent blade.

### 2.1.6 Flashing / Surface Cavitation
- **Location:** Just below the free surface or at hull openings.
- **Mechanism:** When static pressure at depth is too low (e.g., deeply submerged propellers operating at high RPM in rough seas), the local water can flash into vapor.
- **Special Note:** Common in surface-piercing propellers of high-speed craft (planing hulls, SWATHs).

### 2.1.7 Supercavitation
- **Definition:** A stable, attached cavity that completely envelops the blade section.
- **Application:** Designed into **supercavitating propellers** for very high-speed vessels (submarines, torpedoes) where the cavity is intentionally stabilized to reduce drag.

### 2.1.8 Ventilation (Air-Drawing Cavitation)
- **Cause:** Air drawn down from the free surface into the propeller disc.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Cavity contains **air** rather than vapor; not strictly cavitation but behaves similarly.

## 2.2 The Cavitation Number — Thoma's Number

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation Number ($\sigma$)** is the single most important **dimensionless parameter** used to characterize the propensity for cavitation in marine propellers. Introduced by **Dietrich Thoma (1925)** at the German Ship Model Basin (VWS Berlin).

### 2.2.1 Thoma's Cavitation Number (Basic Form)

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,V_0^{2}}$$

Where:
- $p_0$ = reference (static) pressure at the propeller depth [Pa]
- $p_v$ = vapor pressure of the liquid [Pa]
- $\rho$ = density of the liquid [kg/m³]
- $V_0$ = reference velocity [m/s] (usually the ship advance velocity $V_A$)
- $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}$ = dynamic pressure

### 2.2.2 Effective Reference Pressure (Hydrostatic + Atmospheric)

The reference pressure $p_0$ at the propeller shaft depth $h_s$ below the waterline is:

$$p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho\,g\,h_s$$

Where:
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = $\mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
- $h_s$ = shaft immersion depth [m] (depth of propeller centre from waterline)

### 2.2.3 Modified Cavitation Number (Burrill-Roberts Number)

For more accurate correlation with model tests, the **inception cavitation number** uses the local velocity (blade tip speed component):

$$\sigma_n = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho\,V_{0}^{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho\,(2\pi n R)^{2}\,{\overline{C}_L^{2}}}$$

Where:
- $n$ = rotational speed [rev/s]
- $R$ = propeller radius [m]
- $\overline{C}_L$ = section lift coefficient

### 2.2.4 Critical Cavitation Number ($\sigma_c$)

The **critical cavitation number** is the minimum value below which cavitation will occur at a given blade section or for the whole propeller. It depends on:
- Blade geometry (section shape, thickness, camber)
- Local angle of attack
- Reynolds number
- Surface roughness

### 2.2.5 Cavitation Criterion

| Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\sigma > \sigma_c$ | **No cavitation** — local pressure stays above vapor pressure |
| $\sigma = \sigma_c$ | **Inception** — cavitation just begins |
| $\sigma < \sigma_c$ | **Cavitation exists** — extent depends on margin |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}}$ | **Thoma's Cavitation Number** | dimensionless |
| 2 | $p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h_s$ | **Reference pressure at depth** | Pa |
| 3 | $p_{vap,\,seawater,\,20°C} \approx 2.34$ kPa | **Vapor pressure (typical)** | kPa |
| 4 | $V_{tip} = 2\pi n R = \pi D n$ | **Blade tip speed** | m/s |
| 5 | $\sigma \propto \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{n^{2} D^{2}}$ | **Propeller loading parameter** | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^{2} D^{4}}$ | **Thrust coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^{2} D^{5}}$ | **Torque coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_A = V_S(1 - w)$ | **Advance velocity** | m/s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The cavitation number is the **central design tool** for naval architects. By plotting $\sigma$ against $K_T$ (or thrust loading coefficient $C_T = T/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2}\pi D^{2}/4)$), cavitation bucket diagrams are generated. The propeller designer ensures the operating point lies **above** the cavitation inception curve to avoid erosion and noise.

This is critical for:
- **Submarine propeller design:** avoiding tip vortex cavitation that betrays the boat acoustically
- **Warship propellers:** managing discrete-frequency tonal noise from regular cavitation patterns
- **High-speed container ships:** avoiding surface-piercing cavitation and ventilation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Thoma's Cavitation Number from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cavitation number represents the **ratio of the pressure available to suppress cavitation** to the **pressure drop required to produce the desired thrust**.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Bernoulli's Equation on the Suction Side

Apply Bernoulli along a streamline from far upstream (reference condition) to a point on the suction face of the blade:

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} = p_{suction} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{suction}^{2}$$

### Step 2: Rearrange to Find the Suction Side Pressure

$$p_{suction} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{suction}^{2}$$

The minimum suction-side pressure occurs where the local velocity is maximum. Let $V_{max}$ = local maximum velocity on the blade:

$$p_{min} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{max}^{2}$$

### Step 3: Identify the Cavitation Condition

Cavitation begins when $p_{min}$ falls to $p_v$:

$$p_v = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{max}^{2}$$

### Step 4: Define the Local Pressure Drop

The pressure drop from reference to the suction face:

$$p_\infty - p_v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{max}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}$$

### Step 5: Introduce the Thoma Number

To make this ratio non-dimensional and use the free-stream dynamic pressure as a reference:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_\infty - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_\infty^{2}} = \frac{V_{max}^{2} - V_\infty^{2}}{V_\infty^{2}}$$

**Final Thoma's Number Expression:**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma = \dfrac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}}\;}$$

This derivation shows that $\sigma$ measures **how much available pressure suppression we have** relative to the **dynamic pressure driving the flow**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU-style 14-mark computation problem. Solve it step-by-step as in an exam.**

### Problem Statement
A naval destroyer is moving at a service speed of **20 knots**. The propeller has a diameter **$D = 4.5$ m**, rotational speed **$n = 180$ rpm**, and the shaft centre is **$h_s = 6$ m** below the waterline. Given:
- Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- Vapor pressure $p_v = 2.4$ kPa
- Density of seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The Thoma cavitation number $\sigma$ at design conditions.
**(b)** Comment on whether cavitation will occur if the critical cavitation number is $\sigma_c = 0.55$.

### Step 1: Convert Ship Speed to SI Units

The conversion factor for knots to m/s: 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s

$$V_0 = 20 \times 0.5144 = 10.288 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Reference Pressure at Shaft Depth

The reference pressure combines atmospheric and hydrostatic components:

$$p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h_s$$

Substituting the values:

$$p_0 = 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(6)$$

Computing the hydrostatic term:

$$\rho g h_s = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6 = 60{,}331.5 \text{ Pa} = 60.3315 \text{ kPa}$$

Therefore:

$$p_0 = 101.325 + 60.332 = 161.657 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Net Available Pressure

The numerator of the cavitation number is the **net pressure available to suppress cavitation**:

$$p_0 - p_v = 161.657 - 2.4 = 159.257 \text{ kPa} = 159{,}257 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Dynamic Pressure (Denominator)

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (10.288)^{2}$$

Computing $V_0^{2}$:

$$V_0^{2} = 105.843 \text{ (m/s)}^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 105.843 = 54{,}244.5 \text{ Pa} = 54.245 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Thoma Cavitation Number

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}} = \frac{159.257}{54.245}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma \approx 2.936\;}$$

### Step 6: Compare with Critical Cavitation Number

Given: $\sigma_c = 0.55$

Comparison:

$$\sigma = 2.936 \quad \gg \quad \sigma_c = 0.55$$

Since $\sigma \gg \sigma_c$, the available pressure suppression far exceeds the critical threshold.

**Conclusion: NO cavitation is expected at the design point** — the propeller has a **large safety margin** of $2.936 - 0.55 = 2.386$, indicating very conservative operating conditions.

## 3.3 Alternative Derivation: Cavitation Number in Terms of RPM and Diameter

> [!NOTE]
> For a propeller in the open water condition (Burrill diagram), $V_0$ is replaced by a velocity component of the blade section.

### Step 1: Velocity at Blade Section Radius $r$

The velocity of a blade element at radius $r$ has two components:
- **Axial:** $V_A$ (advance velocity, ship speed $\times$ (1−wake))
- **Tangential:** $2\pi n r$ (rotational)

### Step 2: Resultant Velocity Squared

$$V_{resultant}^{2} = V_A^{2} + (2\pi n r)^{2}$$

### Step 3: Substituting into the Cavitation Number

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho (2\pi n r)^{2}}$$

This **advanced form** is used in cavitation bucket calculations at specific radial stations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on $\sigma$

| Parameter | Change | Effect on $\sigma$ | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shaft depth $h_s$** | Increase | $\sigma$ increases (linear) | More hydrostatic pressure suppresses cavitation |
| **Ship speed $V_0$** | Increase | $\sigma$ decreases (inverse square) | Higher dynamic pressure drives cavitation |
| **RPM ($n$)** | Increase | $\sigma$ decreases (denominator grows) | Faster blades generate lower suction pressure |
| **Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm}$** | Decrease (altitude) | $\sigma$ decreases | Less ambient pressure available |
| **Vapor pressure $p_v$** | Increase (warm water) | $\sigma$ decreases | Easier to vaporize water |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cavitation Formation Process — Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Water flows over rotating blade] --> A1[Pressure on suction side drops]
    A1 --> A2{Local pressure < vapor pressure?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[No cavitation - normal flow]
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Water vaporizes - cavity forms]
    A4 --> A5[Cavity swept by rotation into high pressure region]
    A5 --> A6[Violent bubble collapse - microjets]
    A6 --> A7[Effects: Erosion, Noise, Vibration, Thrust loss]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Cavitation Types — Hierarchical Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Marine Propeller Cavitation] --> B1[Sheet or Cloud Cavitation]
    B0 --> B2[Bubble Cavitation]
    B0 --> B3[Vortex Cavitation]
    B0 --> B4[Leading Edge Cavitation]
    B0 --> B5[Trailing Edge Cavitation]
    B0 --> B6[Supercavitation]
    B0 --> B7[Flashing and Ventilation]

    B3 --> B3a[Tip Vortex Cavitation]
    B3 --> B3b[Hub Vortex Cavitation]
    B3 --> B3c[Root Vortex Cavitation]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cavitation Effects and Mitigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Marine Propeller System] --> C1[Operating Condition]
    C1 --> C2[Cavitation Number sigma]
    C2 --> C3{Sigma versus Critical Sigma}

    C3 -- sigma greater than sigma critical --> C4[Safe Operating Region]
    C4 --> C5[Thrust delivered normally]

    C3 -- sigma less than sigma critical --> C6[Cavitation Region]
    C6 --> C7a[Blade Erosion]
    C6 --> C7b[Vibration and Noise]
    C6 --> C7c[Efficiency Loss]
    C6 --> C7d[Pressure Pulse Hull Excitation]

    C7a --> D1[Prevention Strategy 1 - Increase Immersion]
    C7b --> D2[Prevention Strategy 2 - Reduce RPM]
    C7c --> D3[Prevention Strategy 3 - Blade Section Redesign]
    C7d --> D4[Prevention Strategy 4 - Tip Vortex Control]

    D1 --> E0[Restored Safe Operation]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0
```

## 4.4 Cavitation Bucket Schematic — Design Diagram Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Cavitation Bucket Diagram Concept] --> F1[X-axis: Thrust Coefficient KT]
    F0 --> F2[Y-axis: Cavitation Number sigma]
    F2 --> F3[Upper Region - No Cavitation]
    F2 --> F4[Cavitation Inception Curve]
    F4 --> F5[Lower Region - Cavitation Exists]
    F5 --> F6[Tip Vortex Cavitation Zone]
    F5 --> F7[Sheet Cavitation Zone]
    F5 --> F8[Bubble Cavitation Zone]
    F5 --> F9[Supercavitation Zone]
    F3 --> F10[Operating Point must lie HERE]
```

## 4.5 Pressure Distribution Schematic on a Blade Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G0[Blade Cross Section] --> G1[Leading Edge]
    G0 --> G2[Suction Side - low pressure]
    G0 --> G3[Pressure Side - high pressure]
    G0 --> G4[Trailing Edge]
    G2 --> G5[Minimum pressure point P-min]
    G5 --> G6{Is P-min greater than P-vapour}
    G6 -- Yes --> G7[No cavitation]
    G6 -- No --> G8[Bubble forms and grows]
```
