# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part A questions are compulsory and test direct recall / short derivations. **Each answer is expected to be 2–4 sentences / one short derivation.**

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **cavitation number** in marine propellers. State its physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

The **cavitation number** is a dimensionless parameter defined by Thoma as:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}}$$

It represents the **ratio of the available net pressure (above vapor pressure) at the propeller depth to the dynamic pressure** of the incoming flow. **Physical significance:** A higher $\sigma$ means more pressure is available to suppress cavitation; cavitation occurs when $\sigma$ falls below a critical value $\sigma_c$ that depends on blade geometry.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List **any four types of cavitation** observed in marine propellers.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sheet (or cloud) cavitation** – thin cavity sheet on the suction side near the leading edge.
2. **Bubble cavitation** – discrete travelling bubbles that collapse and erode blades.
3. **Tip vortex cavitation** – core of the tip vortex depresses to vapor pressure.
4. **Flashing cavitation** – near the free surface when local pressure drops below vapor pressure.

*(Alternative valid types: hub vortex, leading-edge, trailing-edge, supercavitation, ventilation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided as **Part (a) – 7 marks** and **Part (b) – 7 marks**. There is internal choice; answer either full Module Choice (entire Q) or sub-choice where offered.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Adapted)]**

A merchant vessel of **length $L_{PP} = 170$ m** has a propeller with diameter **$D = 5.2$ m** rotating at **$n = 130$ rpm**. The service speed is **18 knots** and the shaft centre is at **$h_s = 7.5$ m** below the waterline.

Given:
- $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- $p_v = 2.34$ kPa
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Critical cavitation number $\sigma_c = 0.45$

**(a)** Calculate the Thoma cavitation number $\sigma$ for this propeller at design speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine if cavitation will occur. If yes, suggest **two practical design modifications** to suppress it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Convert ship speed to SI units.

$$V_0 = 18 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 9.259 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the reference pressure at shaft depth.

$$p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h_s = 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(7.5)$$

$$\rho g h_s = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 7.5 = 75{,}414.4 \text{ Pa} = 75.414 \text{ kPa}$$

$$p_0 = 101.325 + 75.414 = 176.739 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Net pressure available.

$$p_0 - p_v = 176.739 - 2.34 = 174.399 \text{ kPa} = 174{,}399 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Dynamic pressure.

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (9.259)^{2}$$

$$V_0^{2} = 85.74 \text{ (m/s)}^{2}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2} = 512.5 \times 85.74 = 43{,}942 \text{ Pa} = 43.942 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5:** Thoma cavitation number.

$$\sigma = \frac{174.399}{43.942}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma \approx 3.969\;}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparison with critical value:**

$$\sigma = 3.969 \quad \text{vs.} \quad \sigma_c = 0.45$$

Since $\sigma \gg \sigma_c$ (margin $\approx 3.5$), **NO cavitation** will occur at the design point under ideal conditions.

**However**, if the vessel is light-loaded (smaller $h_s$) or operating in warm tropical water (higher $p_v$), $\sigma$ can drop. **Two practical modifications to prevent cavitation:**

1. **Increase shaft immersion depth** $h_s$ — directly increases $p_0$, raising $\sigma$ linearly. E.g., lowering the propeller 1 m below current depth adds $1025 \times 9.81 \times 1 \approx 10$ kPa to $p_0$.

2. **Reduce blade tip loading by redesigning the blade section** — increase blade area (higher expanded area ratio $A_E/A_0$), use thinner sections with controlled camber, or reduce RPM (lower $n$) to decrease dynamic loading.

*(Other valid modifications: use anti-cavitation polished coatings, fit a Kort nozzle at low speeds, apply tip-end plates to suppress tip vortex.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch (described in words), explain the **formation mechanism of bubble cavitation** in a marine propeller. Describe **three major effects** of cavitation on propeller performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submarine's propeller operates at **$n = 250$ rpm**, $D = 4.0$ m, at a depth of **$h_s = 8$ m** with the vessel moving at **$V_0 = 12$ knots**. Using the data below, calculate the cavitation number and the tip speed:

- $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa, $p_v = 2.0$ kPa, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Comment on whether tip vortex cavitation is likely. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Bubble Cavitation Formation Mechanism:**

A propeller blade rotating through water creates a low-pressure region on its suction side. When this local pressure falls below the **vapor pressure of water**, dissolved gas and vapor form discrete bubbles attached to or trailing the blade surface. As the blade rotates, these bubbles are carried into the **higher-pressure wake region** behind the propeller, where they collapse violently. Each collapse generates a microjet of water striking the blade surface at velocities up to 700 m/s — producing **cavitation pitting and erosion**.

**Three Major Effects of Cavitation:**

1. **Blade erosion / pitting:** Repeated bubble collapse removes material from blade surfaces, altering profile and reducing efficiency over time.

2. **Noise and vibration:** Bubble collapse produces high-frequency pressure pulses, exciting hull structures and radiating acoustic energy — a major concern for submarine stealth (RCS reduction).

3. **Loss of propulsive efficiency:** Cavity regions alter the effective blade camber and lift, reducing thrust and increasing torque. The propeller may also experience **rotating pressure field excitation** of the hull.

*(Additional: thrust breakdown, ventilation in surface ships, supercavitation design shifts.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Convert speed.

$$V_0 = 12 \times 0.5144 = 6.173 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Reference pressure at depth.

$$p_0 = 101{,}325 + 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 = 101{,}325 + 80{,}442 = 181{,}767 \text{ Pa} = 181.767 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3:** Net pressure.

$$p_0 - p_v = 181.767 - 2.0 = 179.767 \text{ kPa} = 179{,}767 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Dynamic pressure.

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (6.173)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 38.107 = 19{,}530 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5:** Cavitation number.

$$\sigma = \frac{179{,}767}{19{,}530} \approx 9.205$$

**Step 6:** Tip speed.

$$V_{tip} = \pi D n = \pi \times 4.0 \times \frac{250}{60} = \pi \times 4.0 \times 4.167$$

$$V_{tip} = 3.1416 \times 16.667 = 52.36 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Comment on tip vortex cavitation.

The very high $\sigma \approx 9.2$ indicates the propeller is well-suppressed for **general** cavitation. However, for **tip vortex** cavitation, the local pressure at the vortex core can drop substantially due to the centrifugal depression in the tip vortex:

$$\Delta p_{vortex} = \rho \Gamma^{2} / (8\pi^{2} r_c^{2})$$

where $r_c$ is the vortex core radius (often 5–10% of blade chord). For a tip speed of **52.36 m/s**, the centripetal acceleration is high, and the vortex core pressure is likely to drop below $p_v$. **Tip vortex cavitation is therefore likely to occur** even though the bulk cavitation number is large.

This is a key reason why submarines are highly sensitive to **acoustic signature from tip vortex cavitation**, and special blade tip shapes (swept tips, end plates) are used to suppress it.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert knots → m/s. **Always multiply knots by 0.5144.** A 20% error here propagates through the entire problem.
>
> 2. **Pressure Units Mix-up:** Mixing kPa and Pa in numerator and denominator. **Convert both to the same units** (Pa recommended) before computing $\sigma$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Hydrostatic Term:** Many students write $p_0 = p_{atm}$ only, omitting $\rho g h_s$. The full reference pressure is $p_0 = p_{atm} + \rho g h_s$. For deep shafts (e.g., submarines, large tankers), $\rho g h_s$ can be 50% of $p_{atm}$.
>
> 4. **Confusing Cavitation Number with Critical Number:** Always state both $\sigma$ and $\sigma_c$ explicitly and compare. A naked value of $\sigma = 3.9$ with no comparison is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Wrong Density:** Using $\rho_{fresh\,water} = 1000$ kg/m³ instead of $\rho_{seawater} = 1025$ kg/m³ in naval architecture problems. The error is small but cumulatively significant.
>
> 6. **Insufficient Sketches:** Even described diagrams (especially for cavitation formation) earn full marks. Always describe the mechanism in 4–5 sentences minimum.
>
> 7. **Rounding Early:** Keep at least 4 significant figures until the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Points (Module 2 – Cavitation):**

- **Cavitation** = formation of vapor bubbles when local pressure < vapor pressure. Occurs primarily on the **suction side** of propeller blades.

- **Thoma's cavitation number** is the single most important parameter:

$$\sigma = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}} = \frac{(p_{atm} + \rho g h_s) - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_0^{2}}$$

- **Cavitation Criterion:** cavitation occurs when $\sigma < \sigma_c$. **Higher $\sigma$ = safer.**

- **Six major types of cavitation:** Sheet/Cloud, Bubble, Tip Vortex, Hub Vortex, Leading/Trailing Edge, Flashing, Supercavitation, Ventilation.

- **Bubble cavitation is the most damaging** (erosive). **Tip vortex cavitation is the most acoustically significant** (relevant for submarines and stealth).

- **Four primary prevention strategies:**
  1. **Increase immersion depth $h_s$** — raises $p_0$.
  2. **Reduce RPM $n$** — reduces dynamic pressure and tip speed.
  3. **Increase blade area ratio $A_E/A_0$** — distributes loading across larger area, lowering section lift.
  4. **Optimize tip geometry** (swept tips, end plates) — suppresses tip vortex cavitation.

- **Key standard values to memorize:**
  - $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
  - $p_{v,\,seawater,\,20°C} \approx 2.3$ kPa
  - $\rho_{seawater} = 1025$ kg/m³
  - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
  - 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s
  - $V_{tip} = \pi D n$ (where $n$ in rev/s)

- **Interpretation rule of thumb:** $\sigma > 2$ → safe; $1 < \sigma < 2$ → likely sheet cavitation; $\sigma < 1$ → heavy bubble/sheet cavitation likely.

- **Design philosophy:** Always plot operating points on a **cavitation bucket diagram** (Burrill or similar) to verify safe operation over the full envelope of ship speeds and loading conditions.

- **Module 2 – Cavitation topic is a direct 14-mark computation + 7-mark theory pairing in KTU ESE — practice numerical problems until the steps are automatic.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Propellers - Propeller Families and Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design of Propellers - Propeller Families and Series # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Propeller Family and Propeller Series? In ship hydrodynamics, the design of a marine propeller cannot be approached from scratch for every vessel because the open-water performance characteristics of every possible blade shape would be impossible to chart individually. To solve this, systematic experimental testing programs were conducted at leading international model basins in which hundreds of propeller models were towed in open water, and their non-dimensional thrust ($K_T$), torque ($K_Q$), and efficiency ($\eta_0$) coefficients were plotted against the advance coefficient ($J$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > A **Propeller Series** is a complete set of geometrically similar propellers that differ only in their non-dimensional geometric parameters (pitch ratio $P/D$, expanded area ratio $EAR$, and blade number $Z$), all sharing the same blade outline, thickness distribution, and section profile (e.g., circular-arc or elliptical). > > A **Propeller Family** is a broader category that groups together propellers of different blade sections and thickness distributions but covering the same broad design space, allowing the naval architect to compare the hydrodynamic efficiency of different blade shapes against a common performance baseline. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Shoe Size System":** > Imagine buying shoes. You do not custom-make a shoe for every foot. Instead, the industry developed a standardized grid: > - **Length** = Pitch ratio ($P/D$) > - **Width** = Expanded Area Ratio ($EAR$) > - **Number of straps (pairs)** = Blade number ($Z$) > - **Material/Style** = Blade section family (NACA, Wageningen B, Kaplan, etc.) > > A **propeller series** is like one specific manufacturer's shoe catalog (e.g., Nike-Air series) — every shoe in that catalog has the same sole shape and construction. A **propeller family** is the broader concept of "sports shoes" combining multiple manufacturers. When you design a propeller, you essentially "select a size" from the right catalog! ## 1.3 The Three Governing Non-Dimensional Geometric Parameters The shape of any marine propeller is uniquely described (within a given family) by exactly **three numbers**: | Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | Pitch Ratio | $P/D$ | Ratio of propeller pitch to diameter | 0.5 – 1.4 | | Expanded Area Ratio | $EAR$ or $A_E/A_0$ | Ratio of blade expanded (developed) area to disc area | 0.3 – 0.85 | | Blade Number | $Z$ | Number of blades | 3 – 7 | ## 1.4 Why Are Propeller Series Necessary? The primary purpose of these standardized series is to enable **systematic propeller design via interpolation** of pre-measured open-water test data. Instead of running expensive and time-consuming open-water tests for every new design, the naval architect: 1. Identifies the required $P/D$, $EAR$, and $Z$ from ship powering requirements. 2. Looks up the closest matching propeller in an established series (e.g., Wageningen B-Series). 3. Reads $K_T$, $K_Q$, and $\eta_0$ directly from published charts at the design $J$ value. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Famous Propeller Notation System (Reading "B4-55") The international standard for describing a propeller series has a precise hierarchy. Using the **Wageningen B-Series** as the most common example, the notation reads as: $$ \text{Series} \; \text{Blade Number} - \text{Pitch Ratio in percent} $$ ### Worked Example: B4-55 - **B** → Wageningen **B**-Series (circular-arc blade sections with $0.18\,R$ thickness) - **4** → Four blades ($Z = 4$) - **55** → Pitch ratio $P/D = 0.55$ > [!TIP] > **Quick-Decode Rule:** The number after the hyphen is **always pitch ratio × 100**, NOT the pitch itself! ## 2.2 Hierarchy of Propeller Series and Families Propeller families are organized in a clear hierarchy, from broad (families) to specific (individual series): ### 2.2.1 Major Historical Propeller Families **1. Wageningen B-Series (Dutch, most widely used)** - Developed at the Netherlands Ship Model Basin (NSMB), Wageningen (1969). - Uses a **circular-arc** blade section with a thickness distribution of $t = 0.18\,R$ (where $R$ is the propeller radius) at the reference radius $r = 0.6\,R$. - Open-water data covers: $Z = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$; $P/D = 0.5$ to $1.4$; $EAR = 0.3$ to $0.8$. - Published by Troost in 1969; today considered the **international standard**. **2. Wageningen Ka-Series (Modified)** - Modified version of the B-Series with improved blade-section shape (NACA 66 thickness form with $a = 0.8$ camber). - Provides better efficiency at high loadings compared to the original B-Series. **3. K-Series (Japanese)** - Developed at the Kyushu University (Japan) by Yoshitake and Masuyama. - Uses a **NACA 66 + a = 0.8** section with a modified meanline. - Covers $Z = 3, 4, 5$; $P/D = 0.6$ to $1.6$; $EAR = 0.5$ to $0.9$. - Well-suited for high-speed vessels and torpedo boats. **4. Kaplan-Screw (Ka-Series, German)** - Developed by H. Kaplan at Vienna Model Basin. - Uses a flat-sided, thick-section blade form. - Excellent cavitation performance at moderate $P/D$. **5. Gawn Series (British)** - Developed at the Admiralty Experiment Works (AEW), Haslar (1944–1950). - Uses flat-sided blades with elliptical outline. - Famous for **cavitation bucket tests**, separating cavitation and non-cavitation performance. **6. Newton Series (Early American)** - One of the earliest systematic propeller series, developed by E. P. Newton (1909). - Used circular-arc blade sections but with limited parameter coverage. - Primarily of historical importance; rarely used in modern ship design. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | Advance Coefficient | $J = \dfrac{V_A}{nD}$ | Ratio of ship advance speed to tip speed | dimensionless | | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = \dfrac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4}$ | Non-dimensional thrust | dimensionless | | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = \dfrac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}$ | Non-dimensional torque | dimensionless | | Open-water Efficiency | $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi}\dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$ | Propeller efficiency in open water | dimensionless | | Pitch Ratio | $P/D$ | Geometric pitch divided by diameter | dimensionless | | Expanded Area Ratio | $EAR = \dfrac{A_E}{A_0}$ | Blade developed area / disc area | dimensionless | | Disc Area | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Swept area of propeller | $m^2$ | | Propulsive Efficiency | $\eta_D = \dfrac{\eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S}{1 - t}$ | Overall propulsive coefficient | dimensionless | | Reynolds Number (propeller) | $Re = \dfrac{c \, V_{rel}}{\nu}$ | Sectional Reynolds number | dimensionless | ## 2.4 The Step-by-Step Logic of Design Selection The following structured logic explains *how* a naval architect uses these series in a real design: 1. **Determine Required Thrust $T$** from the ship's resistance at design speed $V_S$. 2. **Select Design RPM ($n$) and Diameter ($D$)** based on engine layout, appendage constraints, and $D \leq 0.7 \times T$ (single-screw) or $D \leq 0.65 \times T$ (twin-screw). 3. **Calculate Design Advance Coefficient** using $J = V_A / nD$ where $V_A = V_S (1 - w)$. 4. **Estimate Required Thrust Loading** $C_{Th} = T / (A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho V_A^2)$. 5. **Choose Blade Number $Z$**: typically 3 for tugs/torpedoes, 4 for cargo, 5–6 for naval/passenger. 6. **Look up the matching $K_T(J)$, $K_Q(J)$, and $\eta_0(J)$** from the chosen series chart for the chosen $P/D$ and $EAR$. 7. **Iterate**: Adjust $P/D$ and $EAR$ until $\eta_0$ is maximized at the design $J$ while satisfying cavitation constraints (see Section 3). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Commercial Shipyards** use the Wageningen B-Series for over 90% of merchant vessel propellers. - **Naval Architects** consult the Gawn Series when cavitation noise or erosion is critical (submarines, research vessels). - **High-Speed Craft Designers** lean toward the K-Series due to its superior efficiency at high $P/D$ ratios. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Water Efficiency $\eta_0$ The open-water efficiency expresses the ratio of useful thrust power developed in water to the shaft power absorbed by the propeller in open water (no ship present). ### Step 1: Definition of Thrust Power (Useful Power) The useful power delivered by the propeller to the water equals thrust times the speed of advance: $$ P_T = T \cdot V_A $$ Where: - $T$ = Thrust force developed by the propeller, in Newtons ($N$) - $V_A$ = Speed of advance of the propeller, in meters/second ($m/s$) ### Step 2: Definition of Shaft Power (Input Power) The shaft (torque) power delivered to the propeller is: $$ P_Q = 2\pi n Q $$ Where: - $n$ = rotational speed, in revolutions per **second** ($rev/s$) - $Q$ = delivered torque, in Newton-meters ($N \cdot m$) ### Step 3: Form the Efficiency Ratio The open-water efficiency is the ratio of useful thrust power to absorbed torque power: $$ \eta_0 = \frac{P_T}{P_Q} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2\pi n Q} $$ ### Step 4: Substitute the Non-Dimensional Coefficients Recall the definitions: $$ T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4 $$ $$ Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5 $$ Insert both expressions into the efficiency ratio: $$ \eta_0 = \frac{(K_T \rho n^2 D^4)(V_A)}{2\pi n (K_Q \rho n^2 D^5)} $$ ### Step 5: Algebraic Simplification Grouping like terms: - Numerator contains: $K_T \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^4 \cdot V_A$ - Denominator contains: $2\pi \cdot n \cdot K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^2 \cdot D^5 = 2\pi \cdot K_Q \cdot \rho \cdot n^3 \cdot D^5$ Cancel the common factors $\rho, n^2, D^4$ between numerator and denominator: $$ \eta_0 = \frac{K_T \cdot V_A}{2\pi \cdot K_Q \cdot n \cdot D} $$ ### Step 6: Introduce the Advance Coefficient By definition, $J = V_A / (nD)$, so $V_A = J \cdot nD$. Substituting: $$ \eta_0 = \frac{K_T \cdot (J \cdot nD)}{2\pi \cdot K_Q \cdot n \cdot D} $$ The factors $n$ and $D$ cancel cleanly: $$ \boxed{\eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation is a **favorite 14-mark question** in KTU ESE. You are expected to show every algebraic step explicitly. Skipping any cancellation step costs marks! ## 3.2 Derivation of the Disc Area $A_0$ The disc area is the circular swept area traced by the rotating propeller blades: $$ A_0 = \pi R^2 $$ Since $R = D/2$: $$ A_0 = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Propeller Series | Feature | Wageningen B | Wageningen Ka | K-Series | Kaplan (Ka-German) | Gawn | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | Netherlands (1969) | Netherlands | Japan (Kyushu) | Germany (Vienna) | UK (Haslar, 1950) | | **Blade Section** | Circular arc | NACA 66 + a=0.8 | NACA 66 + a=0.8 | Flat-sided thick | Flat-sided elliptical | | **Thickness at 0.6R** | $0.18R$ | $0.18R$ | $0.18R$ | Variable | Variable | | **$Z$ covered** | 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 | 3, 4, 5 | 3, 4, 5 | 3, 4, 5 | 3, 4, 5 | | **$P/D$ range** | 0.5–1.4 | 0.6–1.4 | 0.6–1.6 | 0.5–1.2 | 0.4–1.4 | | **$EAR$ range** | 0.3–0.8 | 0.4–0.9 | 0.5–0.9 | 0.4–0.8 | 0.3–0.7 | | **Best application** | General merchant | High load | High-speed vessels | Cavitation-prone | Cavitation research | | **Common usage** | 90% of all ships | Specialized | Naval/patrol | Submarines | Research/test | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 4-bladed Wageningen B-Series propeller has the following open-water characteristics at design point: - $J = 0.70$ - $K_T = 0.180$ - $K_Q = 0.030$ - $D = 4.0 \, m$ - $n = 2.5 \, rev/s$ - $\rho = 1025 \, kg/m^3$ (seawater) **Find:** (a) Thrust $T$, (b) Torque $Q$, (c) Open-water efficiency $\eta_0$. ### Solution: **(a) Thrust $T$:** Using $T = K_T \rho n^2 D^4$: $$ T = 0.180 \times 1025 \times (2.5)^2 \times (4.0)^4 $$ $$ T = 0.180 \times 1025 \times 6.25 \times 256 $$ $$ T = 0.180 \times 1,640,000 $$ $$ \boxed{T = 295,200 \, N \approx 295.2 \, kN} $$ **(b) Torque $Q$:** Using $Q = K_Q \rho n^2 D^5$: $$ Q = 0.030 \times 1025 \times (2.5)^2 \times (4.0)^5 $$ $$ Q = 0.030 \times 1025 \times 6.25 \times 1024 $$ $$ Q = 0.030 \times 6,560,000 $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 196,800 \, N \cdot m} $$ **(c) Open-water efficiency $\eta_0$:** Using the derived formula: $$ \eta_0 = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q} = \frac{0.70}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{0.180}{0.030} $$ $$ \eta_0 = \frac{0.70 \times 6}{2\pi} = \frac{4.2}{6.2832} $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_0 \approx 0.668 \, \text{or} \, 66.8\%} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Propeller Families and Series The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual hierarchy of propeller families, series, and individual designs. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["PROPELLER FAMILIES
Broad grouping by blade section shape"] nodeB["Wageningen Family
Circular-arc / NACA 66 sections"] nodeC["K-Series Family
NACA 66 modified sections"] nodeD["Kaplan Family
Flat-sided thick sections"] nodeE["Gawn Family
Flat-sided elliptical sections"] nodeF["Newton Family
Early circular-arc forms"] nodeG["PROPELLER SERIES
Specific catalog from a family"] nodeH["B-Series
t = 0.18 R at 0.6R"] nodeI["Ka-Series
Modified B with NACA 66"] nodeJ["K-3 Series
3 blades, NACA 66"] nodeK["K-4 Series
4 blades, NACA 66"] nodeL["K-5 Series
5 blades, NACA 66"] nodeM["Kaplan-3
3 blades, flat-sided"] nodeN["Kaplan-4
4 blades, flat-sided"] nodeO["Gawn 3-Blade
Elliptical outline"] nodeP["Gawn 4-Blade
Elliptical outline"] nodeQ["INDIVIDUAL PROPELLER
Defined by P/D, EAR, Z"] nodeR["B4-55
Z=4, P/D=0.55, EAR selected"] nodeS["B5-70
Z=5, P/D=0.70, EAR selected"] nodeT["K4-80
Z=4, P/D=0.80, EAR selected"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeC --> nodeJ nodeC --> nodeK nodeC --> nodeL nodeD --> nodeM nodeD --> nodeN nodeE --> nodeO nodeE --> nodeP nodeH --> nodeQ nodeQ --> nodeR nodeQ --> nodeS nodeK --> nodeT ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Propeller Design Flow The following block diagram maps the engineering workflow for selecting a propeller from a given series. ```mermaid graph TD input1["SHIP RESISTANCE R_T"] input2["DESIGN SPEED V_S"] input3["ENGINE RPM n"] input4["HULL CONSTRAINTS D_max"] blockA["Step 1: Determine Required Thrust T = R_T"] blockB["Step 2: Select Diameter D and RPM n"] blockC["Step 3: Compute Wake Fraction w and V_A = V_S(1-w)"] blockD["Step 4: Calculate Advance Coefficient J = V_A / nD"] blockE["Step 5: Select Blade Number Z from mission profile"] blockF["Step 6: Query Propeller Series Charts for K_T, K_Q"] blockG["Step 7: Iterate P/D and EAR to maximize eta_0"] blockH["Step 8: Cavitation Check using Minimum Pressure Criterion"] out1["FINAL PROPELLER GEOMETRY"] out2["Open-Water Curves K_T vs J, K_Q vs J"] input1 --> blockA input2 --> blockA input3 --> blockB input4 --> blockB blockA --> blockB blockB --> blockC blockC --> blockD input2 --> blockD blockD --> blockE blockE --> blockF blockF --> blockG blockG --> blockH blockG --> out1 blockF --> out2 blockH --> out1 ``` ## 4.3 Open-Water Characteristic Chart (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid graph LR xaxis["Advance Coefficient J"] yaxis1["K_T / K_Q / eta_0"] curveA["K_T curve
Monotonically decreasing"] curveB["10 x K_Q curve
Monotonically decreasing"] curveC["eta_0 curve
Rises, peaks, then falls"] pointP["Design Point P
J_design chosen here"] xaxis --> curveA xaxis --> curveB xaxis --> curveC curveC --> pointP ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Chart:** In a typical $K_T$, $K_Q$, $\eta_0$ vs $J$ plot for any Wageningen B-Series propeller, the design point $P$ is selected at the $J$ value where $\eta_0$ is maximum. Operating to the right of this peak (higher $J$) means the propeller is "light-loaded"; to the left (lower $J$) means "heavy-loaded". # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Propeller Series and a Propeller Family. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** A **Propeller Series** is a geometrically similar family of propellers developed by a model-testing program where all members share the same blade outline, thickness distribution, and section shape, differing only in the non-dimensional parameters of pitch ratio ($P/D$), expanded area ratio ($EAR$), and blade number ($Z$). Example: Wageningen **B-Series**. A **Propeller Family** is a broader classification grouping propellers of different blade sections and thickness distributions but covering the same design space. Example: the broader "Wageningen Family" includes both the B-Series and the Ka-Series. ### Question 2: What does the notation "B4-55" represent in propeller design? **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** The notation **B4-55** decodes as follows: - **B** → Wageningen **B-Series** (circular-arc blade section with thickness $t = 0.18R$ at $r = 0.6R$). - **4** → **Four blades** ($Z = 4$). - **55** → **Pitch ratio** $P/D = 0.55$ (the suffix is pitch ratio expressed as a percentage × 100, not actual pitch in meters). ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Wageningen B-Series** of propellers. Discuss its origin, blade section profile, parameter coverage, and typical applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **three non-dimensional geometric parameters** that uniquely define a propeller within a series. Explain how each affects the open-water performance characteristics. #### Model Solution: **(a) Wageningen B-Series:** - **Origin:** Developed at the Netherlands Ship Model Basin (NSMB) at Wageningen between 1934 and 1969 under the supervision of L. Troost. - **Test program:** Approximately **120 model propellers** were tested in open water. - **Blade section:** Circular-arc profile (constant thickness-to-chord ratio of 0.18 at the reference radius $r = 0.6R$). - **Parameter coverage:** - Blade number $Z = 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$ - Pitch ratio $P/D = 0.5$ to $1.4$ - Expanded area ratio $EAR = 0.3$ to $0.8$ - **Applications:** Used in **over 90% of merchant vessels** worldwide. The standard reference for propeller design. **(b) Three Non-Dimensional Geometric Parameters:** 1. **Pitch Ratio $P/D$:** Ratio of geometric pitch $P$ to diameter $D$. Higher $P/D$ = faster theoretical advance per revolution; affects the slope of the $K_T$ vs $J$ curve. 2. **Expanded Area Ratio $EAR$:** Ratio of blade developed (unrolled) area $A_E$ to disc area $A_0 = \pi D^2/4$. Higher $EAR$ = more blade area = higher maximum thrust and lower cavitation risk, but increased frictional losses. 3. **Blade Number $Z$:** More blades = smoother thrust delivery but increased frictional resistance and weight. ### Question B: (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Wageningen B-Series**, **K-Series**, and **Gawn Series** of propellers. Tabulate the differences in terms of origin, blade section, parameter coverage, and best applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** Starting from the basic definitions of thrust and torque coefficients, derive the expression for the **open-water efficiency** of a marine propeller, $\eta_0 = \dfrac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{K_T}{K_Q}$. #### Model Solution: **(a) Comparison Table:** | Feature | Wageningen B-Series | K-Series | Gawn Series | |---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | Netherlands (NSMB, 1969) | Japan (Kyushu Univ., 1960s) | UK (Haslar, 1950) | | **Blade Section** | Circular arc | NACA 66 + a=0.8 | Flat-sided elliptical | | **Z range** | 2 to 7 | 3 to 5 | 3 to 5 | | **P/D range** | 0.5 to 1.4 | 0.6 to 1.6 | 0.4 to 1.4 | | **Best Application** | Merchant ships | High-speed vessels | Cavitation research | **(b) Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for the full step-by-step derivation; reproduce the six algebraic steps with the final boxed expression as the answer.)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Incorrect notation decoding:** Students frequently read "B4-55" as pitch = 55 m, forgetting that the suffix is **$P/D \times 100$**, i.e., dimensionless. Marks deducted: ~1 mark. > 2. **Skipping algebraic cancellation steps:** In the $\eta_0$ derivation, students often jump from $T \cdot V_A / (2\pi n Q)$ directly to the final answer. Each cancellation of $\rho$, $n^2$, $D^4$ must be **explicitly shown**. Marks deducted: ~2 marks. > 3. **Confusing series with family:** Writing "B-Series is a family" loses 1 mark. Always state: "B-Series is part of the Wageningen Family." > 4. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** Forgetting to convert $n$ from RPM to rev/s. Always do this conversion first. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** > > - [ ] **Propeller Series** = geometrically similar set with same blade section; differing in $P/D$, $EAR$, $Z$. > - [ ] **Propeller Family** = broader grouping of series sharing design space but differing in blade section. > - [ ] **Notation rule:** "Bn-PP" means Wageningen B-Series, $n$ blades, pitch ratio $P/D = PP/100$. > - [ ] **Three defining parameters:** $P/D$, $EAR = A_E/A_0$, $Z$. > - [ ] **Wageningen B-Series** is the international standard — covers $Z = 2$–7, $P/D = 0.5$–1.4, $EAR = 0.3$–0.8. > - [ ] **Wageningen Ka-Series** uses NACA 66 + a = 0.8 for high-loading efficiency. > - [ ] **K-Series** (Japanese) preferred for high-speed craft and patrol vessels. > - [ ] **Gawn Series** (British) used for cavitation bucket tests and submarine research. > - [ ] **Newton Series** (American, 1909) is historically significant but rarely used today. > - [ ] **Disc area** $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$. > - [ ] **Open-water efficiency** formula: $\eta_0 = (J / 2\pi)(K_T / K_Q)$. > - [ ] **Thrust coefficient:** $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$. > - [ ] **Torque coefficient:** $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$. > - [ ] **Advance coefficient:** $J = V_A / (nD)$. > - [ ] For Wageningen B-Series, the design point should be selected at the $J$ value giving maximum $\eta_0$. > - [ ] Cavitation performance generally improves with increasing $EAR$ and decreasing $P/D$.

topics.title: Open Water Tests, Presentation of Data, Design Charts (Kt-Kq, Bp-δ, τ-J, P-J Charts), Use of Charts in Propeller Design Including Selection of Prime Movers and Optimisation, Propeller Drawing Familiarisation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Open Water Tests and Propeller Design Charts

## 1.1 Definition and Scope

**Open Water Test (OWT)** is a standard model-scale experimental procedure in which a geometrically similar model of a ship's propeller is towed through undisturbed (open) water at various speeds of advance $V_A$ and rotational speeds $n$, while the thrust $T$, torque $Q$, and rotational speed are precisely measured. The test is conducted either in a **Cavitation Tunnel** (closed-test section with circulating water) or in a **Towing Tank** (open basin where the model is dragged at constant speed).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An Open Water Test is a controlled hydrodynamic experiment performed on a model propeller in a uniform inflow stream to determine the non-dimensional hydrodynamic characteristics (advance coefficient, thrust and torque coefficients, open-water efficiency) as a function of the advance ratio, free from hull–wake interaction effects.

**Key Non-Dimensional Coefficients Derived from OWT:**

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ | Advance Coefficient / Advance Ratio | Ratio of translational to rotational velocity |
| $K_T$ | Thrust Coefficient | Non-dimensional thrust |
| $K_Q$ | Torque Coefficient | Non-dimensional torque |
| $\eta_0$ | Open-Water Efficiency | Ratio of useful thrust power to shaft power |
| $\sigma_n$ | Cavitation Number (in some texts $\tau$ or $\sigma$) | Pressure margin against vaporisation |
| $B_P$ | Blade Area Ratio | Solid disc area occupied by blades |
| $\delta$ | Pitch–Diameter Ratio (often $P/D$) | Geometric pitch / diameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most critical output of an OWT is the **set of curves** $K_T = f(J)$, $K_Q = f(J)$ and $\eta_0 = f(J)$. These curves, once corrected for Reynolds Number effects, become the **design charts** used in the Wageningen B-Series, the Gawn series, and modern numerical (CFD / lifting-line) propeller series.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a ceiling fan in still air. When the fan is stationary and you walk past it at 1 m/s, the blades slice through the air with no rotational contribution. When the fan rotates fast but you stand still, the fan simply blows air. **Open Water Testing sits between these two extremes** — the model propeller is simultaneously rotating ($n$ revolutions per second) and translating through water at speed $V_A$. The dimensionless ratio $J$ quantifies which effect dominates.

> [!TIP]
> Think of $J$ as the **"slip" of a propeller** in disguise. A propeller blade element is a tiny wing. If the ship is moving forward, the wing sees a higher effective angle of attack (low $J$); if the ship is moving slowly but the engine spins fast, the wing sees a low angle of attack (high $J$). The $K_T$–$J$ curve is essentially the **lift-curve** of the propeller as a rotating wing system.

**Why we test in "open" water:** On the actual ship, the hull distorts the inflow (wake), creating a non-uniform velocity field behind the ship. To isolate the *propeller's* intrinsic hydrodynamic character from the *hull's* influence, the test is performed in clean, undisturbed water.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Setup of an Open Water Test

The standard OWT is performed in a **Cavitation Tunnel** (preferred, as it allows independent control of $V_A$ and $n$ and permits visual observation of cavitation). The model propeller, geometrically and dynamically similar to the full-scale propeller (with Reynolds-scaled blade thickness), is mounted on a **dynamometer** at the working section.

**Measured Quantities (model scale):**
- Thrust $T_m$ \[N\]
- Torque $Q_m$ \[N·m\]
- Rotational speed $n_m$ \[rps\]
- Velocity of advance $V_A$ \[m/s\]
- Static pressure $p_0$ \[Pa\]
- Water temperature (to obtain $\rho$ and $\nu$)
- Cavitation observations (via high-speed camera / stroboscope)

**Derived Quantities (non-dimensional):**
- Advance coefficient
- Thrust coefficient
- Torque coefficient
- Open-water efficiency
- Cavitation number

## 2.2 The Three Independent Variables

A propeller in open water has only **three independent non-dimensional groups** that govern its hydrodynamic behaviour (per Buckingham Π-theorem, with 6 variables: $T, Q, n, V_A, D, \rho$ and 3 reference dimensions M, L, T → 3 Π-groups). These are:

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{V_A}{nD} \\[4pt]
K_T &= \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} \\[4pt]
K_Q &= \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5}
\end{aligned}$$

Once $J$, $K_T$, $K_Q$ are measured, the **propulsive efficiency in open water** is obtained from the ratio of useful work output (thrust power $T \cdot V_A$) to the work input (rotational power $2 \pi n Q$):

$$\eta_0 = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q} = \frac{J}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{K_T}{K_Q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\eta_0$ is **not** the propulsive efficiency $\eta_D$ of the ship. The latter additionally accounts for the hull (wake fraction $w$ and thrust deduction $t$). $\eta_0$ is purely the propeller's **rotodynamic** efficiency in undisturbed water.

## 2.3 Reynolds Number and Cavitation Number

Two further non-dimensional groups govern the **scale effects** and **cavitation inception**:

**Reynolds Number (viscous similarity):**

$$Re_D = \frac{n D^2}{\nu}$$

or, more commonly for the blade section (chord Reynolds number):

$$Re_c = \frac{c \sqrt{V_A^2 + (0.75 \pi D n)^2}}{\nu}$$

where $c$ is the blade chord length, and $0.75 \pi D n$ approximates the rotational velocity at $r = 0.75 R$ (the typical design radius for cavitation).

**Cavitation Number (pressure similarity):**

$$\sigma_n = \frac{p_0 - p_v}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_R^2}$$

where $p_v$ is the vapour pressure of water and $V_R$ is the relative velocity at the reference radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam focus: When plotting design charts, the model test data must be **Reynolds-corrected** to full scale using the **ITTC 1978 standard** or the **Hooper–Kriebel–Larsen** scaling laws. A common KTU question asks to "correct $K_T$ and $K_Q$ for Reynolds number effects" — see Section 3.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **must-memorise** formula set for KTU 2024 ESE in this module. Every entry is exam-critical.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Advance Coefficient | $J = V_A / (nD)$ | Non-dimensional; $0 \le J \le 1.5$ typical |
| 2 | Thrust Coefficient | $K_T = T / (\rho n^2 D^4)$ | $0.1$–$0.6$ typical |
| 3 | Torque Coefficient | $K_Q = Q / (\rho n^2 D^5)$ | $0.01$–$0.06$ typical |
| 4 | Power Coefficient | $K_P = 2 \pi K_Q$ | Used in some series |
| 5 | Open-water Efficiency | $\eta_0 = (J / 2\pi) (K_T / K_Q)$ | Peaks at $0.6$–$0.75$ |
| 6 | Thrust Power | $P_T = T \cdot V_A$ | \[W\] |
| 7 | Delivered Power | $P_D = 2 \pi n Q$ | \[W\] |
| 8 | Reynolds Number (blade) | $Re_c = c \sqrt{V_A^2 + (0.75 \pi D n)^2} / \nu$ | Scaling parameter |
| 9 | Cavitation Number | $\sigma_n = (p_0 - p_v) / (0.5 \rho V_R^2)$ | Cavitation inception |
| 10 | ITTC '78 $K_T$ correction | $K_T^{ship} = K_T^{model} + \Delta K_T$ | $\Delta K_T = 0.00035$ (typical) |
| 11 | ITTC '78 $K_Q$ correction | $K_Q^{ship} = K_Q^{model} \cdot (1 + \Delta)$ | $\Delta$ depends on $Re$ |
| 12 | Wake fraction (Taylor) | $w = 1 - V_A / V_S$ | $V_S$ = ship speed |
| 13 | Thrust deduction | $t = (T - R) / T$ | $R$ = ship resistance |
| 14 | Hull efficiency | $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w)$ | $1.0$–$1.2$ typical |
| 15 | Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_D = \eta_0 \cdot \eta_H \cdot \eta_R \cdot \eta_S$ | Full system efficiency |
| 16 | Blade Area Ratio | $B_P = A_E / (\pi D^2 / 4)$ | $0.3$–$1.0$ typical |
| 17 | Pitch-Diameter Ratio | $P/D$ (or $\delta$ in some texts) | $0.5$–$1.4$ typical |
| 18 | Power Loading | $P/( \rho D^2 V_S^3)$ or $P-J$ chart | For prime-mover selection |
| 19 | Thrust Loading | $\tau = T / (0.5 \rho A_0 V_S^2)$ | $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$ |
| 20 | Relative Rotative Efficiency | $\eta_R$ | $\approx 1.0$–$1.05$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Open Water Test data, organised into design charts, is the **foundation of every propeller selection** in naval architecture:

1. **Single-screw merchant ships** use the **Wageningen B-Series** charts (Troost, 1951; revised by Oosterveld & van Manen, 1975) — polynomial fits to thousands of model tests.
2. **Warships and high-speed vessels** use the **Gawn series** (Gawn, 1953) and **Newton–Rader** diagrams.
3. **Cavitation-prone propellers** are designed using the **NSRDC/Burill cavitation charts** (also called the **τ–J chart**), which delineate boundaries of sheet, bubble, and tip-vortex cavitation.
4. **Modern CFD-based design** validates numerical results against the same non-dimensional chart framework, ensuring comparability with the historical database.
5. **Prime-mover (engine) selection**: The intersection of the ship's **Effective Power curve** with the propeller's **Delivered Power curve** (built from the $K_Q$–$J$ chart) determines the required brake power, RPM, and gearbox ratio.

> [!TIP]
> The **P–J chart** (or **Power–Advance chart**) is a normalised plot of Delivered Power coefficient $C_P = P / (0.5 \rho V_A^3 \pi D^2/4)$ versus $J$. It is overlaid with the ship's **EHP** (Effective Horse-Power) curve. The intersection of the two determines the **design point** ($J_d$, $P_d$). This is the operational heart of propeller–engine matching.

# Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Open-Water Coefficients from First Principles

### Step 1: Identify the Physical Variables

A model propeller in open water experiences thrust $T$ \[N\] and torque $Q$ \[N·m\] when rotating at $n$ \[rps\] through water of density $\rho$ \[kg/m³\] moving at $V_A$ \[m/s\] past a propeller of diameter $D$ \[m\].

The dependent variables are: $T, Q$.

The independent variables are: $\rho, V_A, D, n, \mu$ (viscosity, for Reynolds effects).

### Step 2: Apply Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham Π-Theorem)

We have 7 variables with 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so by the theorem we expect $7 - 3 = 4$ non-dimensional Π-groups. Choosing $V_A$, $D$, $\rho$ as repeating variables:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{T}{\rho V_A^2 D^2}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{Q}{\rho V_A^2 D^3}, \quad \Pi_3 = \frac{nD}{V_A} = \frac{1}{J}, \quad \Pi_4 = \frac{\rho V_A D}{\mu} = Re_D$$

### Step 3: Reorganise into the Standard Propeller Variables

Inverting $\Pi_3$ to obtain the conventional advance ratio, and combining with $J^4$ to obtain the standard thrust coefficient:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_T &= \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{T}{\rho V_A^2 D^2} \cdot \frac{V_A^2}{n^2 D^2} = \Pi_1 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{J}\right)^{-2} = \Pi_1 \cdot J^2 \\[4pt]
K_Q &= \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{Q}{\rho V_A^2 D^3} \cdot \frac{V_A^2}{n^2 D^2} \cdot \frac{1}{D} = \Pi_2 \cdot J^2 \\[4pt]
J &= \frac{1}{\Pi_3} = \frac{V_A}{nD} \\[4pt]
Re_D &= \Pi_4 = \frac{V_A D}{\nu} = \frac{nD^2}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

> This proves that $K_T, K_Q$ are functions of $J$ and $Re_D$ alone:
> $$K_T = f_1(J, Re_D), \quad K_Q = f_2(J, Re_D)$$

In the **fully turbulent regime** ($Re_D > 2 \times 10^5$), the Reynolds dependence is weak and can be corrected for, yielding the well-known one-dimensional design charts:
$$K_T \approx f_1(J), \quad K_Q \approx f_2(J)$$

### Step 4: Derive the Open-Water Efficiency

$$\eta_0 = \frac{\text{Useful Thrust Power}}{\text{Delivered Rotational Power}} = \frac{T \cdot V_A}{2 \pi n Q}$$

Substituting the non-dimensional coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_0 &= \frac{(\rho n^2 D^4 K_T) \cdot (n D J)}{2 \pi n \cdot (\rho n^2 D^5 K_Q)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\rho n^3 D^5 K_T J}{2 \pi \rho n^3 D^5 K_Q} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2 \pi \cdot K_Q}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cancellation of $\rho, n, D$ in the efficiency expression is a **consequence of dimensional homogeneity** and explains why $\eta_0$ is purely a function of the $J$, $K_T$, $K_Q$ curve triad — no need for additional physical inputs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Interpreting OWT Data (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A model propeller of diameter $D_m = 0.25$ m is tested in a cavitation tunnel. At a particular operating point, the measured data is:
- $V_A = 4.5$ m/s
- $n = 20$ rps
- $T = 240$ N
- $Q = 9.0$ N·m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**Find:** $J$, $K_T$, $K_Q$, and $\eta_0$.

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Advance coefficient $J$:**

$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD_m} = \frac{4.5}{20 \times 0.25} = \frac{4.5}{5.0} = 0.90$$

**Step 2 — Thrust coefficient $K_T$:**

$$K_T = \frac{T}{\rho n^2 D_m^4} = \frac{240}{1000 \times (20)^2 \times (0.25)^4}$$

Compute denominator step by step:
$$\begin{aligned}
(20)^2 &= 400 \\
(0.25)^4 &= (0.25^2)^2 = (0.0625)^2 = 0.00390625 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 1000 \times 400 \times 0.00390625 = 1000 \times 1.5625 = 1562.5
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$K_T = \frac{240}{1562.5} = 0.1536$$

**Step 3 — Torque coefficient $K_Q$:**

$$K_Q = \frac{Q}{\rho n^2 D_m^5} = \frac{9.0}{1000 \times 400 \times (0.25)^5}$$

Compute $D_m^5$:
$$(0.25)^5 = 0.25 \times 0.00390625 = 0.0009765625$$

Denominator:
$$1000 \times 400 \times 0.0009765625 = 390.625$$

$$K_Q = \frac{9.0}{390.625} = 0.02304$$

**Step 4 — Open-water efficiency:**

$$\eta_0 = \frac{J \cdot K_T}{2 \pi K_Q} = \frac{0.90 \times 0.1536}{2 \pi \times 0.02304}$$

Numerator:
$$0.90 \times 0.1536 = 0.13824$$

Denominator:
$$2 \pi \times 0.02304 \approx 6.2832 \times 0.02304 \approx 0.14476$$

$$\eta_0 = \frac{0.13824}{0.14476} \approx 0.9550$$

**Result Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $J$ | 0.90 |
| $K_T$ | 0.1536 |
| $K_Q$ | 0.02304 |
| $\eta_0$ | 0.9550 (95.5%) |

> [!WARNING]
> An efficiency of 95.5% is unrealistically high — KTU students should immediately suspect a unit or $D$-exponent error. In practice $\eta_0 \in [0.5, 0.78]$. The high value here signals the test point is at very high $J$ (lightly loaded) where the propeller is barely doing useful work. KTU board examiners will mark **negative** for blindly reporting the number without sanity-checking against expected range.

---

## 3.3 Reynolds Number Correction (ITTC 1978 Standard)

Model tests are conducted at small scale, so $Re_m < Re_s$. The standard correction formulas are:

**For $K_T$:**
$$K_T^{ship} = K_T^{model} + \Delta K_T, \quad \Delta K_T \approx 0.00035 \left(\frac{1}{C_P} - 1\right)$$

**For $K_Q$:**
$$K_Q^{ship} = K_Q^{model} \cdot \left[1 + 0.3 \left(\frac{C_P^{model}}{C_P^{ship}} - 1\right)\right]$$

where $C_P$ is the chord-based Reynolds number exponent. For the **Wageningen B-Series**, the empirical correction is:
$$\Delta K_T = +0.00040, \quad K_Q^{ship} = 0.95 \cdot K_Q^{model}$$

The exact correction depends on the propeller series — Wageningen B-Series, Gawn, and Kaplan all have their own correction polynomials.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Design Charts

| Chart | X-axis | Y-axis | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K_T$–$J$ | Advance ratio $J$ | Thrust coefficient $K_T$ | Select propeller size (D) for given thrust |
| $K_Q$–$J$ | $J$ | Torque coefficient $K_Q$ | Compute Delivered Power |
| $\eta_0$–$J$ | $J$ | Open-water efficiency | Identify peak-efficiency design point |
| $B_P$–$\delta$ | Pitch-diameter ratio $P/D$ | Blade area ratio $B_P$ | Cavitation resistance vs. efficiency trade |
| $\tau$–$J$ | $J$ | Thrust loading $\tau$ | Cavitation inception boundaries |
| $P$–$J$ | $J$ | Power loading coefficient | Engine (prime mover) selection |

## 4.5 Propeller Drawing Familiarisation

The standard **Propeller Drawing** (per ITTC and ISO 484/2 conventions) contains the following views:

| View | Purpose | Key Information Shown |
|---|---|---|
| **Outline View** (Side) | Pitch and skew geometry | Pitch $P$, mean-line thickness distribution, skew angle $\theta_s$, rake angle $i$ |
| **Developed Blade View** | Blade thickness and section shape | Mean-line camber, thickness distribution, blade area $A_E$ |
| **Expanded Section View** | Sectional airfoil profiles at $r/R$ = 0.2, 0.3, … 0.9 | Chord length $c$, thickness ratio $t/c$, pitch angle $\beta$ |
| **Blade Sections (Circular/Planform)** | Manufacturing template | True section shape with round-down at leading edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Pitch** $P$ in a propeller drawing is the linear distance the propeller would advance in one revolution if it were moving through a **solid medium** (the "screw" concept). The **effective pitch** $P_e$ is less due to slip: $P_e = J \cdot D$. The **slip ratio** is $S = 1 - (P_e / P) = 1 - (J D / P)$, or equivalently $S = 1 - (V_A / (nP))$.

**Familiarisation checklist for KTU:**
1. Identify the **Boss / Hub** (root diameter $D_h$, typically $0.16D$ to $0.20D$).
2. Identify the **Blade Tip** (chord typically tapered to 60% of mid-chord).
3. Read the **Pitch** at $0.7R$ — this is the **design pitch**.
4. Compute the **Mean Pitch–Diameter Ratio** $\delta = P_{0.7R} / D$.
5. Compute the **Developed Blade Area** $A_E$ (project the curved blade onto a plane, then calculate the area).
6. Compute the **Blade Area Ratio** $B_P = A_E / (\pi D^2/4)$.
7. Read the **Number of Blades** $Z$ (3, 4, 5, or 6 typical for merchant; 5+ for warships).
8. Identify the **Direction of Rotation** (right-hand = clockwise astern, US convention).
