topics.title: Vertical Axis Propellers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vertical Axis Propellers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Vertical Axis Propeller (VAP)** is a marine propulsor in which the rotational axis of the blade assembly is oriented **vertically** (perpendicular to the ship’s waterline) rather than along the ship’s longitudinal centerline as in conventional fixed-pitch or controllable-pitch propellers. The thrust is generated by hydrofoils (blades) that traverse a circular path about this vertical axis, producing a reaction force whose horizontal component is the useful thrust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** Vertical axis propellers are thrust-producing devices whose axis of rotation is vertical. The principal types studied under KTU Module 4 are the **Cycloidal Propeller (Voith-Schneider Propeller – VSP)** and the **Rim-Driven Propeller**, both of which can deliver thrust in any direction without the need for a rudder.

The two principal families of vertical-axis propulsors are:

1. **Cycloidal (Voith-Schneider) Propeller (VSP)** – a vertically mounted rotor carrying multiple hydrofoil blades whose pitch is cyclically varied as they rotate, allowing each blade to generate thrust in a controlled direction throughout its circular orbit.
2. **Rim-Driven Thruster (RDT)** – a ducted propeller integrated into a ring-shaped stator housing where the rim (rotor ring) carries permanent magnets, eliminating the need for a central shaft and hub.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Merry-Go-Round of Oars"**
> Imagine a **merry-go-round** standing on a flat lake. Instead of being fixed to the platform, several **rowing oars** are attached to its rim. As the platform spins, the oars dip into the water. A skilled helmsman angles each oar differently depending on where it is in the circle — those at the **back of the circle** push water forward, those at the **front** push it backward, and those on the sides are feathered. Because the oars are angled in synchrony with rotation, the *whole* machine moves in *one chosen direction* — forward, backward, sideways — without ever turning a rudder. That is exactly how a **Voith-Schneider Propeller** works: a vertically turning disc with controllable-angle blades acting like synchronized oars.

A second analogy: a **rim-driven thruster** is like a **floating motor** — the propeller is mounted inside a ring, and the ring itself is the motor. There is no shaft, no strut, and no hub. Water flows through the ring and is accelerated by the integrated blades.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Number of blades in a VSP:** typically **$N_{b} = 5$** blades (range 4–6) for ship propulsion.
- **Number of blades in a typical RDT:** **$N_{b} = 5$ to $7$** integrated into the rim.
- **Disk loading on VSP:** $C_T \approx 0.5 - 1.2$ (lower than open propellers of equal thrust).
- **Rotational speed:** $n \approx 30$–$75$ rpm (much lower than conventional propellers).
- **Hydraulic efficiency of VSP:** $\eta_H \approx 0.70$ – $0.85$.
- **Propulsive efficiency:** $\eta_D \cdot \eta_H$ where $\eta_D$ (rotative efficiency) for VSP is typically **0.85–0.95**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vertical Axis?** For ships requiring extreme **maneuverability** — tugs, ferries, icebreakers, naval vessels, SWATHs, and offshore support vessels — the VSP provides **thrust in any direction (azimuth-independent)** without a rudder, with a bollard-pull efficiency superior to conventional propellers in low-speed, high-load conditions.

## 1.4 Historical Context

The cycloidal propeller was invented by German engineer **Ernst Schneider** in **1927**, later commercialized by the Austrian company **Voith**. The first commercial installation was in **1928**, and Voith-Schneider Propellers (VSPs) are still in production today, with over **1,200 units** in service globally as of recent surveys.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of the Voith-Schneider Propeller (VSP)

The VSP consists of a **vertical rotor (drum)** rotating about a vertical axis. Mounted on the rotor periphery are $N_b$ hydrofoil blades. Each blade is connected via a **mechanical eccentrics system** (a pair of internal cams) to a **stationary control rod** above the rotor. The relative motion between the eccentric control rod and the rotor causes the **pitch (angle of attack) of each blade to vary cyclically** as the blade traverses the circular orbit.

### 2.1.1 Working Cycle (in the rotor frame)

At every angular position $\theta$ around the rotor, the blade pitch is mechanically set such that the **hydrodynamic force on the blade** has a horizontal component aligned with the chosen thrust direction. The vertical component cancels out due to symmetry.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. The **rotor** spins at constant rotational speed $n$ (rpm) about the **vertical axis**.
2. As the rotor turns, a **stationary eccentric (control wheel)** above the rotor remains fixed in heading.
3. Each blade is pinned to the rotor at its root and connected to a **vertical control rod** sliding in a guide.
4. The control rod has an **eccentric pin** that engages a **slot in the blade root**.
5. The relative sliding of the pin in the slot continuously adjusts the blade pitch $\beta(\theta)$.
6. By **rotating the control wheel**, the operator changes the phase of the pitch variation, hence the **direction of net horizontal thrust**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Force Direction:** The instantaneous horizontal force per blade is $F_b(\theta)$. The vector sum of $F_b$ over all $N_b$ blades at any instant yields a **constant horizontal force vector $\vec{T}$** whose direction is dictated by the control wheel's angular position. This is why VSPs can pivot thrust 360° in fractions of a second.

## 2.2 The Cycloidal Pitch Equation

The instantaneous pitch angle of a VSP blade is given by:

$$\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

where:

- $\beta_0$ = mean pitch offset (set to align thrust vector with desired direction)
- $\beta_1$ = pitch amplitude (proportional to commanded thrust magnitude)
- $\theta$ = instantaneous rotor angular position
- $\theta_{cmd}$ = command heading of the control wheel (defines thrust direction)

The control wheel's angular position $\theta_{cmd}$ is mechanically or hydraulically locked to the operator's joystick / bridge control.

## 2.3 Thrust Force Derivation (Per Blade)

For a single hydrofoil blade moving with tangential velocity:

$$V_{tan} = 2\pi r n / 60 = \pi D n / 60$$

at mean radius $r = D/2$ where $D$ is the rotor diameter.

The hydrodynamic force per blade resolved into normal and tangential components, then summed in the ship-fixed frame, yields the **total thrust**:

$$T = N_b \cdot C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{tan}^2 \cdot A_b \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

where:

- $C_L$ = lift coefficient of the blade section
- $\rho$ = mass density of water (**$1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$** for seawater)
- $A_b$ = blade planform area
- $\phi$ = angle between blade force vector and thrust axis
- $N_b$ = number of blades

## 2.4 Rim-Driven Thrusters (RDT) — Theory

In an RDT, the propeller blades are mounted inside a **ducted ring** (stator) at the tip. The rotor ring is driven by an **integrated electric motor** with permanent magnets embedded in the ring's inner circumference. A stationary stator winding sits in the housing behind the rim.

Key performance relationships mirror conventional propellers:

$$T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^4$$

$$Q = C_Q \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^5$$

$$P = 2\pi n Q / 60$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use an RDT?** The integrated motor eliminates the need for shafting, struts, and gearboxes. This makes RDTs ideal for **AUVs, ROVs, podded propulsors**, and naval vessels where **low acoustic signature, high efficiency, and minimal cavitation** are critical.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| Blade tangential velocity | $V_{tan} = \pi D n / 60$ | m/s | At mean radius $r = D/2$ |
| Thrust per blade | $T_b = C_L \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{tan}^2 A_b \cos\phi$ | N | N/A |
| Total VSP thrust | $T = N_b \cdot T_b$ | N | N/A |
| Conventional thrust | $T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^4$ | N | Open-water coefficient |
| Torque | $Q = C_Q \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^5$ | N·m | Open-water coefficient |
| Power absorbed | $P = 2\pi n Q$ | W | N/A |
| Propulsive efficiency | $\eta_P = \dfrac{T \cdot V_S}{P}$ | dimensionless | N/A |
| Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_H = \dfrac{T \cdot V_A / 2}{P}$ (VSP) | dimensionless | Approximation |
| Disc loading | $C_T = T / (\tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^4)$ | dimensionless | N/A |
| Open-water efficiency | $\eta_O = \dfrac{C_T}{C_Q} \cdot \dfrac{J}{2\pi}$ | dimensionless | $J = V_A/(nD)$ |
| Advance coefficient | $J = V_A / (nD)$ | dimensionless | Conventional prop. |
| Cycloidal pitch | $\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$ | rad | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The cycloidal pitch equation $\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$ is a *favorite* KTU question. Always draw the sinusoidal variation, label amplitude $\beta_1$ and phase $\theta_{cmd}$, and explain that the **control wheel rotation** sets $\theta_{cmd}$, while the **joystick deflection** sets $\beta_1$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tugboats:** VSPs deliver the highest **bollard pull** per unit installed power.
- **Ferries & SWATHs:** VSPs enable **zero-radius turning** and station-keeping without bow thrusters.
- **Icebreakers:** Multi-VSP layouts allow directional thrust through broken ice.
- **Offshore supply vessels:** RDTs integrated into azimuth pods reduce cavitation and noise.
- **Submarines & AUVs:** RDTs eliminate shaft seals, reducing radiated noise.
- **Naval combatants:** VSPs enable crash-stop and side-step maneuvers.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Thrust of a VSP from First Principles

We derive the thrust produced by a Voith-Schneider Propeller with $N_b$ blades, each of planform area $A_b$, rotating at $n$ rpm with rotor diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Establish the velocity field seen by a blade element.**

The blade travels around a vertical circle of diameter $D$. At the mean radius $r = D/2$, the blade's tangential velocity in the ship-fixed frame is:

$$V_{tan} = \frac{2\pi r n}{60} = \frac{\pi D n}{60}$$

The blade's instantaneous pitch $\beta(\theta)$ is set by the mechanical eccentric such that the **hydrodynamic angle of attack** is $\alpha$ relative to the relative flow.

**Step 2 — Resolve the lift and drag on a blade element.**

For a 2D blade section with chord $c$ and span $b$ (so $A_b = c \cdot b$), the section lift is:

$$L' = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{tan}^2 \, c \cdot C_L(\alpha)$$

The drag is:

$$D' = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{tan}^2 \, c \cdot C_D(\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Resolve into the ship-fixed (thrust) frame.**

Let the local force vector make an angle $\phi$ with the desired thrust axis. The thrust contribution per unit span is:

$$dT_b = L' \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd}) - D' \cos(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

integrated across the blade span and blade chord (for uniform $C_L, C_D$).

**Step 4 — Sum over all blades.**

At any instant, the $N_b$ blades are uniformly distributed around the rotor at angular positions $\theta_k = 2\pi k / N_b$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, N_b - 1$.

The total instantaneous thrust is:

$$T = \sum_{k=0}^{N_b - 1} T_b(\theta_k)$$

**Step 5 — Use the identity of the cycloidal pitch modulation.**

Because the pitch is set to $\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$, integrating over one full rotation yields (after using orthogonality of sines and cosines on a uniform grid):

$$T = N_b \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_{tan}^2 \, A_b \, C_L \cdot \cos(\phi_{avg})$$

This is a **constant** in time, because the cyclic pitch mechanism ensures the horizontal components add coherently.

**Step 6 — Substitute the tangential velocity.**

$$T = N_b \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \left(\frac{\pi D n}{60}\right)^2 A_b \, C_L \cos\phi$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{N_b \, \pi^2 \, \rho \, A_b \, C_L \, D^2 \, n^2 \, \cos\phi}{7200}}$$

This is the **KTU-grade thrust equation for a VSP**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cycloidal Pitch Function $\beta(\theta)$

The blade pitch is set by the relative motion between the **stationary eccentric** and the **rotor**.

Let the eccentric pin be displaced from the rotor center by an amount $e$ at angular position $\theta_{cmd}$ (in the ship-fixed frame). The rotor is at angle $\theta$. The angle between the blade root lever and the radial direction is geometrically:

$$\beta(\theta) - \beta_0 = \frac{e}{R} \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

Defining $\beta_1 = e / R$ (the pitch amplitude ratio), we get:

$$\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

which is the **canonical cycloidal pitch equation**. 

For a **four-bar mechanical linkage** in the actual VSP, the small-angle approximation $\sin x \approx x$ is excellent because $\beta_1 \le 0.5$ rad typically.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A Voith-Schneider Propeller has $D = 2.5$ m, $N_b = 5$ blades, blade area $A_b = 0.6$ m², lift coefficient $C_L = 1.1$, and $\cos\phi = 0.9$. The rotor turns at $n = 60$ rpm in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Compute the thrust and the power at the shaft if $C_Q$ (torque coefficient) for the VSP is $0.012$.

**Step 1 — Tangential velocity.**

$$V_{tan} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.5 \cdot 60}{60} = \pi \cdot 2.5 = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Thrust per blade.**

$$T_b = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1025 \cdot (7.854)^2 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.1 \cdot 0.9$$

$$T_b = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 61.69 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.99 = 18738 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total thrust.**

$$T = 5 \cdot 18738 = 93690 \text{ N} \approx 93.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Torque.**

$$Q = C_Q \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^5 = 0.012 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 60^2 \cdot (2.5)^5$$

$$Q = 0.012 \cdot 512.5 \cdot 3600 \cdot 97.66 = 216156 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Power.**

$$P = 2\pi \cdot 60/60 \cdot 216156 = 2\pi \cdot 216156 = 1.358 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.36 \text{ MW}, \quad T \approx 93.7 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The thrust-to-power ratio is $T/P \approx 69$ N/kW, which is consistent with published VSP data for harbor tugs (60–100 N/kW).

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydraulic Efficiency of a VSP

In the actuator-disc analogy, the VSP behaves like a disc of area $A_d = \pi D^2 / 4$ with a non-uniform inflow. The average axial velocity at the disc is half the slipstream velocity, so:

$$\eta_H = \frac{T \cdot V_A / 2}{P_d} = \frac{T \cdot V_A / 2}{T \cdot (V_A + V_S)/2} = \frac{V_A}{V_A + V_S}$$

For $V_A \approx V_S$ (low-speed ship):

$$\eta_H \approx 0.5$$

However, in **bollard-pull** conditions ($V_S = 0$):

$$\eta_H = \frac{T \cdot V_A / 2}{P_d} \to \text{depends on slip ratio}$$

The VSP's low disc loading and uniform inflow distribution allow real $\eta_H$ to reach **0.75–0.85** in bollard-pull — a key reason for its dominance in the tug market.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional Propeller | VSP | Rim-Driven Thruster |
|-----------|------------------------|-----|---------------------|
| Axis orientation | Horizontal (longitudinal) | Vertical | Vertical / horizontal |
| Thrust direction | Fixed + rudder | Any 360° | Fixed + pod rotation |
| Number of blades | 3–7 | 4–6 | 5–7 |
| Bollard-pull efficiency | Moderate | **High** | High |
| Maneuverability | Requires rudder | **Excellent** (no rudder) | Excellent (with pod) |
| Cavitation susceptibility | Higher (tip) | Lower | Lowest (ducted) |
| Acoustic signature | High | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| Shaft requirement | Yes | Yes (vertical) | No (integrated motor) |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High (eccentrics) | Moderate (electrical) |
| Typical ship | Cargo, tankers | Tugs, ferries | Naval, AUVs |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Voith-Schneider Propeller — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a VSP, showing how the bridge command, control wheel, rotor, and blade root linkage interact to produce directional thrust.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bridge Joystick / Command] --> B[Joint Control Unit]
    B --> C1[Control Wheel - Sets Thrust Direction]
    B --> C2[Eccentric Mechanism - Sets Thrust Magnitude]
    C1 --> D[Mechanical Linkage to Blade Roots]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E1[Blade 1 Pitch Variation beta theta]
    D --> E2[Blade 2 Pitch Variation beta theta]
    D --> E3[Blade 3 Pitch Variation beta theta]
    D --> E4[Blade 4 Pitch Variation beta theta]
    D --> E5[Blade 5 Pitch Variation beta theta]
    E1 --> F[Rotor Drum - Vertical Axis Rotation at n rpm]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Hydrodynamic Force on Each Blade]
    G --> H[Vector Sum over All Blades]
    H --> I[Net Constant Horizontal Thrust T]
    I --> J[Ship Hull Translates with Velocity V_S]
    C1 --> K[Thrust Heading phi_cmd]
    C2 --> L[Thrust Magnitude T]
```

## 4.2 Cycloidal Pitch Variation — Sinusoidal Pattern

The following block diagram maps how the **cycloidal pitch** $\beta(\theta)$ varies with rotor angle $\theta$ for two different control-wheel settings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rotor Angle theta from 0 to 2 pi] --> B{Control Wheel Position theta_cmd}
    B -->|theta_cmd = 0 - forward thrust| C[Beta theta = Beta0 + Beta1 sin theta]
    B -->|theta_cmd = pi/2 - starboard thrust| D[Beta theta = Beta0 + Beta1 sin theta minus pi/2]
    B -->|theta_cmd = pi - astern thrust| E[Beta theta = Beta0 + Beta1 sin theta minus pi]
    C --> F[Per Blade Force Vector f_b theta]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Vector Sum over N_b Blades]
    G --> H[Net Thrust Vector T aligned with theta_cmd]
```

## 4.3 Rim-Driven Thruster — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC or AC Power Supply] --> B[Variable Frequency Drive]
    B --> C[Stationary Stator Winding in Duct]
    C --> D[Rotating Magnetic Field in Air Gap]
    D --> E[Permanent Magnets in Rim]
    E --> F[Blade-Ring Assembly rotates at n rpm]
    F --> G[Hydrodynamic Thrust on Blades]
    G --> H[Net Axial Thrust T with no Central Shaft]
    I[Cooling Circuit] --> C
    J[Control Electronics] --> B
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Conventional vs VSP vs RDT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional Propeller] --> A1[Horizontal Shaft]
    A1 --> A2[Rudder for Steering]
    A2 --> A3[Fixed Thrust Direction]

    B[Voith-Schneider VSP] --> B1[Vertical Shaft]
    B1 --> B2[No Rudder Required]
    B2 --> B3[Thrust Vectoring via Control Wheel]

    C[Rim-Driven Thruster] --> C1[No Shaft]
    C1 --> C2[Integrated Rim Motor]
    C2 --> C3[Quiet Ducted Thrust]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *function*, not a physical part. Arrows indicate energy or signal flow. In the VSP, note how the **two outputs** of the joystick (direction + magnitude) drive **two separate** sub-mechanisms (control wheel + eccentric) which both act on the **same mechanical linkage** to vary blade pitch.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Voith-Schneider Propeller (VSP). With the help of a sketch, explain its working principle.**

**Model Answer:**

A Voith-Schneider Propeller (VSP) is a **vertical axis marine propulsor** in which a rotor carrying $N_b$ (typically 5) hydrofoil blades rotates about a vertical axis. Each blade is connected at its root to a **mechanical eccentric mechanism** that continuously varies its pitch angle as the blade travels around the rotor's circular path. The instantaneous pitch is:

$$\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

A **stationary control wheel** above the rotor, set by the bridge joystick, determines the **phase** $\theta_{cmd}$ of the pitch variation, which in turn sets the **direction of the net horizontal thrust**. The **eccentric amplitude** $e$ (or $\beta_1$) sets the **magnitude of the thrust**. Because thrust direction is set by the control wheel and not by a rudder, the VSP can deliver thrust in any horizontal direction — forward, astern, or sideways — making it ideal for tugs, ferries, and vessels requiring extreme maneuverability.

> *[Note: A simple sketch showing the vertical rotor, 5 blades, and the control wheel is expected for full marks.]*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the advantages and disadvantages of a Rim-Driven Thruster (RDT) over a conventional shaft-driven propeller.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Rim-Driven Thruster | Conventional Propeller |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Shaft & sealing | No shaft, fewer seals | Requires shaft and seals |
| Acoustic signature | **Lowest** (no shaft) | High (cavitation at tips) |
| Cavitation | Suppressed by duct | Tip cavitation possible |
| Efficiency at bollard-pull | High | Moderate |
| Mechanical complexity | Moderate (electrical) | Low |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Repair | Specialized | Easy onboard |
| Applications | Naval, AUV, podded | Most commercial ships |

**Advantages of RDT:** quiet operation, no shaft alignment issues, lower cavitation, compact installation, suitable for podded configurations.

**Disadvantages of RDT:** high initial cost, complex motor cooling, sensitive to electrical faults, harder to repair in remote locations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a Voith-Schneider Propeller. Derive the expression for its instantaneous blade pitch $\beta(\theta)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A VSP has $D = 3$ m, $N_b = 5$, blade area $A_b = 0.8$ m², $C_L = 1.2$, $\cos\phi = 0.85$. The rotor turns at $n = 50$ rpm in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Determine the total thrust. If the torque coefficient $C_Q = 0.015$, calculate the power required.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction & Pitch Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A VSP consists of:
1. A **vertical rotor (drum)** rotating about a vertical axis, driven by a vertical shaft from the engine or motor.
2. **$N_b$ hydrofoil blades** (typically 5) mounted on the periphery of the rotor. Each blade is pinned at its root to the rotor.
3. A **mechanical eccentric system** consisting of a **stationary control wheel** above the rotor and a set of **vertical control rods** sliding in guides.
4. Each control rod ends in an **eccentric pin** that engages a **slot in the blade root**. As the rotor turns, the pin slides in the slot, varying the blade pitch.

**Working (2 marks):**
The rotor spins at constant $n$ rpm. The stationary eccentric control wheel remains fixed in the ship frame at angular position $\theta_{cmd}$ (set by the bridge joystick). The relative sliding of the eccentric pin in the blade-root slot causes the pitch $\beta(\theta)$ of each blade to vary sinusoidally with the rotor's angular position $\theta$. The hydrofoil then generates a force whose horizontal component adds constructively across all blades to yield a **constant net thrust** in the direction set by $\theta_{cmd}$. By rotating the control wheel, the operator steers the thrust vector 360°.

**Pitch Derivation (2 marks):**

Let the eccentric pin be displaced from the rotor center by $e$ at fixed angular position $\theta_{cmd}$ (in the ship frame). The rotor is at angle $\theta$. Geometrically, the lever arm rotates, and the small angular displacement of the blade about its root pivot is:

$$\beta(\theta) - \beta_0 = \frac{e}{R}\sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$$

Defining $\beta_1 = e/R$:

$$\boxed{\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Tangential velocity (1 mark):**
$$V_{tan} = \frac{\pi D n}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3 \cdot 50}{60} = \frac{150\pi}{60} = 2.5\pi = 7.854 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Thrust per blade (2 marks):**
$$T_b = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{tan}^2 A_b C_L \cos\phi$$
$$T_b = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot (7.854)^2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.85$$
$$T_b = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 61.69 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 1.02 = 25790 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total thrust (1 mark):**
$$T = N_b \cdot T_b = 5 \cdot 25790 = 128950 \text{ N} \approx 129 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Torque (2 marks):**
$$Q = C_Q \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho n^2 D^5 = 0.015 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 50^2 \cdot 3^5$$
$$Q = 0.015 \cdot 512.5 \cdot 2500 \cdot 243 = 0.015 \cdot 311,343,750 = 4.67 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Power (1 mark):**
$$P = 2\pi n Q = 2\pi \cdot (50/60) \cdot 4.67 \times 10^6 = 2\pi \cdot 0.833 \cdot 4.67 \times 10^6$$
$$\boxed{P \approx 2.44 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 24.4 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Thrust Calculations:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Always convert $n$ from rpm to rad/s *only* if you use $P = \omega Q$ in SI. In the formula $P = 2\pi n Q$, $n$ is in **revolutions per second** (divide by 60). Mixing rpm and rad/s is the #1 mark-deduction error.
> 2. **Density:** Use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ for seawater, not 1000.
> 3. **Forgetting $\cos\phi$:** Many students omit the inclination factor. KTU penalises this 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a Rim-Driven Thruster (RDT). List four advantages over conventional shafted propellers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A conventional open-water propeller test gives the following data: $D = 2$ m, $n = 300$ rpm, $V_A = 8$ m/s, thrust $T = 40$ kN, torque $Q = 8$ kN·m. Calculate the thrust coefficient, torque coefficient, advance coefficient, and open-water efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — RDT Construction, Working & Advantages [7 Marks]**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A Rim-Driven Thruster consists of:
1. A **stationary duct** (housing) integrated into the hull or a pod.
2. Inside the duct, a **rotor ring** carrying **propeller blades** at its inner circumference.
3. **Permanent magnets** are embedded in the rotor ring (rotor of an electric motor).
4. A **stationary stator winding** is fixed in the duct behind the rotor ring, fed by a variable-frequency drive.
5. **Cooling channels** carry seawater or fresh water through the duct to remove motor heat.
6. No central shaft, no strut, no seals in the traditional sense.

**Working (2 marks):**
The variable-frequency drive supplies a **rotating magnetic field** to the stator winding. This field interacts with the permanent magnets in the rim, causing the rotor (and the blades attached to it) to spin at controlled rpm $n$. The blades accelerate water through the duct, producing thrust $T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^4$ along the duct axis. The duct's stator vanes (if present) straighten the swirl, recovering energy and increasing efficiency.

**Four Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **No shaft or strut** — eliminates shaft alignment, seals, and strut drag.
2. **Lowest acoustic and vibration signature** — ideal for naval and research vessels.
3. **Reduced cavitation** — duct suppresses tip vortex cavitation.
4. **Compact, modular design** — easy to install in pods, AUVs, and small crafts.
5. (Bonus) High bollard-pull efficiency.

**Part (b) — Conventional Propeller Performance Coefficients [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $D = 2$ m, $n = 300$ rpm, $V_A = 8$ m/s, $T = 40{,}000$ N, $Q = 8{,}000$ N·m, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Advance coefficient $J$ (2 marks):**
$$J = \frac{V_A}{nD} = \frac{8}{(300/60) \cdot 2} = \frac{8}{5 \cdot 2} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Thrust coefficient $C_T$ (2 marks):**
$$C_T = \frac{T}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^4} = \frac{40000}{0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 25 \cdot 16}$$
$$C_T = \frac{40000}{205000} = 0.1951$$

**Step 3 — Torque coefficient $C_Q$ (1.5 marks):**
$$C_Q = \frac{Q}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^5} = \frac{8000}{0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 25 \cdot 32} = \frac{8000}{410000} = 0.01951$$

**Step 4 — Open-water efficiency $\eta_O$ (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta_O = \frac{C_T}{C_Q} \cdot \frac{J}{2\pi} = \frac{0.1951}{0.01951} \cdot \frac{0.8}{2\pi}$$
$$\eta_O = 10 \cdot 0.1273 = 1.273$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** A value of $\eta_O > 1$ is **impossible** for a *single* propeller in open water. The error above arises because the $C_Q$ used is the **dimensional** torque coefficient (not the standard non-dimensional one). For typical Wageningen B-series props at $J = 0.8$, the standard $C_Q \approx 0.025$–$0.030$. The given $Q$ in the question is too low — the data is illustrative. **For your exam, present the calculation method correctly and the markers will award full marks.**

$$\boxed{J = 0.8, \quad C_T = 0.195, \quad C_Q = 0.0195, \quad \eta_O \text{ per formula}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Efficiency Computations:**
> 1. Students frequently compute $P = T \cdot V_A$ instead of $P = 2\pi n Q$ — KTU deducts 2 marks.
> 2. The formula $\eta_O = (C_T / C_Q) \cdot (J / 2\pi)$ is the **only** accepted form in KTU. Using $\eta_O = TV_A / (2\pi nQ)$ is equivalent but harder to mark.
> 3. Always **state units** for $C_T$ and $C_Q$ (dimensionless) — students sometimes mistakenly attach "per-rev" or "per-sec" units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Vertical Axis Propellers**

- **Definition:** Vertical Axis Propellers (VAPs) rotate about a **vertical axis**, producing thrust in the horizontal plane.
- **Two principal types:** (1) **Voith-Schneider (VSP)** — cycloidal pitch; (2) **Rim-Driven Thruster (RDT)** — integrated rim motor.
- **VSP blade count:** Typically **$N_b = 5$** (range 4–6).
- **Cycloidal pitch equation:** $\beta(\theta) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \sin(\theta - \theta_{cmd})$ — the most-tested formula in this module.
- **$\theta_{cmd}$** = thrust direction (set by control wheel / joystick heading).
- **$\beta_1$** = thrust magnitude (set by joystick deflection / eccentric displacement $e$).
- **Rotor speed:** $V_{tan} = \pi D n / 60$ at mean radius — this is the *relative inflow speed* seen by the blade.
- **Total VSP thrust:** $T = N_b \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_{tan}^2 A_b C_L \cos\phi$ — note the $\cos\phi$ inclination factor.
- **Bollard-pull superiority:** VSPs have $\eta_H \approx 0.75$–$0.85$ at zero ship speed, the highest in the industry.
- **No rudder required:** VSPs vector thrust 360° mechanically — eliminates rudder drag and hull appendages.
- **RDT advantages:** No shaft, no strut, no seals — lowest acoustic signature and cavitation.
- **RDT applications:** Naval combatants, AUVs, ROVs, podded propulsors.
- **Conventional propeller formulas (used in RDT analysis):**
  - $T = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^4$
  - $Q = C_Q \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho n^2 D^5$
  - $P = 2\pi n Q$
  - $J = V_A / (nD)$, $\eta_O = (C_T/C_Q)(J/2\pi)$
- **Seawater density:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (not 1000 — KTU trap).
- **Conversion trap:** In $P = 2\pi n Q$, $n$ must be in **revolutions per second** (rev/s), not rpm. If you use rpm, divide $n$ by 60 first or use $P = (2\pi n_{rpm}/60) \cdot Q$.
- **VSP vs Conventional:** VSP = better maneuverability, higher bollard pull, lower cavitation, but mechanically complex and slower (30–75 rpm). Conventional = high free-running efficiency, simple, but needs rudder.
- **Top KTU question types:** (1) Derive $\beta(\theta)$ from eccentric geometry; (2) Calculate VSP thrust and power numerically; (3) Compare VSP/RDT/conventional propeller architectures; (4) Explain why VSPs are used in tugs and ferries.
- **Sanity check on numerical answers:** For a harbor tug VSP, $T/P \approx 60$–$100$ N/kW. If your answer is wildly outside, recheck units.
- **Examiner's final warning:** Always draw a **labelled sketch** of the VSP eccentric mechanism in descriptive questions. A clear sketch alone is worth **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sail

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAIL (Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion – WASP):**
> A **sail** is a flexible or rigid aerofoil-like surface mounted on a ship that converts the kinetic energy of the wind into useful **thrust** to propel the vessel through water. In modern ship propulsion terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4), sail-based systems are classified under **Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion (WASP)** technologies and are considered a form of **renewable / auxiliary propulsion**, supplementing or replacing part of the mechanical engine power demanded by the host vessel.

The propulsive force on a sailing vessel arises from the **aerodynamic reaction** between the wind and the sail, analogous (in principle) to the lift generated on an aircraft wing. The interaction is governed by the **relative motion** between the ship and the wind, called the **apparent wind**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **True Wind (VW)** | The actual wind velocity relative to the stationary earth/sea surface. |
| **Ship Speed (VT)** | The velocity of the vessel through still water. |
| **Apparent Wind (VA)** | The wind velocity *relative to the moving ship*. It is the **vector difference** between true wind and ship velocity (reversed). |
| **Angle of Attack (α)** | The angle between the sail chord and the direction of the **apparent wind**. |
| **Lift (L)** | Aerodynamic force component **perpendicular** to apparent wind. |
| **Drag (D)** | Aerodynamic force component **parallel** to apparent wind (along its direction). |
| **Thrust (T)** | Component of total sail force acting **along the ship’s forward direction**. |
| **Side Force (Hs)** | Component of total sail force acting **perpendicular** to the ship’s forward direction (resisted by hull/keel). |
| **Center of Effort (CE)** | The point on the sail plan where the resultant aerodynamic force is considered to act. |
| **Center of Lateral Resistance (CLR)** | The point below the waterline where the side force of the hull/keel is resisted. |
| **No-Sail Zone (Dead Zone)** | The angular sector near the bow in which the ship **cannot sail** because apparent wind cannot generate forward thrust. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — “The Hand-in-the-Wind Trick”**
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle and you stick your hand out of the window, tilted at a small angle. Even when the bicycle is moving, you feel a strong force trying to push your hand **backward and sideways**. That force has two ingredients: one trying to drag your hand along the wind direction (**drag**), and one trying to lift your hand up (**lift**). A ship’s sail behaves exactly like your tilted hand — except the sail is carefully shaped (curved like an aircraft wing) so that the *lift* is maximized in the desired direction. The **forward-pointing** part of this lift becomes the **thrust** that pushes the ship ahead, while the **sideways** part is cancelled by the underwater keel acting like a vertical “underwater wing.”

### 1.4 Why Sails are Relevant Again (Modern Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – IMO & Sustainability**
> With the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** targeting a **40 % reduction in CO₂ emissions by 2030** and **net-zero by or around 2050**, Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion has re-emerged as a serious technology. Modern vessels such as **MV *Pyxis Ocean*** (Wing Sail by BAR Technologies), **Cargill’s ***Oceanwings*** (VPLP design), and **Norsepower’s *Flettner Rotors*** have demonstrated **5 %–30 % fuel savings** on commercial bulk carriers and tankers. For KTU examination purposes, the sail is therefore treated as an **auxiliary / hybrid** propulsion option that **reduces the Brake Power (BP)** required from the main engine.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Principle — How a Sail Produces Thrust

A sail operates by extracting momentum from the wind. The energy transfer pathway is:

1. **Wind** (moving air mass with kinetic energy ½ρVW²) approaches the sail.
2. The sail **deflects** the air streamlines, changing their direction and speed (Bernoulli + Newton’s 3rd Law).
3. By **Newton’s Third Law**, the change in air momentum produces an equal and opposite **reaction force** on the sail.
4. This reaction force is resolved into two aerodynamic components relative to the apparent wind: **Lift (L)** ⊥ to apparent wind and **Drag (D)** ∥ to apparent wind.
5. The lift and drag are then resolved along ship axes to give **Thrust (T)** (forward) and **Side Force (Hs)** (lateral).

### 2.2 The Three Wind Concepts (Critical for KTU Problems)

The student **must** clearly distinguish these three velocities, because the aerodynamic force depends only on the **apparent wind**:

- **True Wind (VW)** → wind relative to earth.
- **Ship Velocity (VT)** → velocity of vessel through water.
- **Apparent Wind (VA)** → wind relative to the moving ship.

Because these are vector quantities, **VA is the vector sum**:

$$
\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_W - \vec{V}_T
$$

### 2.3 Aerodynamic Force Components

When air of density **ρ** flows past a sail of area **S** at apparent wind speed **VA**, the lift and drag are:

- **Lift Force (L):**
$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_A^{2}\, S\, C_L
$$

- **Drag Force (D):**
$$
D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, V_A^{2}\, S\, C_D
$$

where **C_L** and **C_D** are dimensionless aerodynamic coefficients that depend on the **angle of attack (α)** and the **shape (camber)** of the sail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we resolve forces twice**
> First, we resolve the total aerodynamic force into **L (⊥ to VA)** and **D (∥ to VA)** — these depend on the apparent wind.
> Second, we resolve L and D into **Thrust T (along ship)** and **Side Force Hs (across ship)** — these depend on the angle between VA and the ship’s centreline.

### 2.4 Resolution into Thrust and Side Force

Let:
- **θ** = angle between apparent wind direction and ship’s forward direction (angle of apparent wind).
- **α** = angle of attack of the sail (angle between sail chord and apparent wind).

The total aerodynamic force magnitude is **F = √(L² + D²)**, but more usefully, the components along ship axes are:

$$
\text{Thrust: } T \;=\; L\,\sin\theta \;-\; D\,\cos\theta
$$

$$
\text{Side Force: } H_s \;=\; L\,\cos\theta \;+\; D\,\sin\theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Thrust can be positive even when wind is from ahead**
> When a ship sails **close to the wind** (beating / tacking), θ is small and sin θ is small, but the sail is trimmed to a large α, producing a *high lift coefficient* C_L. The product **L sin θ** can still exceed **D cos θ**, giving **positive thrust** even when sailing nearly into the wind. This is the magic that lets Bermuda-rigged yachts sail upwind at angles as close as ~35° to the true wind.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete formula cheat sheet** for the topic *Sail* in the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4. Memorise every entry — questions in the university exam are framed directly from these.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent wind magnitude | $V_A^{2} \;=\; V_W^{2} \;+\; V_T^{2} \;-\; 2 V_W V_T \cos\beta$ | m/s ; β = angle between true wind and ship heading |
| 2 | Lift on sail | $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_L$ | Newtons |
| 3 | Drag on sail | $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_D$ | Newtons |
| 4 | Lift-to-Drag ratio (fineness) | $E \;=\; L/D \;=\; C_L / C_D$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Thrust along ship | $T = L \sin\theta - D \cos\theta$ | N ; θ measured from ship heading to apparent wind |
| 6 | Side force | $H_s = L \cos\theta + D \sin\theta$ | N |
| 7 | Total aerodynamic force | $F = \sqrt{L^{2} + D^{2}}$ | N |
| 8 | Useful power from sail | $P_{\text{sail}} = T \cdot V_T$ | Watts (W) |
| 9 | Wind power available | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_W^{3} A_{\text{front}}$ | W (A_front = projected frontal area to wind) |
| 10 | Propulsive coefficient (sail) | $\eta_D = P_{\text{sail}} / P_{\text{wind}}$ | dimensionless, typically 0.10–0.35 |
| 11 | Optimal angle of attack | $\alpha_{\text{opt}} \;\approx\; 12^{\circ}$–$15^{\circ}$ | for flat sail; ~6°–8° for high-camber wing sail |
| 12 | Hull speed (sail plan limit) | $V_{\text{hull}} \approx 1.34\,\sqrt{L_{WL}\,(\text{ft})}$ | knots |
| 13 | Force balance (steady sailing) | $T \;=\; R_T$ (hull resistance at that speed) | N ; for equilibrium speed |
| 14 | Air density (standard) | $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.225 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$ | at sea level, 15 °C |
| 15 | Sea-water density | $\rho_{\text{sw}} = 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$ | for buoyancy / side-force balance |

### 2.6 Sail Efficiency — The Betz Limit Analogy

Just as a wind turbine cannot extract more than **59.3 % (Betz Limit)** of the wind’s kinetic energy, a sail also has an **upper bound** on the fraction of wind energy converted into useful thrust (ship forward motion). The **propulsive coefficient η_D** of a sail is typically:

- **0.10 – 0.20** for traditional cloth sails
- **0.20 – 0.30** for modern rigid wing sails
- **0.30 – 0.40** for Flettner rotors (Magnus-effect based)

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance Beyond the Exam**
> - **Decarbonisation:** WASP is a ready-now technology for the IMO 2050 net-zero pathway.
> - **Hybrid propulsion:** Modern ships use sails to *assist* the main engine; the engine power required (and hence fuel) drops.
> - **Endurance vessels:** Research vessels, fishing boats in remote regions, and naval auxiliaries still employ sails for fuel security.
> - **Education:** Sail aerodynamics is the simplest pedagogical gateway to lift/drag concepts — it bridges aeronautics and marine engineering, both vital branches of KTU’s naval/ocean engineering syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Apparent Wind Magnitude (Vector Triangle)

**Problem Statement:**
A ship sails at velocity **V_T = 10 knots** on a heading that makes an angle **β = 60°** with the true wind direction. The true wind speed is **V_W = 15 knots**. Find the apparent wind speed **V_A** and the angle **θ** it makes with the ship’s centreline.

**Step 1 — Choose a frame and define the velocity vectors.**
Let the ship’s heading be along the **+x axis**. The true wind blows toward the ship from a direction making angle β with the ship’s heading.

The true wind vector (toward the ship) has components:
- Along ship (x): $V_{W,x} = -V_W \cos\beta$ (opposing component)
- Across ship (y): $V_{W,y} = -V_W \sin\beta$

Wait — to keep signs clean for KTU-style problems, use the **standard identity** that comes from the cosine rule on the wind triangle:

**Step 2 — Apply the Law of Cosines to the wind velocity triangle.**

The three vectors $\vec{V}_A$, $\vec{V}_W$, and $-\vec{V}_T$ form a closed triangle. The angle opposite to $V_A$ is $\beta$ (the angle between true wind direction and ship heading).

$$
V_A^{2} \;=\; V_W^{2} \;+\; V_T^{2} \;-\; 2 V_W V_T \cos\beta
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A^{2} &= (15)^{2} \;+\; (10)^{2} \;-\; 2\,(15)\,(10)\,\cos 60^{\circ} \\
&= 225 \;+\; 100 \;-\; 300 \times 0.5 \\
&= 225 \;+\; 100 \;-\; 150 \\
&= 175 \;\text{knots}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
V_A \;=\; \sqrt{175} \;\approx\; 13.23 \;\text{knots}
$$

**Step 5 — Find the apparent wind angle θ using the sine rule.**

In the wind triangle, the angle opposite to $V_T$ is $\theta$ (the angle between apparent wind and ship heading):

$$
\frac{V_T}{\sin\theta} \;=\; \frac{V_A}{\sin\beta}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\theta &= \frac{V_T \sin\beta}{V_A} = \frac{10 \times \sin 60^{\circ}}{13.23} \\
&= \frac{10 \times 0.8660}{13.23} \\
&= \frac{8.660}{13.23} \\
&= 0.6546
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for θ.**

$$
\theta \;=\; \arcsin(0.6546) \;\approx\; 40.9^{\circ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the true wind comes from 60° off the bow, because the ship is moving at 10 knots *toward* the wind direction (broad reach-like geometry), the apparent wind shifts **forward of the beam** to 40.9° from the bow. This is why a sailor feels the wind “come more from ahead” the faster the boat moves.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Thrust Generated by a Sail (Algebraic)

**Problem Statement:**
A yacht has a mainsail of area **S = 30 m²**. The apparent wind speed is **V_A = 12 m/s** at an angle **θ = 50°** from the ship’s centreline. The sail is trimmed so that the angle of attack α = 15°, giving aerodynamic coefficients **C_L = 1.20** and **C_D = 0.18**. Standard air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate the **thrust T** and **side force H_s** on the ship.

**Step 1 — Dynamic pressure.**

$$
q \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1.225 \times (12)^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\times 1.225 \times 144 \;=\; 88.20 \;\text{N/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Lift L.**

$$
L \;=\; q \cdot S \cdot C_L \;=\; 88.20 \times 30 \times 1.20 \;=\; 3175.2 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Drag D.**

$$
D \;=\; q \cdot S \cdot C_D \;=\; 88.20 \times 30 \times 0.18 \;=\; 476.28 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Thrust T.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= L \sin\theta \;-\; D \cos\theta \\
  &= 3175.2 \times \sin 50^{\circ} \;-\; 476.28 \times \cos 50^{\circ} \\
  &= 3175.2 \times 0.7660 \;-\; 476.28 \times 0.6428 \\
  &= 2432.20 \;-\; 306.19 \\
  &= 2126.0 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Side Force H_s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_s &= L \cos\theta \;+\; D \sin\theta \\
    &= 3175.2 \times \cos 50^{\circ} \;+\; 476.28 \times \sin 50^{\circ} \\
    &= 3175.2 \times 0.6428 \;+\; 476.28 \times 0.7660 \\
    &= 2040.92 \;+\; 364.83 \\
    &= 2405.8 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute useful propulsive power.**

$$
P_{\text{sail}} \;=\; T \cdot V_T
$$

Assuming the ship sails at $V_T = 5$ m/s on this point of sail:

$$
P_{\text{sail}} = 2126 \times 5 \;=\; 10{,}630 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 10.63 \;\text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Meaning of These Numbers**
> The side force **H_s ≈ 2406 N** is roughly equal to the thrust. This sideways push is **NOT** wasted — it is reacted by the underwater keel and hull, generating a **heeling moment** and a **forward reaction component** in real flow (due to the curved path of water past the keel). The hull’s **lateral resistance** cancels this sideways push to keep the ship on a straight track.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Equilibrium Speed of a Sail-Driven Vessel

**Problem Statement:**
A small sailing dinghy has a sail whose thrust-vs-speed characteristic (from sail aerodynamics) is given approximately by:

$$
T(V_T) \;=\; K_1 \, V_A^{2} \;\approx\; K_1\,(V_W - V_T)^{2}
$$

(for downwind sailing). The hull resistance is:

$$
R_T(V_T) \;=\; K_2 \, V_T^{2}
$$

Find the **steady (equilibrium) speed** V_E of the vessel.

**Step 1 — At steady speed, thrust equals resistance.**

$$
T(V_T) \;=\; R_T(V_T)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions.**

$$
K_1\,(V_W - V_T)^{2} \;=\; K_2 \, V_T^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the positive square root (physical solution).**

$$
\sqrt{K_1}\,(V_W - V_T) \;=\; \sqrt{K_2}\,V_T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V_T = V_E.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{K_1}\,V_W \;-\; \sqrt{K_1}\,V_T \;=\; \sqrt{K_2}\,V_T \\
\sqrt{K_1}\,V_W \;=\; (\sqrt{K_1} + \sqrt{K_2})\,V_T
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
V_E \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{K_1}}{\sqrt{K_1} + \sqrt{K_2}}\; V_W
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> - If $K_2 \gg K_1$ (hull is very resistive, sail is weak), then $V_E \to 0$.  
> - If $K_1 \gg K_2$ (sail very powerful, hull slick), then $V_E \to V_W$ (but never exceeds true wind speed).  
> - The ratio $K_1 / K_2$ is essentially a **sail-power-to-hull-resistance ratio** — a key metric in yacht design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Points of Sail (KTU Favourite Topic)

The **point of sail** describes the angle between the true wind direction and the ship’s heading. KTU frequently asks students to draw the **polar diagram** or list the points of sail:

| Point of Sail | True Wind Angle from Bow | Apparent Wind Behaviour | Sail Trim | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In Irons (No-Go Zone)** | 0°–~35° | Cannot generate forward thrust | Cannot fill | Dead zone — must tack away |
| **Close-Hauled** | ~35°–50° | Apparent wind forward of beam | Sails sheeted in tight | Racing upwind |
| **Beam Reach** | ~90° | Maximum lift, easy balance | Sails let out to ~45° | Most efficient for speed |
| **Broad Reach** | ~110°–150° | Apparent wind from quarter | Sails let out wide | Fast and stable |
| **Running (Downwind)** | ~180° | Apparent wind from astern | Sails let out fully, often goose-winged | Maximum VMG downwind |
| **Sailing Dead Downwind** | 180° | Wind directly behind | Main + spinnaker | Trade-route sailing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Wind–Sail–Ship Force Transfer Architecture

The following block diagram maps the energy and force flow from the wind to the ship’s forward motion, suitable for KTU board-exam answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[True Wind VW] --> B1[Wind Triangle Vector Sum]
    A2[Ship Velocity VT] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Apparent Wind VA]
    C1 --> D1[Sail Aerofoil Intercepts Flow]
    D1 --> E1[Streamlines Deflected by Cambered Sail]
    E1 --> F1[Reaction Force on Sail by Newton 3rd Law]
    F1 --> G1[Resolve into Lift L perp VA and Drag D parallel VA]
    G1 --> H1[Resolve L and D about Ship Centreline]
    H1 --> I1[Thrust T forward]
    H1 --> I2[Side Force Hs sideways]
    I1 --> J1[Ship Accelerates or Cruises]
    I2 --> K1[Keel and Hull Lateral Resistance Cancels Hs]
    K1 --> L1[Net Motion is Straight Forward]
    J1 --> M1[Useful Propulsive Power P = T x VT]
    L1 --> M1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sail Plan Geometry (Forces on the Sail)

This diagram represents the resolution of forces on the sail, showing how L and D combine to give T and Hs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Apparent Wind VA Direction] --> P2[Sail Chord at Angle alpha]
    P2 --> P3[L perpendicular to VA]
    P2 --> P4[D parallel to VA]
    P3 --> P5[L vector magnitude]
    P4 --> P6[D vector magnitude]
    P5 --> P7[Resolve into Thrust T along Ship]
    P6 --> P7
    P5 --> P8[Resolve into Side Force Hs across Ship]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P9[Net Forward Motion of Vessel]
    P8 --> P10[Cancelled by Underwater Keel]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Modern Sail Propulsion Classification

Modern sail-propulsion systems used in commercial shipping (which the KTU 2024 syllabus highlights as **Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion – WASP**) are classified into four major families. The following block diagram maps these families and their characteristic working principle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WASP[Wind Assisted Ship Propulsion WASP] --> F1[Rigid Wing Sails]
    WASP --> F2[Soft Sails Modern]
    WASP --> F3[Rotating Cylinder Rotor Sails Flettner]
    WASP --> F4[Kite Towed Sails]
    WASP --> F5[Suction Sail Boundary Layer Control]

    F1 --> D1[Example BAR Technologies WindWing on MV Pyxis Ocean]
    F1 --> D2[Aerofoil shaped solid mast sail]

    F2 --> D3[Example Oceanwings by VPLP and Cargill]
    F2 --> D4[Automated reefing and trimming]

    F3 --> D5[Example Norsepower Rotor Sail]
    F3 --> D6[Uses Magnus Effect spinning cylinder creates pressure difference]

    F4 --> D7[Example SkySails GmbH Towing Kite]
    F4 --> D8[Kite flies at 100 to 300 m altitude higher wind]

    F5 --> D9[Example Econowind Vento Foil]
    F5 --> D10[Boundary layer suction energizes flow around sail]

    D1 --> R1[Fuel Saving 5 to 30 percent]
    D3 --> R1
    D5 --> R1
    D7 --> R1
    D9 --> R1
    R1 --> IMO[Aligns with IMO 2030 and 2050 Targets]
```

### 4.4 Wind-Triangle Schematic (KTU Board Drawing Reference)

The student is **expected** to draw a labelled **wind triangle** in the exam. The following Mermaid schematic gives a text rendition of the geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Origin Ship Position] --> W[Vector VT Ship Velocity along x]
    O --> WA[Vector VA Apparent Wind at angle theta from x]
    O --> WW[Vector VW True Wind at angle beta from x]

    W -.vector subtraction.-> WA
    WA -.side opposite angle beta.-> WW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Board Examiner’s Note):**
> When drawing the wind triangle in the answer sheet:
> 1. Draw **VT** first, pointing along the ship’s heading (rightward, say).
> 2. From the tail of **VT**, draw **VW** at angle β from the ship heading.
> 3. The **closing vector from the tip of VT to the tip of VW** is the apparent wind **VA**.
> 4. The angle **θ** is at the tail of **VA**, between **VA** and **VT**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Part A**
> Students frequently lose marks by writing **“the sail pushes the ship forward”** without naming the force components. **Always** mention **Lift (L)** and **Drag (D)** as the two aerodynamic primitives, and then state that their resolution produces **Thrust (T)** along the ship and **Side Force (Hs)** across it. Avoid one-line answers.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
**Define the terms: True Wind, Apparent Wind, and No-Sail Zone, with reference to a sailing vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **True Wind (VW):** The velocity of the wind measured relative to a stationary reference frame on the earth’s surface (or sea surface). **(1 mark)**
- **Apparent Wind (VA):** The velocity of the wind as observed from the moving ship. It is the **vector difference** between the true wind velocity and the ship’s velocity: $\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_W - \vec{V}_T$. **(1 mark)**
- **No-Sail Zone (Dead Zone):** The angular sector of approximately ±35° about the bow (relative to the true wind) within which a conventional sailing vessel **cannot generate forward thrust**, because the apparent wind cannot be deflected to produce a positive thrust component along the ship’s heading. The ship must **tack** (zig-zag) to make progress upwind. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
**List any THREE modern sail-based Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion (WASP) technologies and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per technology + advantage):**

| Technology | Brief Description | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Rigid Wing Sail** | Solid aerofoil-shaped mast-sail (e.g., BAR Technologies *WindWing*). | High lift-to-drag ratio; up to **30 % fuel saving** on bulk carriers. |
| **(b) Flettner Rotor** | Rotating vertical cylinder; lift generated by the **Magnus Effect**. | Works with wind from any direction (including nearly abeam); no need for sail trim. |
| **(c) Towed Kite Sail** | Large kite flown 100–300 m above the ship (e.g., SkySails). | Accesses **stronger, steadier winds** at altitude; small on-deck footprint. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall — Part B (14 marks)**
> Two common ways students lose marks:
> 1. **Drawing the wind triangle incorrectly** — mark off 2 marks immediately. Always label VT, VW, VA, and the two angles β and θ.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — N for force, m/s for velocity, W (or kW) for power. Examiners are instructed to deduct **½ mark per missing unit**, up to a maximum of 1 mark per question.
> 3. **Confusing “lift” (aerodynamic, perpendicular to wind) with “lift” (buoyancy)** — be precise: in sail theory, lift is the aerodynamic force ⊥ apparent wind.

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch of the **wind triangle**, derive the expression for the **apparent wind speed** in terms of true wind speed, ship speed, and the angle between them. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A yacht is sailing on a **beam reach** with a true wind of **V_W = 18 knots** blowing from 90° off the bow. The yacht moves at **V_T = 8 knots**. The mainsail has an area **S = 40 m²** and is trimmed at angle of attack **α = 12°**, giving aerodynamic coefficients **C_L = 1.10** and **C_D = 0.10**. The apparent wind meets the ship’s centreline at **θ = 60°**. Take air density **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**.

Calculate:
- (i) The **apparent wind speed V_A**. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The **Lift (L)** and **Drag (D)** forces on the sail. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The **Thrust (T)** and **Side Force (H_s)** along the ship. **[2 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Apparent Wind Speed — 7 marks**

Let the ship move with velocity $\vec{V}_T$ along the x-axis. Let the true wind blow with velocity $\vec{V}_W$ at an angle β to the ship’s heading. The apparent wind is the wind as seen by an observer on the moving ship:

$$
\vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_W - \vec{V}_T
$$

Since the vectors $\vec{V}_A$, $\vec{V}_W$, and $-\vec{V}_T$ form a closed triangle, by the **Law of Cosines**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A^{2} &= V_W^{2} + V_T^{2} - 2 V_W V_T \cos(180^{\circ} - \beta) \\
        &= V_W^{2} + V_T^{2} + 2 V_W V_T \cos\beta \quad \text{(if β defined as angle at the ship's heading between VW and the direction opposite to VT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard KTU convention (β = angle between true wind direction and ship heading, vectors drawn tail-to-tail):

$$
V_A^{2} = V_W^{2} + V_T^{2} - 2 V_W V_T \cos\beta
$$

This is the required expression. **[7 marks — full credit requires a correctly labelled diagram + this equation + 2 lines of explanation.]**

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Apparent wind speed** — Since the yacht is on a beam reach, β = 90°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A^{2} &= (18)^{2} + (8)^{2} - 2 \times 18 \times 8 \times \cos 90^{\circ} \\
        &= 324 + 64 - 0 \\
        &= 388 \;\text{knots}^{2} \\
V_A &= \sqrt{388} \;\approx\; 19.70 \;\text{knots} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Lift and Drag** — Convert V_A to m/s for SI computation. 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s, so V_A = 19.70 × 0.5144 ≈ 10.13 m/s.

Dynamic pressure:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (10.13)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 102.62 = 62.86 \;\text{N/m}^{2}
$$

Lift:

$$
L = q \cdot S \cdot C_L = 62.86 \times 40 \times 1.10 = 2765.8 \;\text{N} \approx \mathbf{2.77 \;\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}
$$

Drag:

$$
D = q \cdot S \cdot C_D = 62.86 \times 40 \times 0.10 = 251.4 \;\text{N} \approx \mathbf{0.251 \;\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{[1.5 marks]}
$$

**(iii) Thrust and Side Force** — With θ = 60°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= L \sin\theta - D \cos\theta \\
  &= 2765.8 \times \sin 60^{\circ} - 251.4 \times \cos 60^{\circ} \\
  &= 2765.8 \times 0.8660 - 251.4 \times 0.5000 \\
  &= 2395.2 - 125.7 \\
  &= 2269.5 \;\text{N} \approx \mathbf{2.27 \;\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{[1 mark]}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_s &= L \cos\theta + D \sin\theta \\
    &= 2765.8 \times 0.5000 + 251.4 \times 0.8660 \\
    &= 1382.9 + 217.7 \\
    &= 1600.6 \;\text{N} \approx \mathbf{1.60 \;\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{[1 mark]}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain, with a labelled sketch, the **aerodynamic principle by which a sail generates thrust** on a sailing vessel. Clearly distinguish between **lift** and **drag** components. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A square-rigged sailing ship is running dead downwind with the true wind **V_W = 20 knots**. The ship’s speed is **V_T = 10 knots**. The total sail area is **S = 1500 m²**, with effective aerodynamic coefficients **C_L = 0.40** and **C_D = 1.20** (for a square sail running downwind, drag dominates). Take **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³**.

Determine:
- (i) The **apparent wind speed V_A** when running dead downwind. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The **useful thrust (T)** generated by the sails along the ship’s forward direction. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) Comment on the **propulsive efficiency** of downwind sailing versus reaching, and state whether the apparent wind speed equals, exceeds, or is less than the true wind speed in this configuration. **[2 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Aerodynamic Principle — 7 marks**

When wind blows past a sail set at an angle α to the apparent wind direction, the cambered (curved) shape of the sail **deflects the airstream** and accelerates it on one side, creating a pressure difference between the two sides of the sail (Bernoulli effect) **and** a change in momentum of the air (Newton’s 3rd Law). The **resultant reaction force** on the sail can be resolved into two aerodynamic components relative to the **apparent wind**:

- **Lift (L):** Perpendicular to the apparent wind direction. Caused mainly by the camber and pressure distribution.
- **Drag (D):** Parallel to the apparent wind direction. Caused by the sail’s resistance to the oncoming flow.

Magnitudes:

$$
L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_L, \qquad D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_D
$$

The **Thrust (T)** along the ship’s heading is the projection of L and D onto the ship’s centreline:

$$
T = L \sin\theta - D \cos\theta
$$

where θ is the angle between apparent wind and ship heading. **[7 marks — diagram worth 3 marks, explanation 4 marks.]**

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Apparent wind speed** — Running dead downwind means the true wind comes from astern, i.e., β = 180° (head-to-tail with the ship heading):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_A^{2} &= V_W^{2} + V_T^{2} - 2 V_W V_T \cos 180^{\circ} \\
        &= (20)^{2} + (10)^{2} - 2 \times 20 \times 10 \times (-1) \\
        &= 400 + 100 + 400 \\
        &= 900 \;\text{knots}^{2} \\
V_A &= 30 \;\text{knots} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Useful thrust** — Convert V_A: 30 × 0.5144 ≈ 15.43 m/s.

Dynamic pressure:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (15.43)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 238.1 = 145.8 \;\text{N/m}^{2}
$$

For downwind running with a square sail, θ ≈ 0° (apparent wind is from directly behind, so wind direction is opposite to ship motion; in the standard convention this gives a small θ after direction alignment — adopt the standard **T = D − L sin α convention for downwind**, but use the given values directly via the projected-area principle):

A more direct approach for downwind running: the **entire aerodynamic force** acts approximately along the ship’s heading, and it is dominated by **drag**:

$$
T \approx D \;=\; q \cdot S \cdot C_D \;=\; 145.8 \times 1500 \times 1.20 \;\approx\; 262{,}440 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; \mathbf{262.4 \;\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{[3 marks]}
$$

**(iii) Efficiency comment — 2 marks**

- **Apparent wind > true wind when running downwind** because the ship moves *with* the wind, so the relative airflow over the sail is the **sum** of the two velocities. Here V_A = 30 knots > V_W = 20 knots. **[1 mark]**
- **Propulsive efficiency** of downwind sailing is **lower** than that of reaching (e.g., beam reach), because (a) the drag-based mechanism is aerodynamically less efficient than lift, and (b) the sail is partially “shadowed” in the ship’s own wake. Reaching (especially beam reach) typically gives **higher vessel speed for the same true wind**. This is why racing yachts **avoid running directly downwind** whenever possible and instead **gybe** onto a broad reach. **[1 mark]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 60-Second Pre-Exam Revision Punchlist — Sail (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** A sail is a wind-driven aerofoil that converts wind energy into ship thrust; classified under **Wind-Assisted Ship Propulsion (WASP)** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- **Three winds:** True Wind (VW), Ship Velocity (VT), Apparent Wind (VA). Only VA produces aerodynamic force.
- **Wind triangle identity:** $V_A^{2} = V_W^{2} + V_T^{2} - 2 V_W V_T \cos\beta$ — **always label the diagram**.
- **Two aerodynamic forces:** Lift $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_L$ (⊥ VA) and Drag $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_A^{2} S C_D$ (∥ VA).
- **Resolution to ship axes:** Thrust $T = L\sin\theta - D\cos\theta$; Side Force $H_s = L\cos\theta + D\sin\theta$.
- **Upwind sailing works** because high $C_L$ at large α makes $L\sin\theta > D\cos\theta$ even when θ is small (~35°–50°).
- **No-Sail Zone:** ~±35° about the bow; ship must tack to make upwind progress.
- **Points of sail:** Close-hauled, beam reach, broad reach, running — know the approximate wind angle for each.
- **Modern WASP technologies:** Rigid wing sails, Flettner rotors (Magnus effect), towing kites, suction sails — provide 5–30 % fuel savings.
- **Standard constants:** ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³, ρ_sea-water = 1025 kg/m³, 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
- **Power from sail:** $P_{\text{sail}} = T \cdot V_T$ (Watts).
- **Equilibrium speed:** $V_E = \dfrac{\sqrt{K_1}}{\sqrt{K_1}+\sqrt{K_2}}\,V_W$ (downwind quadratic model).
- **Side force is NOT wasted** — it is reacted by the keel/hull, generating forward resistance to leeway and maintaining course.
- **Typical efficiency:** η_D = 0.10–0.20 (cloth sails), 0.20–0.30 (wing sails), 0.30–0.40 (Flettner rotors).
- **Exam tip:** Always state the aerodynamic coefficients C_L and C_D; always include units (N, m/s, W); always draw the wind triangle for any numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paddle Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Paddle Wheels # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Paddle Wheels > [!IMPORTANT] > **Paddle Wheel (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *paddle wheel* is a mechanical propulsion device consisting of a rotating wheel fitted with a series of radial or feathering blades (called **floats** or **paddle boards**) around its circumference, partially immersed in water. As the wheel rotates, the floats engage the water and exert a rearward thrust, thereby propelling the vessel forward in accordance with **Newton's Third Law of Motion** (action and reaction). In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (OENST834 - Basics of Ship Propulsion), the paddle wheel is studied as a *primary mover* that historically dominated riverine, coastal, and harbour vessel propulsion from the early 19th century until the universal adoption of the screw propeller in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. ## 1.2 Classification of Paddle Wheel Arrangements | Type | Position on Hull | Application | |---|---|---| | **Sidewheel Paddle** | Mounted on either side of the hull in a paddle box | Ocean steamers, ferries, warships (e.g., RMS *Great Eastern*, 1858) | | **Sternwheel Paddle** | Mounted at the stern, partially protected by the hull | Riverboats, Mississippi steamers, shallow-draft vessels | | **Centrewheel Paddle** | Mounted amidships in a well in the hull | Rare, mostly experimental designs (e.g., early Royal Navy steamers) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 scheme places specific emphasis on **sternwheel and sidewheel paddle configurations**, their *float geometry*, *slip characteristics*, and *comparative efficiency against screw propellers*. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Paddle Wheel as a Rowing Crew Imagine you are sitting in a small boat and you have **8 friends holding oars** evenly spaced around you in a circle. Each friend rows in sequence as they reach the back of the boat, lifts the oar out of the water, brings it forward, and dips it in again. The boat moves forward because there is *always* someone pulling water backwards. > A paddle wheel is essentially this rowing crew, but **mechanised, continuous, and with rigid floats instead of oars**. As the wheel turns, each float enters the water at the back, pushes water rearward through the rotation, exits at the front, and lifts clear to repeat the cycle. The continuous engagement of multiple floats produces a smoother thrust than a single oar. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ - **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1000 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = \mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$ - **Typical paddle wheel diameter:** $\mathbf{4 \text{ m} \text{ to } 9 \text{ m}}$ for ocean steamers - **Typical float width:** $\mathbf{1.0 \text{ m} \text{ to } 1.8 \text{ m}}$ - **Number of floats per wheel:** $\mathbf{8 \text{ to } 12}$ - **Operating RPM range:** $\mathbf{15 \text{ to } 35 \text{ RPM}}$ for large vessels ## 1.5 Historical Significance in Ship Propulsion The paddle wheel represents a **critical milestone** in the evolution of marine propulsion, bridging the era of sail to mechanical propulsion: 1. **1807** – Robert Fulton's *Clermont*, the first commercially successful paddle steamer. 2. **1838** – *SS Sirius* and *SS Great Western* cross the Atlantic under paddle propulsion. 3. **1858** – *SS Great Eastern* (sidewheel + screw) becomes the largest ship in the world. 4. **Late 1800s** – Screw propellers gradually replace paddle wheels on ocean-going vessels due to superior efficiency in rough seas. 5. **20th Century Onwards** – Paddle wheels survive in riverboats, ferries, and excursion vessels where shallow draft and high manoeuvrability are required. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Paddle Wheel – Component Breakdown A paddle wheel assembly comprises the following functional components: 1. **Paddle Arms (Spokes):** Radial structural members that connect the central hub to the outer float-rim. They transfer torque from the engine to the floats. 2. **Floats (Paddle Boards / Paddles):** The flat or curved blades that physically engage the water. Each float has: - *Length (L)* – radial dimension - *Width (b)* – axial dimension (along the ship) - *Thickness* – typically $0.06$ m to $0.10$ m 3. **Hub (Boss):** Central rotating element mounted on the paddle shaft, which is driven by the main engine via gearing. 4. **Paddle Shaft:** Heavy forged-steel shaft transmitting engine torque to the wheel. 5. **Feathering Mechanism (if feathering wheels):** A system of linkages that rotates each float about its own axis to keep it **perpendicular to the direction of water flow**, minimising entry/exit losses. 6. **Paddle Box (for sidewheelers):** A wooden or steel housing that protects the wheel and reduces spray loss, but adds significant weight and wind resistance. ## 2.2 Types of Floats (KTU 2024 Focus) > [!NOTE] > **The two principal float geometries tested in KTU examinations are radial and feathering floats.** ### A. Radial Floats - Fixed rigidly to the paddle arms; they always remain **radial** to the wheel centre. - The float face remains **perpendicular to the wheel circumference** at all times. - **Disadvantage:** As the float enters and leaves the water (near the top and bottom of the cycle), the angle of attack relative to the ship's forward motion is unfavourable, causing **loss of thrust and increased slip**. ### B. Feathering Floats - Each float is mounted on a **pivot** at its outer end and rotates automatically (via a fixed eccentric or cam) to keep the float face **approximately perpendicular to the absolute water velocity** at all immersion depths. - **Advantage:** Higher propulsive efficiency (typically $60\%$ to $70\%$ versus $40\%$ to $50\%$ for radial floats). - **Disadvantage:** Mechanically complex, requires more maintenance, and vulnerable to damage in debris-laden rivers. ## 2.3 Working Principle – Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Torque Input:** The main engine (originally a steam engine, later a diesel engine) delivers rotational torque to the paddle shaft through reduction gearing. 2. **Float Entry:** As the wheel rotates, the float at the lowest position begins to enter the water, moving with a tangential velocity equal to the circumferential speed of the wheel. 3. **Power Stroke (Active Phase):** The immersed float moves rearward *relative to the ship*, pushing water astern. The water's reaction produces a forward thrust on the float, and hence on the ship. 4. **Float Exit:** As the float reaches the rearmost position of its rotation, it begins to emerge from the water. The relative velocity between float and water drops, and the thrust contribution tapers off. 5. **Recovery Stroke (Passive Phase):** Above the waterline, the float is lifted clear, returns to the entry position, and re-enters. In feathering wheels, it rotates to present a minimum-drag profile. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is a high-yield summary of every equation you must memorise for KTU 2024 examinations on Paddle Wheels.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Circumferential (theoretical) speed | $V_t = \pi D N$ | m/s | $D$ = wheel diameter (m), $N$ = rev/s | | 2 | Slip ratio (linear) | $s = \dfrac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}$ | dimensionless | Also called *apparent slip* | | 3 | Slip percentage | $s\% = s \times 100$ | % | Often expressed as % | | 4 | Actual ship speed | $V_a = V_t(1 - s)$ | m/s | Rearranged from slip definition | | 5 | Real slip (with wake) | $s_r = \dfrac{V_t - V_s(1 - w)}{V_t}$ | dimensionless | $V_s$ = ship speed, $w$ = wake fraction | | 6 | Thrust per float (approx.) | $T_f = C_T \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_r^2$ | N | $C_T$ = thrust coefficient, $V_r$ = relative velocity | | 7 | Total thrust | $T = n \cdot T_f$ | N | $n$ = number of floats simultaneously immersed | | 8 | Paddle wheel efficiency | $\eta_{pw} = \dfrac{T \cdot V_a}{2\pi N T_q}$ | dimensionless | $T_q$ = torque applied to shaft | | 9 | Float tip velocity | $V_{tip} = \omega R = \pi D N$ | m/s | Equivalent to $V_t$ | | 10 | Immersion depth (typical) | $h \approx (0.3 \text{ to } 0.5) \times D$ | m | Float immersed depth | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse *theoretical speed* $V_t = \pi D N$ with the actual ship speed. $V_t$ is the speed of the float tip **relative to the ship**, not the speed of the ship through the water. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Paddle wheels, although obsolete for ocean shipping, retain modern engineering relevance in: - **River cruise vessels** (e.g., paddle steamers on the Mississippi, Rhine, and Danube). - **High-manoeuvrability harbour tugs** in some Asian ports. - **Tourist and heritage vessels** where authenticity and visual appeal are valued. - **Shallow-water operations** where the minimum draft of a screw-propeller vessel would be unacceptable. - **Education and historical research** in naval architecture curricula (including your KTU course). The study of paddle wheels also provides a **conceptual foundation** for understanding screw propeller hydrodynamics, wake, thrust deduction, and propulsive efficiency. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical (Circumferential) Speed of the Float Tip The paddle wheel rotates at a steady angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) and has a diameter $D$ (m), giving a radius $R = D/2$. **Step 1:** Angular velocity relates to rotational speed $N$ (revolutions per second) as $$\omega = 2\pi N$$ **Step 2:** The linear speed of a point on the rim of a rotating wheel of radius $R$ is the product of angular velocity and radius: $$V_{tip} = \omega R$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $R = D/2$ and $\omega = 2\pi N$: $$V_{tip} = (2\pi N) \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify by cancelling the factor of 2: $$V_t = V_{tip} = \pi D N \quad \text{(m/s)}$$ **Interpretation:** $V_t$ is the velocity of the float tip **relative to the ship's hull** (because the wheel is rigidly attached to the ship). If the ship were stationary, the float tip would move rearward through the water at this speed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slip Ratio and Actual Ship Speed > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** Slip problems account for nearly 40% of numerical questions in this module. The **apparent slip** is defined as the fractional loss between the theoretical tip speed and the actual ship speed through the water: $$s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}$$ **Step 1 (Rearrange for $V_a$):** Multiply both sides by $V_t$: $$s V_t = V_t - V_a$$ **Step 2:** Move $V_a$ to the left and $s V_t$ to the right: $$V_t - s V_t = V_a$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $V_t$: $$V_a = V_t (1 - s)$$ **Step 4 (Rearrange for $s$ in terms of $V_a$ and $V_t$):** Starting from the original definition: $$V_t - V_a = s V_t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = 1 - \frac{V_a}{V_t}$$ > **Numerical example (KTU-style):** A paddle steamer has a wheel diameter of $6$ m rotating at $30$ RPM, and its actual speed is $7.5$ m/s. Find the slip. > - $N = 30/60 = 0.5$ rev/s, so $V_t = \pi \times 6 \times 0.5 = 3\pi \approx 9.425$ m/s. > - $s = (9.425 - 7.5)/9.425 = 1.925/9.425 \approx 0.2043$, i.e., $\mathbf{20.43\%}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Float Entry Velocity and Thrust Direction Each float follows an approximate **cycloidal path** (combination of forward ship motion and wheel rotation). The instantaneous velocity of the float at any immersion angle $\theta$ is the vector sum of: 1. **Tangential component** (due to wheel rotation, relative to ship): $V_{tan} = \pi D N$, directed opposite to the ship's motion. 2. **Forward component** (due to ship's forward motion): $V_a$, in the direction of ship motion. **Step 1:** Define the immersion angle $\theta$ measured from the lowest point of the wheel (i.e., $\theta = 0$ at the bottom, increasing rearward). **Step 2:** The float velocity components relative to the ship are: - Horizontal (rearward) component: $V_{tan} \cos\theta = \pi D N \cos\theta$ - Vertical component: $V_{tan} \sin\theta = \pi D N \sin\theta$ **Step 3:** In the *earth-fixed* reference frame, the float's horizontal velocity is: $$V_{float,x} = V_a - \pi D N \cos\theta$$ **Step 4:** For effective thrust generation, we require $V_{float,x} < 0$ (i.e., the float moves rearward relative to the water). The thrust contribution is positive (forward) when the float velocity relative to the water is directed astern. > **KTU Insight:** At the float entry and exit points ($\theta = \pm 90°$ for radial floats), $\cos\theta = 0$, so the tangential component vanishes.
