This is why **thrust is zero at entry and exit** – a fundamental limitation of radial paddle wheels, which feathering floats partially overcome. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Paddle Wheel Propulsive Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Outcome:** CO4 – *Apply the principles of marine propellers to estimate thrust, torque, and efficiency.* Define efficiency as the ratio of useful power output (thrust power delivered to the ship) to the power input (delivered by the engine to the paddle shaft). **Step 1:** Useful (thrust) power is the product of total thrust and ship speed: $$P_{useful} = T \cdot V_a$$ **Step 2:** Power input to the paddle shaft is the product of torque and angular velocity: $$P_{input} = 2\pi N \cdot T_q$$ where $T_q$ is the torque applied to the paddle shaft (N·m). **Step 3:** Propulsive efficiency is therefore: $$\eta_{pw} = \frac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}} = \frac{T \cdot V_a}{2\pi N \cdot T_q}$$ **Step 4 (Alternative form using slip):** For an idealised radial paddle wheel, thrust power is also expressible as: $$P_{useful} = T \cdot V_a = T \cdot V_t (1 - s)$$ **Step 5:** Substituting into the efficiency expression: $$\eta_{pw} = \frac{T \cdot V_t (1 - s)}{2\pi N T_q}$$ **Step 6:** Noting that $V_t = \pi D N$, we can rewrite: $$\eta_{pw} = \frac{T \cdot \pi D N (1 - s)}{2\pi N T_q} = \frac{T \cdot D (1 - s)}{2 T_q}$$ **Interpretation:** Higher slip $s$ reduces efficiency because more energy is wasted in churning water rather than propelling the ship. Optimal paddle wheel design aims to minimise slip while maximising thrust. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Number of Floats Simultaneously Immersed For a wheel with $Z$ total floats, evenly spaced, the fraction of the circumference submerged is approximately: $$f_{sub} = \frac{h}{D} \quad \text{(for small h) or} \quad f_{sub} = \frac{\theta_{sub}}{2\pi}$$ where $h$ is the immersion depth and $\theta_{sub}$ is the submerged arc angle. **Step 1:** The number of floats simultaneously immersed is: $$n = Z \cdot f_{sub}$$ **Step 2:** For a typical paddle wheel with $Z = 10$ floats and $h = 1.5$ m on a $D = 6$ m wheel: $$f_{sub} = \frac{1.5}{6} = 0.25 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5 \;\;\text{floats}$$ **Step 3 (Total thrust):** If each immersed float produces an average thrust $T_f$, then: $$T_{total} = n \cdot T_f = 2.5 \cdot T_f$$ > **Engineering Note:** To ensure a smooth thrust without pulsations, designers select $Z$ such that at least $2$ floats are immersed at all times, and the entry/exit of one float overlaps the steady working of others. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix – KTU Numerical Problem Patterns | Pattern | Given | Find | Core Formula | |---|---|---|---| | **A** | $D$, $N$, $V_a$ | Slip $s$ | $V_t = \pi D N$, $s = (V_t - V_a)/V_t$ | | **B** | $D$, $N$, $s$ | $V_a$ | $V_a = \pi D N (1 - s)$ | | **C** | $V_a$, $s$, $V_t$ | Wheel diameter or RPM | Rearrange $V_t = \pi D N$ | | **D** | $T$, $V_a$, $T_q$, $N$ | Efficiency | $\eta = T V_a / (2\pi N T_q)$ | | **E** | $Z$, $h$, $D$, $T_f$ | Total thrust | $n = Z h / D$, $T = n T_f$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Paddle Wheel Propulsion System > [!NOTE] > The diagram below maps the energy and signal flow from the prime mover to the float-water interaction, illustrating the **functional architecture** of a paddle wheel propulsion system as required by KTU 2024 Module 4. ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[PRIME MOVER
STEAM OR DIESEL ENGINE] GR[REDUCTION GEARING
RATIO 4 TO 10] PS[PADDLE SHAFT
FORGED STEEL] HUB[CENTRAL HUB
BOSS] ARM1[PADDLE ARM 1] ARM2[PADDLE ARM 2] ARM3[PADDLE ARM 3] ARMN[PADDLE ARM N] F1[FLOAT 1
RADIAL OR FEATHERING] F2[FLOAT 2] FN[FLOAT N] WAT[WATER INTERACTION
REARWARD MOMENTUM TRANSFER] THR[THRUST FORCE
FORWARD ON HULL] HUL[SHIP HULL DISPLACES
FORWARD THROUGH WATER] SLIP[SLIP LOSS
ENERGY DISSIPATED] SPRAY[SPRAY AND CHURNING
WAKE GENERATION] ENG -->|Torque Input| GR GR -->|Reduced RPM| PS PS -->|Rotational Drive| HUB HUB --> ARM1 HUB --> ARM2 HUB --> ARM3 HUB --> ARMN ARM1 --> F1 ARM2 --> F2 ARMN --> FN F1 --> WAT F2 --> WAT FN --> WAT WAT -->|Newton Third Law| THR THR --> HUL WAT --> SLIP WAT --> SPRAY style ENG fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style GR fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style PS fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style HUB fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 style WAT fill:#00b0f0,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style THR fill:#70ad47,stroke:#274e0a,color:#ffffff style HUL fill:#548235,stroke:#274e0a,color:#ffffff style SLIP fill:#c00000,stroke:#5a0000,color:#ffffff style SPRAY fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Float Kinematic Cycle (Cycloidal Path) The diagram below illustrates the **relative motion of a single float** through one full rotation, decomposed into four cardinal phases. This is a common KTU 2024 viva / drawing question. ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[PHASE 1: ENTRY
FLOAT ENTERS WATER
AT REAR OF WHEEL
LOW VELOCITY COMPONENT] P2[PHASE 2: POWER STROKE
FLOAT IMMERSED AT MID-DEPTH
MAXIMUM REARWARD VELOCITY
MAXIMUM THRUST] P3[PHASE 3: EXIT
FLOAT LEAVES WATER
AT FRONT OF WHEEL
THRUST DECAYS] P4[PHASE 4: RECOVERY
FLOAT IN AIR ABOVE WATERLINE
RETURNS TO ENTRY POINT] P1 -->|Quarter rotation| P2 P2 -->|Quarter rotation| P3 P3 -->|Quarter rotation| P4 P4 -->|Quarter rotation| P1 style P1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000000 style P2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#274e0a,color:#ffffff style P3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#000000 style P4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Sidewheel vs Sternwheel Configuration Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SW[Sidewheel Paddle Configuration] SW1[Hull Centreline] SW2[Starboard Paddle Wheel
in Paddle Box] SW3[Port Paddle Wheel
in Paddle Box] SW4[Drive Shaft Through Hull] SW5[Two Independent Engines
for Steering] SW1 --- SW2 SW1 --- SW3 SW2 --- SW4 SW3 --- SW4 SW4 --- SW5 end subgraph ST[Sternwheel Paddle Configuration] ST1[Hull with Shallow Draft] ST2[Sternwheel Aft of Hull] ST3[Protective Stern Frame] ST4[Single Engine Drive] ST5[Rudder Behind Sternwheel] ST1 --- ST2 ST2 --- ST3 ST3 --- ST4 ST3 --- ST5 end SW -.Comparative.-> ST style SW1 fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 style SW2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 style SW3 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 style ST1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000 style ST2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.4 Component Schematic – Paddle Wheel Cross-Section (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[ENGINE ROOM
BELOW DECK] B[REDUCTION GEARBOX] C[PADDLE SHAFT] D[PADDLE WHEEL ASSEMBLY] E1[FLOAT POSITION 1
POWER STROKE] E2[FLOAT POSITION 2
ENTRY TRANSITION] E3[FLOAT POSITION 3
EXIT TRANSITION] E4[FLOAT POSITION 4
RECOVERY IN AIR] F[WATERLINE] G[THRUST VECTOR FORWARD] H[SLIP AND SPRAY LOSSES] A -->|Mechanical Power| B B -->|High Torque Low RPM| C C -->|Rotational Drive| D D --> E1 D --> E2 D --> E3 D --> E4 E1 --> F E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G E1 --> H E2 --> H E3 --> H style A fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style B fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style C fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#0b2545,color:#000000 style D fill:#00b0f0,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff style G fill:#70ad47,stroke:#274e0a,color:#ffffff style H fill:#c00000,stroke:#5a0000,color:#ffffff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define *paddle wheel* as a marine propulsion device. With the aid of a sketch, distinguish between **sidewheel** and **sternwheel** configurations. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > A paddle wheel is a rotating wheel fitted with radial or feathering blades (called *floats*) around its circumference, partially immersed in water to produce thrust via Newton's Third Law. (1 mark) > - **Sidewheel:** Two paddle wheels mounted on the port and starboard sides of the hull, each enclosed in a *paddle box*. They offer good manoeuvrability (independent wheel reversal for turning) but are exposed to wave damage and add wind resistance. (1 mark) > - **Sternwheel:** A single paddle wheel mounted at the stern, partially protected by the hull. It allows very shallow draft, simpler construction, and better performance in rivers. (1 mark) ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is *slip* in a paddle wheel? Why is it always positive for radial floats? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Slip** is the difference between the theoretical speed of the float tip ($V_t = \pi D N$) and the actual forward speed of the ship ($V_a$), expressed as a fraction of $V_t$: > $$s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t}$$ (2 marks) > Slip is always positive for radial floats because, due to the geometry of the radial float, water is pushed *sideways and downward* (not purely astern) at the entry and exit points, so the effective rearward momentum transfer is less than the theoretical value, and the ship moves slower than $V_t$. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Part B questions offer internal choice. Both alternatives below are designed in KTU style with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** A sidewheel paddle steamer has the following particulars: - Wheel diameter $D = 6$ m - Number of floats per wheel $Z = 10$ - Rotational speed $N = 32$ RPM - Immersion depth of floats $h = 1.2$ m - Ship's actual speed $V_a = 7.5$ m/s - Average thrust per immersed float $T_f = 18$ kN **(a)** Calculate the **theoretical speed** $V_t$, the **slip ratio** $s$, and the **slip percentage**. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the **total propulsive thrust** $T$, the **power input to the paddle shaft** (assuming torque $T_q = 95$ kN·m), and the **propulsive efficiency** of the paddle wheel. (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Speed and Slip Calculation** **Step 1:** Convert rotational speed to rev/s. $$N = \frac{32}{60} = 0.5333 \;\;\text{rev/s}$$ **Step 2:** Compute theoretical (circumferential) tip speed. $$V_t = \pi D N = \pi \times 6 \times 0.5333 = 3.2 \pi \approx 10.053 \;\;\text{m/s}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the slip ratio. $$s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = \frac{10.053 - 7.5}{10.053} = \frac{2.553}{10.053} \approx 0.2539$$ **Step 4:** Express slip as a percentage. $$s\% \approx 25.39\%$$ > **Marks distribution:** $V_t$ = 2 marks, $s$ = 3 marks, $s\%$ = 2 marks. --- **Part (b) – Thrust, Power Input, and Efficiency** **Step 1:** Compute the number of simultaneously immersed floats. $$n = Z \cdot \frac{h}{D} = 10 \cdot \frac{1.2}{6} = 10 \cdot 0.2 = 2 \;\;\text{floats}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the total propulsive thrust. $$T = n \cdot T_f = 2 \times 18\,000 = 36\,000 \;\;\text{N} = 36 \;\;\text{kN}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the power input to the paddle shaft. $$P_{input} = 2\pi N \cdot T_q = 2\pi \times 0.5333 \times 95\,000$$ Breaking it down: $$P_{input} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.5333 \times 95\,000$$ $$= 6.2832 \times 0.5333 \times 95\,000$$ $$= 3.3510 \times 95\,000 \approx 318\,345 \;\;\text{W} \approx 318.35 \;\;\text{kW}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the useful thrust power delivered to the ship. $$P_{useful} = T \cdot V_a = 36\,000 \times 7.5 = 270\,000 \;\;\text{W} = 270 \;\;\text{kW}$$ **Step 5:** Compute the propulsive efficiency. $$\eta_{pw} = \frac{P_{useful}}{P_{input}} = \frac{270}{318.35} \approx 0.8482 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 84.82\%$$ > **Marks distribution:** $n$ = 2 marks, $T$ = 1 mark, $P_{input}$ = 2 marks, $\eta$ = 2 marks. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Question A:** > 1. **Unit conversion of $N$:** Many students forget to convert RPM to rev/s. Always convert: $N (\text{rev/s}) = N (\text{RPM}) / 60$. Marks deducted: **1 to 2 marks**. > 2. **Immersion fraction:** Using $h/D$ directly assumes the float is at the bottom of the wheel. For more precise answers, use the submerged arc angle. Marks deducted: **0.5 to 1 mark**. > 3. **Torque units:** $T_q$ is in N·m, not kN·m. Be consistent. Marks deducted: **1 mark**. > 4. **Efficiency cap:** A paddle wheel efficiency exceeding $85\%$ is unusual; if you obtain a value above $90\%$, re-check your calculations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** (a) With neat sketches, describe the **construction and working of a paddle wheel**. Explain the difference between **radial** and **feathering** floats, and state which type is more efficient and why. (7 marks) (b) A paddle wheel has a diameter of $5$ m and rotates at $25$ RPM. The ship moves at $6.5$ m/s. Compute: **(i)** theoretical speed $V_t$, **(ii)** apparent slip $s$, **(iii)** slip percentage, **(iv)** ship speed in **knots** ($1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s), and **(v)** the slip in **m/s** (absolute slip = $V_t - V_a$). (7 marks) #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Construction, Working, and Float Types** > **Construction (3 marks):** A paddle wheel consists of (i) a *paddle shaft* driven by the main engine through reduction gearing, (ii) a *central hub* (boss) keyed to the shaft, (iii) multiple *paddle arms* (spokes) radiating from the hub, and (iv) *floats* (paddle boards) bolted or pivoted to the arms. The whole assembly rotates with the lower portion immersed in water. > **Working (2 marks):** As the wheel rotates, the floats enter the water at the rear, move downward and forward relative to the ship (or rearward in the earth frame), pushing water astern. By Newton's Third Law, the water exerts a forward reaction thrust on the floats, propelling the ship. The floats then lift clear at the front and return to repeat the cycle. > **Radial vs Feathering (2 marks):** *Radial floats* are rigidly fixed perpendicular to the arm; they remain radial at all times. *Feathering floats* pivot at their outer end and automatically rotate to keep their face perpendicular to the absolute water velocity, minimising entry and exit losses. Feathering floats are more efficient because they maintain a favourable angle of attack throughout the immersion arc, producing higher thrust for the same input power and reducing slip. --- **Part (b) – Numerical Computation** **Step 1:** Convert rotational speed. $$N = \frac{25}{60} = 0.4167 \;\;\text{rev/s}$$ **(i) Theoretical tip speed:** $$V_t = \pi D N = \pi \times 5 \times 0.4167 = 2.0833 \pi \approx 6.545 \;\;\text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Apparent slip ratio:** $$s = \frac{V_t - V_a}{V_t} = \frac{6.545 - 6.5}{6.545} = \frac{0.045}{6.545} \approx 0.00688$$ **(iii) Slip percentage:** $$s\% = 0.00688 \times 100 \approx 0.688\%$$ **(iv) Ship speed in knots:** $$V_{a,knots} = \frac{V_a}{0.5144} = \frac{6.5}{0.5144} \approx 12.636 \;\;\text{knots}$$ **(v) Absolute slip in m/s:** $$\Delta V = V_t - V_a = 6.545 - 6.5 = 0.045 \;\;\text{m/s}$$ > **Marks distribution:** $V_t$ = 2 marks, $s$ = 1.5 marks, $s\%$ = 1 mark, knots = 1.5 marks, $\Delta V$ = 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Question B:** > 1. **Sketch quality:** A neat labelled diagram earns full marks; a vague sketch costs **1 to 2 marks**. > 2. **Knot conversion:** Students commonly use $1$ knot $= 0.514$ m/s (the KTU textbook value is $0.5144$ m/s). Use the value given in your course material. Marks deducted: **0.5 mark**. > 3. **Zero slip trap:** If $V_t \approx V_a$, slip will be very small but **never exactly zero** – always show at least 4 decimal places to demonstrate rigour. > 4. **Working explanation:** A bare numerical answer with no formula earns only 1 mark out of the 2 allocated. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 – Paddle Wheels** - **Definition:** Paddle wheel = rotating wheel with floats that produce thrust by Newton's Third Law. - **Two main configurations:** **Sidewheel** (port + starboard, in paddle boxes) and **sternwheel** (single wheel aft). Sternwheel is preferred for shallow rivers; sidewheel for higher manoeuvrability and seakeeping. - **Two float types:** **Radial** (rigid, simpler, lower efficiency) and **feathering** (pivoted, higher efficiency, complex mechanism). - **Theoretical tip speed formula:** $\mathbf{V_t = \pi D N}$ — memorise this in **m/s** with $D$ in m and $N$ in rev/s. - **Slip formula:** $\mathbf{s = (V_t - V_a) / V_t}$ — a *positive* value indicates that the ship is slower than the theoretical tip speed. - **Actual ship speed:** $\mathbf{V_a = V_t(1 - s)}$. - **Typical slip range for radial paddle wheels:** $20\%$ to $30\%$; for feathering wheels: $10\%$ to $20\%$. - **Propulsive efficiency formula:** $\mathbf{\eta_{pw} = T V_a / (2\pi N T_q)}$ — must be between $0$ and $1$ (or $0\%$ and $100\%$). - **Number of immersed floats:** $\mathbf{n = Z \cdot (h / D)}$ for approximate estimation. - **Total thrust:** $\mathbf{T = n \cdot T_f}$. - **Physical constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $1$ knot $= 0.5144$ m/s. - **Common KTU trap:** Confusing the float tip speed *relative to the ship* ($V_t$) with the ship's speed *relative to the water* ($V_a$). - **Common KTU trap:** Using $N$ in RPM instead of rev/s when applying $V_t = \pi D N$. - **Historical fact:** *SS Great Eastern* (1858) was the first major vessel to combine both paddle and screw propulsion. *Clermont* (1807) was Fulton's first commercial paddle steamer. - **Modern relevance:** Paddle wheels survive in riverboats and heritage vessels; the **sternwheeler** is the iconic Mississippi River design. - **Comparative note:** Screw propellers are more efficient than paddle wheels in open seas but paddle wheels win in shallow, debris-free rivers due to lower draft and higher manoeuvrability. - **Future-proof tip:** If asked *"Why are paddle wheels not used in modern ocean vessels?"*, answer: (1) high slip losses, (2) vulnerability to wave damage, (3) large weight and windage of paddle boxes, (4) inferior efficiency to screw propellers at cruising speeds, (5) difficulty operating in heavy seas.

topics.title: Electromagnetic Propulsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST834 - BASICS OF SHIP PROPULSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Propulsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromagnetic Propulsion (EMP)** is a class of non-conventional marine propulsion systems in which thrust is generated by directly converting electrical energy into kinetic energy of the working fluid (seawater or plasma) through the interaction of electric currents and magnetic fields, **without the use of rotating mechanical thrusters, propellers, or shafts**.

The fundamental governing principle is the **Lorentz Force Law**, which describes the mechanical force experienced by a charged particle (or a current-carrying conductor) when subjected to electromagnetic fields. In marine EMP systems, seawater acts as the moving conductor because it contains dissolved salts (primarily **NaCl**), making it electrically conductive.

The two principal families of electromagnetic ship propulsion are:

1. **Magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) Propulsion** — Also known as *magneto-aerodynamic* or *plasma drive*. Uses steady-state or pulsed magnetic fields interacting with seawater or ionized gas.
2. **Superconducting Electromagnetic Propulsion (SEMP)** — Uses high-temperature superconducting (HTS) magnets to produce the intense magnetic flux densities required for practical thrust levels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Tugboat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine pushing a long, salty water hose through a magnetic field. The salt ions in the water feel a sideways push (the Lorentz force), and the entire column of water is shoved backward. If that water is inside a ship with an open nozzle at the stern, the reaction pushes the ship forward — *just like Newton's third law applied to a rocket.* No propeller, no shaft, no gears. The "tug" is an invisible hand made of magnetism.

Think of it this way:
- **Conventional ship** = mechanical paddle (propeller) pushing water.
- **EM ship** = the *water itself* is grabbed by a magnetic "hand" and pulled/pushed, and the ship recoils in the opposite direction.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **high-yield** for KTU derivations and numericals:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ |
| $\sigma_w$ | Electrical conductivity of seawater | $\approx 4 \text{ to } 5 \ \text{S/m}$ |
| $\rho_w$ | Density of seawater | $\approx 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $B$ | Magnetic flux density | Tesla (T) |
| $F$ | Lorentz / thrust force | Newton (N) |
| $J$ | Current density | $\text{A/m}^2$ |
| $\eta$ | Propulsive efficiency | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The conductivity of seawater ($\sigma_w \approx 4$–$5 \ \text{S/m}$) is **one million times greater** than that of fresh water, which is why EMP is only practically feasible in the ocean — not in rivers or lakes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Lorentz Force

When a current-carrying conductor of length $L$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, the force experienced is given by the **Lorentz force law**:

$$F = I \cdot (L \times B) = I \cdot L \cdot B \cdot \sin\theta$$

For a marine MHD thruster, the current flows **through the seawater itself** (perpendicular to the flow direction) and the magnetic field is also oriented perpendicular to both the current and the flow. Therefore, the angle $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin\theta = 1$, simplifying the expression to:

$$F = I \cdot L \cdot B$$

For a continuous distribution of current over a cross-sectional area $A$, the volumetric form of the Lorentz force per unit volume is:

$$f = J \times B$$

where $J$ is the current density vector in $\text{A/m}^2$ and $B$ is the magnetic flux density vector in Tesla.

### 2.2 The Three Orthogonal Vector Relationship

For maximum thrust generation, the **three vectors** — current density ($J$), magnetic field ($B$), and fluid velocity ($v$) — must be **mutually perpendicular**. This is the classic right-hand-rule configuration of MHD propulsion.

| Vector | Symbol | Direction (typical) |
|--------|--------|---------------------|
| Current density | $\vec{J}$ | Vertical (athwartships) |
| Magnetic field | $\vec{B}$ | Horizontal (perpendicular to flow) |
| Resulting force / velocity | $\vec{F} \propto \vec{v}$ | Longitudinal (ships axis) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU Part A and Part B question on this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $F = I \cdot L \cdot B$ | Lorentz force (linear conductor) | Basic thrust derivation |
| 2 | $F = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot A$ | MHD thrust (simplified, ideal) | Channel thrust calculation |
| 3 | $P_{elec} = \sigma_w \cdot v^2 \cdot B^2 \cdot A$ | Electrical power input | Energy balance |
| 4 | $P_{thrust} = F \cdot v = \sigma_w \cdot v^2 \cdot B^2 \cdot A$ | Propulsive power output | Efficiency analysis |
| 5 | $\eta_{ideal} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ | Maximum MHD efficiency | Theoretical limit |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{2x}{1+x^2}$ | Generalized efficiency, $x = v/u$ | Realistic performance |
| 7 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{2r}$ | Solenoid field (Biot–Savart) | Magnet design |
| 8 | $R_m = \mu_0 \cdot \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot L$ | Magnetic Reynolds number | MHD regime classifier |
| 9 | $S = \dfrac{\sigma_w \cdot B^2 \cdot L}{\rho_w \cdot v}$ | Interaction parameter (Stuart number) | Coupling strength |
| 10 | $v_{ship} = v_{ejecta} \cdot \dfrac{1 - k}{1 + k}$ | Two-fluid velocity relation (Rankine-type) | Slip estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Units:** In the MHD thrust equation, $A$ refers to the **cross-sectional area of the propulsion duct** (channel), not the wetted surface of the ship. The magnetic field $B$ is the *applied* field; the local field inside the seawater is slightly reduced by the induced back-EMF (Hall effect / Faraday reaction).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Silent Operation:** No rotating machinery makes EMP nearly anechoic to sonar — invaluable for **naval submarines** and research vessels.
- **No Cavitation:** Since there is no propeller blade, cavitation-induced noise, erosion, and efficiency loss are eliminated.
- **High Theoretical Speed:** Eliminates the cavitation barrier that limits conventional propellers to roughly **30–35 knots**.
- **Reduced Mechanical Wear:** No bearings, seals, or shaft seals in contact with seawater.
- **Strategic Naval Applications:** Considered for **next-generation stealth submarines** and unmanned underwater vehicles (UUVs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — MHD Thrust from First Principles

**Given:**
- A rectangular MHD channel of cross-sectional area $A$ (height $\times$ width).
- Uniform magnetic field $B$ across the channel.
- Seawater (conductivity $\sigma_w$) flowing with velocity $v$.
- Electrodes on top and bottom of the channel drive current $I$ through the water.

**Step 1 — Current density in the seawater:**

The current is distributed over the cross-section, so the average current density is:

$$J = \frac{I}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Force on a current element:**

Using the Lorentz force per unit volume:

$$f = J \times B$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the channel volume:**

For a channel of length $L_c$ (along the flow direction), the volume is $V = A \cdot L_c$. The total thrust is:

$$F = f \cdot V = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $J$:**

$$F = \frac{I}{A} \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c = I \cdot B \cdot L_c$$

This is the **linear Lorentz force** in disguise.

**Step 5 — Express in terms of fluid velocity using Ohm's law for moving conductors:**

In a moving conductor, the effective Ohm's law in the fluid frame is:

$$\vec{J} = \sigma_w \left( \vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B} \right)$$

For a *short-circuited* MHD channel (electrodes connected through an external load), the net electric field in the laboratory frame vanishes: $\vec{E} = 0$. Then:

$$J = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the thrust equation:**

$$F = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

Or, defining a channel length factor $L_c$:

$$\boxed{F = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above is the **fundamental MHD thrust equation**. It shows thrust is **directly proportional** to magnetic field strength squared — which is why engineers strive for very high $B$ fields via superconducting magnets.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of an MHD Thruster

**Step 1 — Define the two powers:**

- **Electrical input power** (dissipated in the seawater as Joule heating + kinetic energy transfer):

$$P_{in} = \frac{J^2}{\sigma_w} \cdot V = \sigma_w \cdot v^2 \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

- **Useful propulsive power** (thrust times fluid exit velocity $v$):

$$P_{out} = F \cdot v = \sigma_w \cdot v^2 \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

Wait — these appear equal. The subtlety is the *load matching* with the external circuit. Let $u$ be the **open-circuit fluid velocity** (no current) and $v$ the **actual fluid velocity** with current flowing. The relationship from the electromagnetic boundary conditions is:

$$\frac{v}{u} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\sigma_w B^2 L_c}{\rho_w u}} = \frac{1}{1 + S}$$

where $S$ is the interaction parameter (Stuart number).

**Step 2 — Define efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Kinetic power added to fluid}}{\text{Electrical power input}}$$

After detailed energy balance accounting for Joule dissipation and the back-reaction of the induced current:

$$\eta = \frac{2x}{1 + x^2}, \quad \text{where} \quad x = \frac{v}{u}$$

**Step 3 — Maximize $\eta$ with respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{2(1+x^2) - 2x(2x)}{(1+x^2)^2} = \frac{2(1 - x^2)}{(1+x^2)^2}$$

Set numerator to zero:

$$1 - x^2 = 0 \implies x = 1 \implies v = u$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate maximum efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{2(1)}{1+1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \ \text{(idealized single-stage)}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **ideal single-stage MHD efficiency is NOT 2/3.** That value of $2/3$ arises in **MHD power generation** (Faraday generator), not propulsion. For a **thruster**, the theoretical upper bound is **100% (unity)** in an idealized lossless case. Many textbooks (and many KTU answer keys) incorrectly state $2/3$ for propulsion — students should clarify *generation vs. propulsion* in their answers.

**Step 5 — Real-world efficiency:**

Accounting for electrode losses, magnet inefficiency, fluid friction, and incomplete coupling, real MHD thrusters operate at **well under 10% efficiency**. This is the central engineering challenge.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Field of a Superconducting Solenoid (Biot–Savart)

For the magnet that produces $B$ in the channel:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{2r}$$

For a solenoid of length $\ell \gg r$ (long-solenoid approximation):

$$\boxed{B = \mu_0 \cdot n \cdot I = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I}{\ell}}$$

where $n = N/\ell$ is the turns per unit length. For practical marine SEMP, $B \sim 2$–$10 \ \text{T}$ requires either low-temperature superconductors (LTS, NbTi at 4.2 K) or high-temperature superconductors (HTS, YBCO at 77 K).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of EMP Sub-Types

| Feature | MHD (Seawater) | SEMP (Superconducting) | Pulsed Plasma |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Working fluid | Seawater | Seawater / plasma | Ionized gas (ablated Teflon) |
| Magnetic field source | Conventional or SC coils | SC coils only | Pulsed coils |
| Typical $B$ | $0.5$–$2 \ \text{T}$ | $2$–$10 \ \text{T}$ | Transient, $>5 \ \text{T}$ |
| Continuous thrust? | Yes | Yes | No (pulsed) |
| Efficiency | $< 10\%$ (current) | $< 15\%$ (current) | $< 5\%$ |
| Maturity | Experimental (Yamato-1) | Lab scale | Lab / space use |
| Best application | Stealth submarines | Large naval ships | Small craft / spacecraft |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — MHD Thruster Block Diagram

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the energy and signal flow inside a MHD propulsion system, from the onboard prime mover to the seawater jet exiting the duct.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Mover - Gas Turbine or Nuclear Reactor] --> B[Electrical Generator - AC to DC]
    B --> C[Superconducting Magnet Power Supply]
    B --> D[Electrode Power Supply - High Current DC]
    C --> E[Superconducting Magnet Coils - High B field]
    D --> F[Top and Bottom Electrodes in Duct]
    E --> G[MHD Channel - Internal Volume of Ship]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Seawater Intake - Forward Grates]
    G --> I[Seawater Acceleration via Lorentz Force]
    I --> J[High Velocity Seawater Jet - Stern Nozzle]
    J --> K[Thrust Reaction on Hull - Newton's Third Law]
    H --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **prime mover** (left) supplies mechanical energy.
- The **generator** converts it to electrical energy.
- The electrical energy is split into two paths: one to power the **magnet** (high voltage, moderate current) and one to drive the **electrodes** (low voltage, very high current).
- The magnet and electrodes together create the crossed $E$ and $B$ fields inside the **MHD channel**.
- **Seawater enters** at the bow grates, is **accelerated** by Lorentz force, and **exits** at the stern as a high-velocity jet.
- The **reaction** on the hull produces forward thrust.

### 4.2 Vector Configuration Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Vector_Configuration
        direction LR
        V1[Current Density J - Vertical Up]
        V2[Magnetic Field B - Horizontal Athwartships]
        V3[Lorentz Force and Flow - Longitudinal Forward]
        V1 -->|Cross Product J x B| V3
        V2 -->|Cross Product J x B| V3
    end
```

This block shows the **right-hand-rule geometry**: $J$ and $B$ are perpendicular to each other, and the resulting force is perpendicular to both, aligned with the ship's longitudinal axis.

### 4.3 Historical Reference — Yamato-1

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Y1[Yamato-1 - Japan 1991] --> Y2[First Superconducting MHD Ship]
    Y2 --> Y3[Length 30 m - Displacement 185 tonnes]
    Y3 --> Y4[Two MHD Thrusters]
    Y4 --> Y5[Demonstrated 6.6 knots at 4 Tesla]
    Y5 --> Y6[Proved feasibility of SC MHD propulsion]
```

The **Yamato-1**, built by the Ship & Ocean Foundation (Japan) in 1991, is the only full-scale MHD-propelled ship to date. It demonstrated that superconducting MHD propulsion is physically possible but exposed the severe efficiency problems that have prevented commercialization.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**  
*Define electromagnetic propulsion and state the fundamental physical law on which it is based.*

**Model Answer:**

Electromagnetic propulsion is a method of generating thrust for marine vessels without moving mechanical parts such as propellers or shafts, by directly accelerating a conducting fluid (seawater or plasma) through the Lorentz force arising from crossed electric and magnetic fields.

It is based on the **Lorentz Force Law**:

$$\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

For a continuous current distribution in seawater:

$$\vec{F}_{vol} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$$

where $\vec{J}$ is the current density in the seawater and $\vec{B}$ is the applied magnetic flux density.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$ (electrostatic) with $\vec{F} = q\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ (magnetostatic). The *velocity-dependent* term is what enables MHD propulsion. Marks are lost when students write the full vector form incorrectly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**  
*Why is electromagnetic propulsion only practical in seawater and not in freshwater?*

**Model Answer:**

EMP requires the working fluid to be electrically conductive so that current can flow through it under the action of an induced or applied electric field. The conductivity of seawater, $\sigma_w \approx 4$–$5 \ \text{S/m}$, arises from dissolved salts (mainly NaCl, KCl, MgCl₂). Fresh water has conductivity of order $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2} \ \text{S/m}$, which is **100 to 1000 times lower**.

From the MHD thrust equation $F = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c$, thrust is directly proportional to $\sigma_w$. Therefore, in freshwater, the thrust produced for the same magnetic field and current is negligibly small, making EMP impractical for rivers and lakes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer *"because seawater is salty"* — this is true but **insufficient** for full marks. You must **quantify** the conductivity ratio and link it to the thrust equation. 3-mark questions demand the *quantitative* justification.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You are expected to attempt *one* of the two choices.

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the thrust produced by a magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) propulsion system, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A seawater MHD thruster has a channel cross-section of $0.5 \ \text{m} \times 0.5 \ \text{m}$ and an effective interaction length of $2 \ \text{m}$. The applied magnetic field is $1.5 \ \text{T}$ and the average current density in the channel is $25{,}000 \ \text{A/m}^2$. Calculate the thrust and the propulsive power at a flow velocity of $2 \ \text{m/s}$. Take $\sigma_w = 4 \ \text{S/m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Key assumptions:

1. The channel is short, so end effects are negligible.
2. Magnetic field $B$ is uniform across the channel cross-section.
3. Current density $J$ is uniform.
4. Seawater is treated as a homogeneous, isotropic, ohmic conductor.
5. Hall effect and electrode voltage drops are neglected.
6. The fluid is incompressible.

Final result:

$$F = \sigma_w \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

Equivalently, using $J = \sigma_w v B$:

$$F = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$A = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute thrust using the volumetric Lorentz force form:**

$$F = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c$$

$$F = 25{,}000 \times 1.5 \times 0.25 \times 2$$

$$F = 25{,}000 \times 0.75 = 18{,}750 \ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute propulsive power:**

$$P = F \cdot v = 18{,}750 \times 2 = 37{,}500 \ \text{W} = 37.5 \ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the conductivity-based form:**

Check: $J = \sigma_w v B = 4 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 12 \ \text{A/m}^2$ — this contradicts the given $J = 25{,}000 \ \text{A/m}^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution:** The given current density ($25{,}000 \ \text{A/m}^2$) is the *imposed* value from the external power supply, **not** the *induced* value from the fluid motion. In a real MHD thruster, an external power source drives the current. Therefore, we use $F = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c$ with the **given** $J$.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F = 18{,}750 \ \text{N} \quad \text{and} \quad P = 37.5 \ \text{kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):** The common student error is to apply the formula $F = \sigma_w v B^2 A L_c$ directly without checking that the current density given in the problem matches the *induced* value from $\sigma_w v B$. When numerical inconsistency arises, **always use the given $J$ in the formula $F = J \cdot B \cdot A \cdot L_c$** — this is the more general and correct formulation. Marks are deducted for blindly substituting $\sigma_w$ when a direct $J$ is provided.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the working principle of a superconducting electromagnetic propulsion (SEMP) system. State its advantages over conventional mechanical propulsion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the limitations of electromagnetic ship propulsion systems and mention at least two practical examples of EMP development worldwide. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Working Principle of SEMP:**

1. **High Magnetic Field Generation:** A superconducting magnet (typically using NbTi or YBCO coils cooled by liquid helium at 4.2 K or liquid nitrogen at 77 K) generates a very strong, steady magnetic field $B$ (typically $2$–$10 \ \text{T}$) inside a duct that runs through the ship.

2. **Current Excitation:** A separate power supply drives a large DC current $I$ through the seawater between electrodes mounted on the top and bottom of the duct. The current density $J$ is perpendicular to $B$.

3. **Lorentz Force Interaction:** The crossed $J$ and $B$ produce a Lorentz force on the seawater in the longitudinal (fore-aft) direction, accelerating the water rearward.

4. **Thrust Generation:** The reaction of the rearward-accelerated water jet (Newton's third law) propels the ship forward.

5. **Closed Cooling Loop:** The cryogenic system maintains the superconductors below their critical temperature using onboard cryocoolers.

**Advantages of SEMP over Conventional Propulsion:**

- **Silent operation** (no propeller cavitation noise) — ideal for submarines.
- **No mechanical moving parts** exposed to seawater — reduced maintenance.
- **No theoretical speed limit** from cavitation (can exceed 40 knots).
- **High reliability** (fewer wearing components).
- **Stealth** (low acoustic, magnetic, and wake signatures).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Limitations of EMP Systems:**

1. **Low Efficiency:** Real MHD thrusters achieve less than 10% propulsive efficiency because most electrical energy is dissipated as Joule heat in the seawater rather than converted to kinetic energy.
2. **Enormous Power Requirement:** Producing thrust in the MN range requires GW-class onboard power — feasible only with nuclear reactors.
3. **Superconducting Magnet Complexity:** Cryogenic systems, quench protection, and high capital cost are major barriers.
4. **Electrode Erosion and Electrolysis:** Sustained high current through seawater causes electrolysis (H₂ and Cl₂ gas evolution) and electrode corrosion.
5. **Seawater Conductivity Variability:** $\sigma_w$ varies with salinity, temperature, and location, causing unpredictable thrust.
6. **External Magnetic Signature:** Strong $B$ fields may interfere with onboard electronics and be detectable by magnetic anomaly detectors (MAD).

**Two Practical Examples:**

1. **Yamato-1 (Japan, 1991):** The world's first (and only) full-scale superconducting MHD-propelled ship. Length 30 m, displacement 185 t, two MHD thrusters with NbTi superconducting magnets at 4.2 K, demonstrated a top speed of **6.6 knots** at 4 T. It conclusively proved the concept but highlighted the efficiency problem.

2. **U.S. Navy / DARPA Programs:** Various research initiatives (1970s onward) into MHD submarine propulsion, including conceptual designs for high-speed stealth submersibles. Though no full-scale vessel was completed, the technology underpins modern **railgun** and **coilgun** naval weapons.

3. *(Optional third)* **Chinese and Russian research programs** have published experimental MHD thruster results for underwater vehicle applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(a):** Students often confuse *MHD* with *SEMP* — they are not the same. MHD can use conventional electromagnets (e.g., copper coils); SEMP specifically uses **superconducting** magnets. Marks are awarded for **distinguishing** between the two. Also, in advantages, students forget the **cavitation-limited speed** argument, which is one of the strongest points.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electromagnetic Propulsion:**

- **Core principle:** Lorentz force on seawater ions / current; $\vec{F} = \vec{J} \times \vec{B}$.
- **Three mutually perpendicular vectors:** $\vec{J}$, $\vec{B}$, fluid velocity $\vec{v}$ — right-hand rule.
- **MHD thrust equation:** $F = \sigma_w v B^2 A L_c$ (or $F = J B A L_c$ when $J$ is imposed).
- **Propulsive power:** $P = F \cdot v$.
- **Why seawater only:** $\sigma_w \approx 4$–$5 \ \text{S/m}$ due to dissolved salts; freshwater is $\sim 1000\times$ less conductive.
- **MHD vs. SEMP:** MHD = general concept; SEMP = uses superconducting magnets for high $B$.
- **Solenoid field:** $B = \mu_0 n I$ for long solenoid; $B = \mu_0 N I / (2r)$ for short.
- **Interaction parameter (Stuart number):** $S = \sigma_w B^2 L / (\rho_w v)$ — measures coupling strength.
- **Magnetic Reynolds number:** $R_m = \mu_0 \sigma_w v L$ — classifies MHD regime.
- **Maximum ideal MHD thruster efficiency:** Unity (100%) in lossless case — NOT 2/3 (that's for generators).
- **Key advantage:** No cavitation → no speed limit; silent → submarine stealth.
- **Key limitation:** Very low real-world efficiency (< 10%) and high power demand.
- **Landmark vessel:** **Yamato-1 (Japan, 1991)** — 6.6 knots, 4 T SC magnet, 30 m length.
- **Electrode problem:** Electrolysis produces H₂ and Cl₂; electrodes corrode.
- **Use the given $J$ in numericals**, not the induced $\sigma_w v B$, when both are provided.
- **Cross product sign convention:** $\hat{x} \times \hat{y} = \hat{z}$ — apply carefully in vector diagrams.
